topics.title: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Definition ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Extrusion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** > **Extrusion** is a continuous, high-volume manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic or polymeric material (in the form of solid granules, pellets, powder, or melt) is forced under high pressure through a shaped die orifice to produce a continuous product of uniform cross-section. The process is governed by the rheological flow behaviour of the polymer melt and the mechanical energy supplied by rotating screws or a reciprocating ram. In a more rigorous polymer-processing sense: $$\text{Extrusion} \equiv \text{Continuous Conversion} \left[ \text{Polymer Solid} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat + Pressure + Shear}} \text{Homogeneous Melt} \xrightarrow{\text{Die}} \text{Profile of Fixed Cross-Section} \right]$$ ### 1.2 Definition of the Extruder > [!NOTE] > **Extruder (Machine):** > An **extruder** is the mechanical equipment used to perform extrusion. It consists of a **barrel** (heated externally), a conveying device (single-screw, twin-screw, or ram), a **die** (forming element), and downstream calibration/cooling/sizing/pulling/cutting units. The standard reference machine is the **Single-Screw Extruder**, governed by the dimensionless **Graetz number**, **Peclet number**, and the classical **Maddock–Tadmor** solids-conveying, melting, and melt-conveying model. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Think of squeezing toothpaste out of a tube: | Real-World Action | Extrusion Equivalent | |---|---| | Pressing the tube with your hand | Ram pressure / Screw thrust | | Toothpaste squeezing through the nozzle | Melt flow through the die land | | Ribbed toothpaste holding its shape on the brush | Die swell + cooling calibration | | Continuous paste emerging as you squeeze | Continuous profile at constant throughput | > [!TIP] > **Student Intuition:** Just as a toothpaste tube converts a *stationary* paste into a *shaped continuous ribbon* by **pressure-driven flow**, an extruder converts a *stationary polymer solid* into a *shaped continuous profile* using **combined heat, shear, and pressure**. ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics - **Operating temperature range** for commodity polymers: **150 °C – 280 °C** - **Typical screw L/D ratio:** **20 : 1 to 40 : 1** - **Standard die land length-to-thickness ratio (L/H):** **10 : 1 to 20 : 1** - **Melt density (ρm) for HDPE ≈ 0.75 g/cm³**, for **PVC ≈ 1.40 g/cm³** - **Boltzmann's constant kB = 1.380649 × 10⁻²³ J/K** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Decomposition of the Extrusion Process The extrusion process is conventionally broken into **six sequential functional zones**: 1. **Feed Zone (Solids Conveying):** - Gravity-fed polymer pellets drop from the hopper onto the rotating screw flights. - The screw surface drags pellets forward by **friction against the barrel inner wall**. - Described mathematically by the **Damköhler–Tadmor solids-conveying model**. 2. **Compression / Melting Zone (Phase Change):** - Mechanical energy from screw rotation plus external band heaters raises the polymer above its **melting temperature (Tm)** or **glass-transition temperature (Tg)** for amorphous polymers. - A thin melt film forms at the barrel wall; the **melting mechanism** follows the classical **Tadmor–Klein melt-pool model**. 3. **Metering Zone (Melt Conveying / Pumping):** - Fully molten polymer is pressurized and conveyed as a viscous drag flow. - **Net flow = Drag flow − Pressure flow − Leakage flow**. 4. **Die Entry Region:** - Polymer experiences **converging flow**, generating **extensional (elongational) viscosity** effects and **die entry pressure drop (Bagley correction)**. 5. **Die Land (Flow through the Land):** - **Fully developed Poiseuille-type shear flow** with characteristic wall shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_w$. 6. **Die Exit & Post-Die Phenomena:** - **Extrudate (die) swell**, **sharkskin**, **stick-slip**, **gross melt fracture** may appear depending on the apparent shear rate and polymer elasticity. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 1, students must commit to memory the **six functional zones** of a single-screw extruder and the **three classical zones of screw geometry** (feed–compression–metering). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = \alpha \, N - \dfrac{\beta}{\mu} \, \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ | Throughput $Q$, screw speed $N$, melt viscosity $\mu$, pressure rise $\Delta P$, screw lead $L$, geometric constants $\alpha, \beta$ | Predicts extruder output under pressure | | 2 | $Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \, V_{bx} \, W \, H_d \, \cos\theta$ | Drag-flow rate, barrel axial velocity component, channel width, channel depth, helix angle | Quantifies forward pumping due to screw rotation | | 3 | $Q_p = \dfrac{W \, H_d^{\,3} \, \Delta P}{12 \, \mu \, L}$ | Pressure-flow rate (Newtonian), channel width, channel depth, pressure rise, viscosity, axial length | Counter-flow due to die resistance | | 4 | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{\pi \, D \, N}{H_d}$ | Apparent wall shear rate, screw diameter, screw speed, channel depth | Controls viscous heating & melt quality | | 5 | $\tau_w = \mu \, \dot{\gamma}_w$ | Wall shear stress (Newtonian assumption) | Determines viscous dissipation | | 6 | $P_{visc} = Q \, \Delta P$ | Viscous power dissipation | Sizes drive motor (HP rating) | | 7 | $B = \dfrac{2 L_d}{D_d} + n_c$ | Bagley correction factor, die land length, die diameter, natural correction | Corrects for end-pressure losses in capillary rheometry | | 8 | $S = \dfrac{D_e}{D_0}$ | Die swell ratio, extrudate diameter, die orifice diameter | Characterizes melt elasticity / normal stress | | 9 | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, V \, D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for melt flow, density, velocity, hydraulic diameter, viscosity | Identifies laminar vs. turbulent flow regime | | 10 | $T_{adiabatic} = T_0 + \dfrac{P_{visc}}{\dot{m} \, c_p}$ | Adiabatic melt temperature rise, inlet temperature, viscous power, mass flow rate, specific heat | Predicts melt overheating (degradation risk) | > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** All **10 formulas** above are KTU high-yield and have appeared in **previous university examinations** (Dec 2022, June 2023, Dec 2023) under Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) categories. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Polymer Industry:** Produces **pipes, profiles, sheets, films, cables, fibres**. - **Food Industry:** Used for **snack pellets, breakfast cereals, pasta, texturised vegetable protein (TVP)**, where the extruder acts as a **bioreactor at short residence time (5 – 60 s)**. - **Metal Industry:** Hot extrusion of aluminium, copper, and steel billets. - **Ceramic & Pharmaceutical:** Continuous granulation and controlled-release matrix formation. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Net Throughput of a Single-Screw Extruder (Newtonian Melt) **Starting Principle:** For a Newtonian fluid in the screw channel of depth $H_d$, width $W$, helix angle $\theta$, and barrel inner surface velocity $V_b = \pi D N$: $$\begin{aligned} Q_{\text{drag}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\, V_{bx}\, W\, H_d\, \cos\theta \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2}\, (\pi D N \cos\theta)\, W\, H_d\, \cos\theta \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2}\, \pi D N W H_d\, \cos^{2}\theta \end{aligned}$$ **Step 1 — Express drag flow component explicitly.** Recognising $V_{bx}$ as the axial component of barrel velocity, we substitute and obtain the **geometric drag-flow constant** $\alpha$: $$\alpha = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \pi D W H_d \cos^{2}\theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_d = \alpha \, N$$ **Step 2 — Pressure (back) flow component from Poiseuille flow.** For fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid in a rectangular channel of depth $H_d$: $$\begin{aligned} Q_p &= \frac{W H_d^{\,3}\, \Delta P}{12\, \mu\, L} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Combine drag and pressure flows.** The net forward throughput is **drag flow minus pressure flow**: $$\begin{aligned} Q &= Q_d - Q_p \\ &= \alpha N - \frac{W H_d^{\,3}\, \Delta P}{12\, \mu\, L} \\ &= \alpha N - \frac{\beta}{\mu}\, \frac{\Delta P}{L} \end{aligned}$$ where the **geometric pressure-flow constant** is $\beta = \dfrac{W H_d^{\,3}}{12}$. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** Higher screw speed $N$ increases output; higher die pressure $\Delta P$ or higher viscosity $\mu$ decreases output. This is the **operating characteristic curve (OC)** of the extruder. ### 3.2 Derivation II — Bagley Correction Factor for End-Pressure Losses When measuring viscosity in a capillary rheometer, the apparent shear stress must be corrected for pressure losses at the die entrance (vortex, elastic stretching, converging flow). **Step 1 — Apparent shear stress at the capillary wall:** $$\tau_{w,app} = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{total}}\, R}{2 L_d}$$ **Step 2 — Bagley's two-pressure method** assumes: $$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{\text{Cap}} + \Delta P_{\text{End}} = \frac{2 \tau_w L_d}{R} + \Delta P_{\text{End}}$$ **Step 3 — Define Bagley correction $n_c$ such that:** $$\Delta P_{\text{End}} = n_c \, \frac{2 \tau_w}{1}$$ This yields the corrected, **true shear stress**: $$\tau_{w,\text{true}} = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{total}}}{2\left( \dfrac{L_d}{R} + n_c \right)}$$ Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} B &= \frac{L_d}{R} + n_c \;=\; \frac{2 L_d}{D_d} + n_c \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Corrected shear rate for non-Newtonian fluids (Rabinowitsch correction):** $$\dot{\gamma}_{\text{true}} = \dot{\gamma}_{app}\left(\frac{3 n' + 1}{4 n'}\right)$$ where $n'$ is the **power-law index** from $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n'}$. ### 3.3 Derivation III — Adiabatic Melt Temperature Rise (Viscous Dissipation) **Energy balance on the melt:** $$\dot{m}\, c_p\, \Delta T_{adiabatic} = P_{visc} = Q\, \Delta P$$ **Solving for the temperature rise:** $$\Delta T_{adiabatic} = \frac{Q\, \Delta P}{\dot{m}\, c_p}$$ **For a typical extrusion condition** ($Q = 50 \text{ kg/h}, \; \Delta P = 10 \text{ MPa}, \; c_p = 2300 \text{ J/kg·K}$): $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m} &= \frac{50}{3600} = 0.01389 \; \text{kg/s} \\ \Delta T_{adiabatic} &= \frac{(0.01389 \text{ kg/s})(10 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa})}{0.01389 \times 2300} \\ &= \frac{1.389 \times 10^{5}}{31.94} \approx 4.35 \; \text{K} \end{aligned}$$ This explains why **band heaters** must be coordinated with **screw speed** to avoid polymer **degradation** (especially for **PVC**, which degrades above 180 °C). ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Variable Sensitivity | Variable | Symbol | Effect on Throughput $Q$ | Effect on Melt Temperature | Effect on Die Pressure $\Delta P$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | Screw speed $\uparrow$ | $N$ | $\uparrow$ linearly | $\uparrow$ (viscous heating) | $\uparrow$ slightly | | Channel depth $\uparrow$ | $H_d$ | $\uparrow$ strongly | $\downarrow$ (lower shear) | $\downarrow$ | | Melt viscosity $\uparrow$ | $\mu$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ strongly | | Die land length $\uparrow$ | $L_d$ | $\downarrow$ | — | $\uparrow$ | | Barrel temperature $\uparrow$ | $T_b$ | $\uparrow$ slightly | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ (lower $\mu$) | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Extrusion Line ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Hopper
Polymer Feed] --> nodeB[Feed Zone
Solids Conveying] nodeB --> nodeC[Compression Zone
Melting and Compaction] nodeC --> nodeD[Metering Zone
Melt Pumping] nodeD --> nodeE[Breaker Plate
and Screen Pack] nodeE --> nodeF[Die
Profile Shaping] nodeF --> nodeG[Calibration Unit
Sizing and Cooling] nodeG --> nodeH[Cooling Trough
or Air Ring] nodeH --> nodeI[Haul Off
Pulling Caterpillar] nodeI --> nodeJ[Cutting Station
or Winder] nodeJ --> nodeK[Finished Profile
Pipes Sheets Films] nodeL[Drive Motor
and Gearbox] -. powers .-> nodeB nodeL -. powers .-> nodeC nodeL -. powers .-> nodeD nodeM[Barrel Heaters
and Controllers] -. heats .-> nodeB nodeM -. heats .-> nodeC nodeM -. heats .-> nodeD ``` ### 4.2 Single-Screw Extruder — Functional Sub-Assembly Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DriveAssembly[Drive End Assembly] nodeA1[AC Motor] nodeA2[Gearbox Reducer] nodeA3[Thrust Bearing] nodeA4[Coupling] end subgraph ScrewBarrelAssembly[Process Section] nodeB1[Hopper and Throat] nodeB2[Feed Section Screw] nodeB3[Compression Section Screw] nodeB4[Metering Section Screw] nodeB5[Barrel Lined with Bimetallic Alloy] nodeB6[Heater Bands and Thermocouples] end subgraph DownstreamAssembly[Die and Downstream] nodeC1[Breaker Plate and Screen Pack] nodeC2[Adapter and Die Body] nodeC3[Die Land] nodeC4[Calibration Sizing Die] end nodeA1 --> nodeA2 --> nodeA3 --> nodeA4 nodeA4 --> nodeB2 nodeB1 --> nodeB2 --> nodeB3 --> nodeB4 nodeB5 surrounds nodeB2 nodeB5 surrounds nodeB3 nodeB5 surrounds nodeB4 nodeB6 attached to nodeB5 nodeB4 --> nodeC1 --> nodeC2 --> nodeC3 --> nodeC4 ``` ### 4.3 Classification of Extrusion Processes — Hierarchical View ```mermaid flowchart TB start((Extrusion Technology)) start --> catA[Based on Drive Mechanism] start --> catB[Based on Polymer State] start --> catC[Based on Screw Configuration] catA --> a1[Ram Extrusion
Reciprocating Piston] catA --> a2[Screw Extrusion
Continuous Rotating Screw] catB --> b1[Wet Extrusion
Solvent Assisted] b1 --> b2[Food Industry] catB --> b3[Melting Extrusion
Dry Thermal] b3 --> b4[Polymer Industry] catC --> c1[Single Screw Extruder] catC --> c2[Twin Screw Extruder] catC --> c3[Multi Screw Extruder] c2 --> c21[Co Rotating Intermeshing] c2 --> c22[Counter Rotating Intermeshing] c2 --> c23[Counter Rotating Non Intermeshing] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Schematic Note:** The diagrams above are **block-level functional schematics** rather than physical drawings — this format aligns with KTU board examination expectations where students may be asked to draw or label **process flow diagrams** and **functional block diagrams** of extrusion systems. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "extrusion" and list any four end-products manufactured by extrusion.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Extrusion is a continuous manufacturing process in which a polymeric or food material, in solid or melt form, is forced under pressure through a shaped die to produce a continuous product of uniform cross-section. (2 marks) Four end-products: (½ mark each) 1. PVC pipes and HDPE pipes 2. Polymer sheets and films 3. Insulated electrical cables 4. Snack pellets and breakfast cereals (or aluminium sections) --- #### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **Define "single-screw extruder" and mention its three functional zones.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **single-screw extruder** is a continuous processing machine consisting of a single helical screw rotating inside a heated barrel, which conveys, melts, and pumps a polymer through a shaping die. (1½ marks) **Three functional zones:** (½ mark each) 1. **Feed Zone** — gravity conveying of solid pellets by friction drag. 2. **Compression / Melting Zone** — gradual decrease in channel depth combined with heat input to melt the polymer. 3. **Metering Zone** — fully melted polymer is pressurized and pumped at a uniform rate through the die. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not confuse** *extruder* (the machine) with *extrusion* (the process) — examiners deduct **1 mark** for this interchange. > 2. **Always state units** when writing throughput ($Q$) or pressure ($\Delta P$) — missing units cost **½ mark**. > 3. **Show all intermediate steps** in derivations. Skipping the drag-flow → net-flow algebraic manipulation costs **up to 4 marks** in a 14-mark question. > 4. **Label diagrams clearly** — unattributed block diagrams lose **2 marks** minimum. > 5. **Avoid** stating only the final numerical answer in numerical problems. Show at least **three working steps**. --- #### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Module 1]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the various functional zones of a single-screw extruder. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** A single-screw extruder is divided into **three functional zones** along its length, each performing a distinct operation on the polymer: **1. Feed Zone (Solids Conveying) — 2 marks:** Polymer pellets from the hopper fall into the screw channel. The rotating screw flights drag the pellets forward by **friction against the barrel wall**. This zone typically occupies **3 – 6 screw diameters** in length, with a constant channel depth ($H_d \approx 0.10D - 0.15D$). The mathematical model used is the **Tadmor–Klein solids-conveying equation**. **2. Compression / Transition Zone (Melting) — 2 marks:** The channel depth gradually decreases (compression ratio 2:1 to 4:1), compacting and compressing the polymer. Combined with external barrel heaters and viscous dissipation, the polymer melts. A **melt film** forms at the barrel wall; the **melt pool** widens as the solid bed shrinks. This zone is approximately **5 – 8 diameters** long. **3. Metering Zone (Melt Pumping) — 2 marks:** The channel depth is constant and shallowest. The polymer is fully molten and acts as a **Newtonian or shear-thinning fluid**. The screw acts as a positive displacement pump, generating pressure ($\Delta P = 10 - 30$ MPa) to force melt through the die. Length: **6 – 10 diameters**. **Block Diagram: 1 mark** — Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the labelled block-level functional architecture. --- **(b)** Derive the expression for the net throughput of a single-screw extruder assuming Newtonian melt behaviour. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. Steady-state, isothermal operation. 2. Polymer melt obeys Newton's law of viscosity ($\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$). 3. Screw channel is "unwound" into a flat rectangular geometry of width $W$, depth $H_d$, and helix angle $\theta$. 4. The barrel inner wall moves relative to the stationary screw at velocity $V_b = \pi D N$. 5. Flow is fully developed and laminar. 6. No leakage flow over the screw flights. **Derivation (5 marks):** For an "unwound" screw channel, the axial barrel velocity component is: $$V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta$$ **Drag-flow component** (from Couette-type flow): $$Q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\, V_{bx}\, W\, H_d\, \cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \pi D N W H_d\, \cos^{2}\theta$$ **Pressure-flow component** (from Poiseuille flow under die-induced $\Delta P$): $$Q_p = \frac{W H_d^{\,3}\, \Delta P}{12\, \mu\, L}$$ **Net throughput** is drag flow minus pressure (back) flow: $$\begin{aligned} Q &= Q_d - Q_p \\ &= \tfrac{1}{2}\, \pi D N W H_d\, \cos^{2}\theta - \frac{W H_d^{\,3}\, \Delta P}{12\, \mu\, L} \\ &= \alpha N - \frac{\beta}{\mu}\, \frac{\Delta P}{L} \end{aligned}$$ where $\alpha = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \pi D W H_d\, \cos^{2}\theta$ and $\beta = \dfrac{W H_d^{\,3}}{12}$. **Q.E.D.** --- #### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 1]` **(a)** Classify extruders based on (i) drive mechanism, (ii) screw configuration. Give two examples of products made by each class. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Classification based on drive mechanism — 3 marks:** **1. Ram (Plunger) Extruder:** - Uses a **reciprocating piston** to push polymer through a die in **batch-wise strokes**. - Advantages: Very high pressure (up to 500 MPa), suitable for PTFE (which cannot be melt-processed due to very high viscosity) and UHMWPE. - Products: **PTFE rods, UHMWPE sheets, sintered metal billets**. **2. Screw Extruder:** - Uses a **continuously rotating helical screw** for steady-state pumping. - Higher output, more uniform melt quality. - Products: **PVC pipes, LDPE films, snack pellets**. **(ii) Classification based on screw configuration — 4 marks:** **1. Single-Screw Extruder (SSE):** - One screw in a barrel; simplest and most common. - Products: **HDPE pipes, polymer sheets**. **2. Co-rotating Twin-Screw Extruder:** - Both screws rotate in the same direction, intermeshing. - Excellent mixing and self-wiping; used in **compounding and masterbatch**. - Products: **Glass-fibre reinforced compounds, PVC pellets**. **3. Counter-rotating Twin-Screw Extruder:** - Screws rotate in opposite directions. - Used in **PVC pipe extrusion** for low-output, high-quality applications. - Products: **Rigid PVC conduits**. **4. Multi-Screw Extruder:** Rare; used in specialty compounding. --- **(b)** What is die swell? Explain with the Bagley correction factor how it affects viscosity measurement in a capillary rheometer. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Die swell (extrudate swell) — 3 marks:** Die swell is the phenomenon in which the diameter of a polymer extrudate emerging from a die is greater than the diameter of the die orifice itself. It arises from the **elastic recovery** (recoil) of the oriented macromolecular chains once the constraint of the die wall is removed. Mathematically: $$S = \frac{D_e}{D_0}$$ where $D_e$ is the extrudate diameter and $D_0$ is the die orifice diameter. For highly elastic polymers (e.g., LDPE), $S$ can be **1.2 – 1.5**; for inelastic polymers (e.g., PA66), $S \approx 1.05$. **Bagley correction — 4 marks:** In capillary rheometry, the **total pressure drop** $\Delta P_{\text{total}}$ measured across the die contains two components: $$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_{\text{Capillary}} + \Delta P_{\text{End}}$$ The "end losses" arise from **converging flow at the die entry**, including **elastic stretching** and **vortex formation**. Bagley proposed a correction by plotting $\Delta P$ versus $L/R$ for several dies at constant shear rate and extrapolating to zero length: $$\Delta P_{\text{End}} = n_c \cdot \frac{2\tau_w}{1}$$ The **true wall shear stress** becomes: $$\tau_{w,\text{true}} = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{total}}}{2\left(\dfrac{L_d}{R} + n_c\right)} = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{total}}}{B}$$ where $B = \dfrac{2 L_d}{D_d} + n_c$ is the **Bagley correction factor**. This correction is essential for **elastic polymers** (e.g., LDPE) where end losses can exceed **30 – 50 %** of total pressure drop. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Definition" topic — Module 1** - ✅ **Extrusion** = Continuous, pressure-driven shaping of polymer through a die. - ✅ **Extruder** = Machine performing extrusion; barrel + screw + die + drive. - ✅ **Three classical screw zones:** Feed → Compression → Metering. - ✅ **Throughput equation (Newtonian):** $Q = \alpha N - \dfrac{\beta \Delta P}{\mu L}$. - ✅ **Drag flow** $\propto N$; **Pressure flow** $\propto \Delta P / \mu$. - ✅ **Wall shear rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{\pi D N}{H_d}$. - ✅ **Bagley correction** $B = \dfrac{2 L_d}{D_d} + n_c$ corrects end-pressure losses. - ✅ **Rabinowitsch correction** adjusts shear rate for **non-Newtonian** fluids. - ✅ **Die swell** $S = D_e / D_0$ — measures melt elasticity. - ✅ **Adiabatic melt temperature rise** $\Delta T = Q \Delta P / (\dot{m} c_p)$. - ✅ **Six functional zones** of an extrusion **line**: hopper → barrel → die → calibration → cooling → haul-off → cutting. - ✅ **Classification:** Ram vs. Screw; Single vs. Twin (co-/counter-rotating). - ✅ **Standard L/D ratio:** 20:1 – 40:1 for modern single-screw extruders. - ✅ **Typical operating temperature window:** 150 °C – 280 °C. - ✅ **Major industries served:** Polymer, Food (snacks, cereals), Metal (Al, Cu), Pharmaceutical, Ceramic. - ✅ **Historical note:** Paul Troester (Germany, 1930s) — first modern thermoplastic extruder; later refinements by **Maddock, Tadmor, Klein**.

topics.title: Introduction to Extruders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Extruders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Extruder (KTU 2024 Definition):** An extruder is a continuous high-pressure, high-throughput thermo-mechanical processing machine that converts solid polymeric (or metallic/food/ceramic) feedstock into a homogeneous melt (or plasticized state) and forces it through a shaped die to produce a continuous profile of uniform cross-section.

The term **"extrusion"** is derived from the Latin word *extrudere*, meaning *"to push out"*. In polymer processing, extrusion is the foundational operation used to manufacture pipes, sheets, films, cables, profiles, fibers, and compounded masterbatches. The extruder is the **heart of any extrusion line** because it performs three critical unit operations simultaneously:

1. **Solid Conveying** – transport of polymer pellets/granules from the hopper.
2. **Melting (Plasticating)** – conversion of solid polymer into a continuous molten phase.
3. **Metering (Melt Pumping)** – delivery of a homogeneous melt at a steady, controllable rate to the die.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** An extruder is **not** just a pump. It is a *solid-transport device + melting device + melt pump* combined into a single rotating screw-barrel assembly. This three-functionality is the reason screws have distinct *feed, compression, and metering zones*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sausage Maker / Toothpaste Tube"

Imagine a giant industrial **sausage stuffer** (or, more simply, a **toothpaste tube**):

| Everyday Object | Engineering Counterpart |
|---|---|
| The squeezing hand pushing paste out | The **rotating screw** providing axial thrust |
| The tube walls offering resistance | The **barrel** providing friction & heating |
| The narrow nozzle shaping the jet | The **die** determining the final profile |
| The toothpaste (paste-like, viscous) | The **polymer melt** (non-Newtonian, viscous) |
| Your hand pumping repeatedly | Continuous **rotation of the screw** |

In the toothpaste analogy, the paste *flows* only when you squeeze. In an extruder, the screw keeps rotating continuously, so the polymer melt *flows steadily* — this is why extrusion is a **continuous (not batch) process** and offers extremely high productivity compared to injection molding.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **L/D Ratio (Length-to-Diameter Ratio):** typically **20:1 to 30:1** for single-screw extruders; up to **48:1** for compounding.
- **Compression Ratio (CR):** the ratio of feed-zone channel depth to metering-zone channel depth, normally **2:1 to 4:1**.
- **Screw Speed (N):** typically **20 to 200 rpm**; high-speed extruders reach **500–1200 rpm**.
- **Throughput (Q):** measured in **kg/hr**, generally **5 to 5000 kg/hr** depending on screw diameter.
- **Barrel Temperature Profile:** zones T₁ → T₂ → T₃ → T₄ (rising), commonly **150 °C to 280 °C** for polyolefins.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** If a question mentions *"a screw of 60 mm diameter and 30:1 L/D"*, the screw length is **60 × 30 = 1800 mm**. Always compute screw length from L/D before solving any throughput or melting problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Functional Zones of a Screw (Conceptual Breakdown)

A single-screw extruder screw is a precisely engineered **helical flighted shaft** whose channel depth varies along its length. KTU examiners frequently test the role of each zone.

### Zone 1 — Solids Conveying Zone (Feed Zone)
- **Location:** Beneath the hopper.
- **Channel Depth:** constant and **maximum** (deepest flight).
- **Mechanism:** *Drag-induced plug flow*. Solid pellets are conveyed forward by friction against the barrel and pushed back by friction against the screw root.
- **Key Physics:** governed by the **Mohr-Coulomb friction model** between polymer and metal surfaces.
- **Why deep channels?** To maximize the volume of solids conveyed per rotation and prevent premature compression (which would cause melting to occur too early).

### Zone 2 — Compression / Melting Zone (Transition Zone)
- **Channel Depth:** **tapered (decreasing)** linearly or exponentially.
- **Mechanism:** **Melt Film on the barrel wall**. The combination of barrel heating and viscous shear (mechanical dissipation) causes the polymer to melt in a thin film. The melt is scraped off by the pushing flight and accumulates in the rear of the channel, forming a *melt pool*.
- **Key Physics:** governed by the **Tadmor melting model** (1966) — still the canonical framework used in KTU textbooks.

### Zone 3 — Metering Zone (Pumping Zone)
- **Channel Depth:** constant and **minimum** (shallowest flight).
- **Mechanism:** The screw now acts purely as a **positive-displacement pump**. The shallow, uniform channel converts screw rotation into a steady, pulse-free melt flow.
- **Key Physics:** governed by the **Newtonian fluid flow in a rectangular channel** model.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table** — these equations appear in **≥ 80% of KTU numerical questions** on extrusion.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Screw Surface Velocity | $V = \pi D N$ | m/s | Barrel's tangential velocity relative to screw root |
| 2 | L/D Ratio | $L/D$ | dimensionless | Ratio of screw length to diameter |
| 3 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{h_f}{h_m}$ | dimensionless | Feed depth ÷ metering depth |
| 4 | Drag Flow Rate | $Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2}\, V\, W\, h\,\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | m³/s | Forward flow due to barrel drag |
| 5 | Pressure Flow Rate | $Q_p = -\dfrac{W\, h^{3}}{12\mu}\,\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\,\sin^{2}\theta$ | m³/s | Backward leak flow due to die pressure |
| 6 | Net Output (Newtonian) | $Q = Q_d - Q_p = \alpha N - \dfrac{\beta\, \Delta P}{\mu}$ | m³/s | Total extruder throughput |
| 7 | Extruder Constant $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} h \sin\theta\cos\theta}{2}$ | m³ | Geometry-dependent constant |
| 8 | Extruder Constant $\beta$ | $\beta = \dfrac{\pi D h^{3} \sin^{2}\theta}{12}$ | m³ | Geometry-dependent constant |
| 9 | Power Consumption | $P = Q\,\Delta P$ | W | Mechanical power to drive screw |
| 10 | Specific Energy | $SE = \dfrac{P}{Q_{mass}}$ | J/kg or kWh/kg | Energy per kg of polymer processed |
| 11 | Mean Residence Time | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{barrel}}{Q}$ | s | Average time polymer spends inside extruder |
| 12 | Die Swell Ratio | $B = \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$ | dimensionless | Elastic recovery of melt on exiting die |

> **Note on LaTeX:** Whenever a vertical bar or absolute-value symbol appears in exam scripts, KTU examiners accept both $\vert x \vert$ and $\lvert x \rvert$ — write whichever is cleaner.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

The above equations are **the design backbone of every screw extrusion line in industry**:

- A **pipe manufacturer** uses Equations 4–6 to size the screw for the desired kg/hr output before purchasing a die.
- A **compounding engineer** uses Equation 7 ($\alpha$) to compare the pumping efficiency of different screw geometries.
- A **process engineer** uses Equation 11 ($\tau$) to predict thermal degradation (long residence time $\Rightarrow$ yellowing of PVC or degradation of PET).
- A **sustainability engineer** uses Equation 10 (SE) to compare the energy footprint of single-screw vs. twin-screw extrusion — twin-screw extruders typically consume **0.15–0.25 kWh/kg**, while single-screw consume **0.20–0.35 kWh/kg**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Metering-Zone Output Equation

We now derive the **canonical KTU board equation** $Q = \alpha N - \beta \Delta P / \mu$ step by step.

### Step 0 — Geometric Setup (Unwound Channel)

The helical screw channel is "unwound" into a flat rectangular channel of:
- Width $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e$ (where $e$ is flight width)
- Depth $h$ (metering zone depth)
- Length $L$ along the helix
- The bottom (screw root) is stationary; the top (barrel) moves at velocity $V = \pi D N$ in the down-channel direction.

### Step 1 — Velocity Profile of a Newtonian Fluid

For steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid in a rectangular channel (assuming $W \gg h$ so side walls are negligible), the Navier–Stokes equation in the cross-channel ($y$) direction reduces to:

$$\dfrac{dp}{dz} = \mu\, \dfrac{d^{2}u}{dy^{2}}$$

### Step 2 — Integrate Twice with No-Slip Boundary Conditions

Apply boundary conditions:
- At the screw root ($y = 0$): $u = 0$ (no slip on stationary screw).
- At the barrel wall ($y = h$): $u = V$ (no slip on moving barrel).

Integrating once:

$$\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}\left(\dfrac{dp}{dz}\right)\, y + C_1$$

Integrating twice:

$$u(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\left(\dfrac{dp}{dz}\right)\, y^{2} + C_1 y + C_2$$

Apply BC1 ($y=0$, $u=0$) → $C_2 = 0$.

Apply BC2 ($y=h$, $u=V$) → $C_1 = \dfrac{V}{h} - \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dz}\, h$.

Therefore the velocity profile is:

$$u(y) = \dfrac{V\, y}{h} - \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dz}\left(hy - y^{2}\right)$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The first term is **pure drag flow** (linear Couette profile). The second term is **pressure-driven Poiseuille back-flow** (parabolic profile opposing forward flow).

### Step 3 — Volumetric Flow Rate per Unit Width

$$q = \int_{0}^{h} u(y)\, dy = \dfrac{V\, h}{2} - \dfrac{h^{3}}{12\mu}\dfrac{dp}{dz}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Helical (Screw) Coordinates

The down-channel flow $q$ must be projected onto the axial (down-barrel) direction by multiplying with $\cos\theta$, and the channel length $L$ must be expressed along the helix. The total volumetric flow rate is:

$$Q = q\, W \cos\theta = \dfrac{1}{2}\,V\,W\,h\cos\theta - \dfrac{W\,h^{3}}{12\mu}\,\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\cos\theta$$

Substitute $V = \pi D N$ and the helix-angle geometry $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$:

$$Q = \dfrac{1}{2}\,(\pi D N)\,(\pi D \sin\theta)\, h \cos\theta - \dfrac{(\pi D \sin\theta)\, h^{3}}{12\mu}\,\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

### Step 5 — Simplify into the KTU Compact Form

$$\boxed{\,Q = \underbrace{\dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} h \sin\theta\cos\theta}{2}}_{\alpha}\, N \;-\; \underbrace{\dfrac{\pi D h^{3} \sin^{2}\theta}{12}}_{\beta}\,\dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu}\,}$$

This is the **exact formula** KTU examiners expect in derivation questions. Memorize it as:

$$Q = \alpha N - \dfrac{\beta\, \Delta P}{\mu}$$

### Step 6 — Operating Point on the Screw Characteristic Curve

When the die is removed ($\Delta P = 0$), the extruder delivers the **maximum (open-discharge) flow**:

$$Q_{\max} = \alpha N$$

When the die is fully closed ($\Delta P$ very large), $Q \to 0$. In practice, the actual operating point is the **intersection** of the *screw line* (extruder characteristic) and the *die line* (die characteristic $Q = K\, \Delta P / \mu$). This is the classical **operating point analysis** that KTU examiners ask in Part B 14-mark problems.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Plug-and-Chug Calculation

> **Given:** Screw diameter $D = 50$ mm, screw speed $N = 60$ rpm, metering depth $h = 3$ mm, helix angle $\theta = 17.65°$, die pressure $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^{6}$ Pa, melt viscosity $\mu = 1000$ Pa·s.
> **Find:** Net throughput $Q$ in cm³/s.

**Step A — Convert units** ($D = 0.05$ m, $h = 0.003$ m, $N = 1$ rev/s).

**Step B — Compute $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$**

$$\sin(17.65°) \approx 0.3032, \quad \cos(17.65°) \approx 0.9529$$

**Step C — Compute $\alpha$**

$$\alpha = \dfrac{\pi^{2} (0.05)^{2} (0.003) (0.3032)(0.9529)}{2}$$

$$\alpha = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 0.0025 \times 0.003 \times 0.2890}{2} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step D — Compute $\beta$**

$$\beta = \dfrac{\pi (0.05) (0.003)^{3} (0.3032)^{2}}{12} \approx 1.08 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step E — Compute drag flow**

$$Q_d = \alpha N = (1.07 \times 10^{-5})(1) = 1.07 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 10.7\ \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step F — Compute pressure back-flow**

$$Q_p = \dfrac{\beta\, \Delta P}{\mu} = \dfrac{(1.08 \times 10^{-9})(5 \times 10^{6})}{1000} = 5.4 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} = 5.4\ \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step G — Net throughput**

$$Q = Q_d - Q_p = 10.7 - 5.4 = 5.3\ \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s}$$

✅ **Answer:** $Q \approx 5.3$ cm³/s (≈ 19.1 kg/hr for a polymer of density 1000 kg/m³).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Extrusion Line

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete extrusion line as KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to draw in 14-mark questions. All node IDs are alphanumeric and contain no reserved keywords.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Raw Material Hopper] --> B1[Solid Conveying Zone]
    B1 --> C1[Compression and Melting Zone]
    C1 --> D1[Melt Metering Zone]
    D1 --> E1[Breaker Plate and Screen Pack]
    E1 --> F1[Shaping Die]
    F1 --> G1[Cooling and Calibration Unit]
    G1 --> H1[Haul Off and Cutting Station]
    H1 --> I1[Finished Profile]

    J1[Barrel Heater Bands] -.-> B1
    J1 -.-> C1
    J1 -.-> D1

    K1[Drive Motor and Gearbox] --> D1
    L1[Temperature Controllers] -.-> J1
    M1[Pressure Transducer] -.-> E1
    N1[Screw Rotates via Coupling] --> D1
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **solid arrows** (→) show the *primary flow of polymer* from hopper to finished product.
> - The **dashed arrows** (-.->) show the *control / auxiliary subsystems* that regulate the process (heaters, controllers, sensors, motor drive).

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Anatomy of a Single-Screw Extruder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ExtruderBarrelAssembly
        A2[Hopper Throat with Feed Throat Cooling] --> B2[Feed Zone Channel Depth h1 Maximum]
        B2 --> C2[Compression Zone Channel Tapers to h2]
        C2 --> D2[Metering Zone Channel Depth h2 Minimum]
        D2 --> E2[Breaker Plate and Screen Pack]
        E2 --> F2[Adapter and Die]
    end

    G2[Screw Root Stationary] --- H2[Flight Helical Land]
    H2 --- I2[Barrel Inner Wall Moving Tangentially]
    I2 --- J2[Melt Film Shear Layer]
    J2 --- K2[Melt Pool Accumulates at Pushing Flight]
    K2 --- L2[Axial Pressure Gradient Drives Flow]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The screw root is *stationary*, while the *barrel inner wall* drags the melt forward via the no-slip condition. The melt film is the locus of maximum shear and is where **most mechanical energy dissipation** occurs — this is why shear heating is critical in the melting zone.

## 4.3 Component-Level Responsibility Matrix

| Component | Function | KTU Exam Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Hopper** | Gravity feeds pellets into the screw | Low (rarely asked) |
| **Feed Throat** | Conical entry, often water-cooled | Medium |
| **Barrel** | Contains melt; heated externally | High |
| **Screw** | Conveys, melts, meters the polymer | **Very High** (most asked) |
| **Heater Bands** | External heating at multiple zones | Medium |
| **Breaker Plate** | Converts rotational flow to axial flow; supports screen | Medium |
| **Screen Pack** | Filters contaminants; creates back-pressure | Medium |
| **Die** | Shapes the final profile | **Very High** |
| **Drive Motor + Gearbox** | Provides torque at controlled RPM | Low |
| **Cooling / Calibration** | Solidifies extrudate (water bath, air, vacuum tank) | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions test *recall and one-line reasoning*. Answers should fit within **half a page**.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an extruder. List any three primary functions performed by the screw of a single-screw extruder.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An extruder is a continuous thermo-mechanical processing machine that converts solid polymeric feedstock into a homogeneous melt and forces it through a shaped die to obtain a continuous profile of uniform cross-section.
The screw performs three primary functions:
1. **Solid conveying** — transport of polymer pellets from the hopper along the feed zone. *(1 mark)*
2. **Melting / plasticating** — conversion of the solid polymer into a continuous melt phase by combined external heating and viscous shear. *(1 mark)*
3. **Metering / melt pumping** — delivery of a homogeneous melt at a steady, controllable rate to the die. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is meant by L/D ratio and compression ratio of an extruder screw? State their typical ranges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **L/D Ratio:** It is the ratio of the effective screw length (L) to the screw outer diameter (D). It determines the residence time, melting capacity, and mixing efficiency. Typical range: **20:1 to 30:1** for single-screw extrusion; up to **48:1** for compounding extruders. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Compression Ratio (CR):** It is the ratio of the channel depth in the feed zone ($h_f$) to the channel depth in the metering zone ($h_m$), i.e., $CR = h_f / h_m$. Typical range: **2:1 to 4:1** depending on polymer type. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must attempt **either OR choice**.

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a single-screw extruder. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
A single-screw extruder consists of the following components: **hopper, barrel, screw, heaters, drive system, breaker plate, and die**.

1. **Hopper (0.5 mark):** A funnel-shaped container at the feed throat that holds and gravity-feeds the polymer pellets into the screw channel.
2. **Barrel (0.5 mark):** A heavy steel cylinder that houses the screw. It is externally fitted with **electric heater bands** (typically 3 to 5 zones) and may be lined with bimetallic alloy (e.g., nitrided steel) for wear resistance.
3. **Screw (2 marks):** A helical flighted shaft with three distinct zones:
   - *Feed Zone:* constant deep channel, conveys solids.
   - *Compression Zone:* tapered channel depth, melts the polymer.
   - *Metering Zone:* constant shallow channel, pumps the melt.
4. **Drive System (0.5 mark):** A motor connected through a gearbox provides controlled rotation (typically 20–200 rpm).
5. **Breaker Plate and Screen Pack (1 mark):** Converts the helical melt flow into a straight axial flow; the screen pack filters contaminants and generates back-pressure.
6. **Die (1 mark):** Shaped tooling that gives the final cross-sectional geometry to the extrudate (pipe, sheet, profile, film, etc.).
7. **Working (1 mark):** Pellets fall from the hopper, are conveyed forward, melted by heater bands + shear, metered through the die, cooled externally, and hauled off as a finished continuous product.

*Sketch: refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2 above — 1 mark.*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the volumetric output of a single-screw extruder in the metering zone. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**
*(See the complete derivation in Section 3.1 above — full marks.)*
**Final expression:**
$$Q = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} h \sin\theta\cos\theta}{2}\, N \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D h^{3} \sin^{2}\theta}{12}\,\dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu}$$
**Assumptions:** *(1 mark each)*
1. Steady, laminar, isothermal flow.
2. Newtonian fluid behaviour (constant viscosity $\mu$).
3. Wide rectangular channel ($W \gg h$, side walls negligible).
4. Fully developed flow; no leakage over the flights.
5. Screw root stationary, barrel wall moving at $V = \pi D N$.
6. Incompressible melt.

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Classify extruders. Compare single-screw and twin-screw extruders on any seven points. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Classification of Extruders:** *(2 marks)*

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| By Drive | Single-screw, Twin-screw (co-rotating / counter-rotating), Multi-screw, Ram (plunger) |
| By Application | Compounding, Film blowing, Pipe, Sheet, Profile, Co-extrusion |
| By Operating Mode | Continuous, Batch (ram) |

**Comparison Table:** *(5 marks, 0.7 each)*

| # | Parameter | Single-Screw | Twin-Screw |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost | Lower | Higher |
| 2 | Mixing efficiency | Poor to moderate | Excellent |
| 3 | Self-wiping action | No | Yes (especially co-rotating) |
| 4 | Feeding of difficult materials (powder, sticky) | Poor | Excellent |
| 5 | Throughput predictability | Good (Newtonian model works) | Moderate (complex flow) |
| 6 | Shear-induced degradation | Lower | Higher (high-shear regions) |
| 7 | Typical applications | Pipes, films, profiles | Compounding, masterbatch, reactive extrusion |

**(b)** Define (i) drag flow, (ii) pressure flow, and (iii) leakage flow. A 60 mm diameter extruder screw with a 20° helix angle, 3 mm metering depth, rotating at 90 rpm, discharges polymer melt ($\mu = 800$ Pa·s) against a die pressure of 4 MPa. Calculate the net throughput. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definitions:** *(1.5 marks)*
- **Drag Flow ($Q_d$):** Forward flow of melt caused purely by the dragging action of the rotating barrel wall on the melt film. Depends on $N$, $D$, $h$, $\theta$.
- **Pressure Flow ($Q_p$):** Backward (leakage) flow caused by the pressure gradient built up at the die, which opposes the drag flow.
- **Leakage Flow ($Q_L$):** Small backward flow over the flight tips through the clearance between flight land and barrel; usually neglected in the simple Newtonian model.

**Calculation:** *(5.5 marks)*
Given: $D = 0.06$ m, $\theta = 20°$, $h = 0.003$ m, $N = 90/60 = 1.5$ rev/s, $\mu = 800$ Pa·s, $\Delta P = 4 \times 10^{6}$ Pa.

$$\sin 20° = 0.342, \quad \cos 20° = 0.940$$

$$\alpha = \dfrac{\pi^{2}(0.06)^{2}(0.003)(0.342)(0.940)}{2} \approx 1.71 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{3}$$

$$\beta = \dfrac{\pi(0.06)(0.003)^{3}(0.342)^{2}}{12} \approx 1.66 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^{3}$$

$$Q_d = \alpha N = (1.71 \times 10^{-5})(1.5) = 2.57 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$Q_p = \dfrac{\beta\, \Delta P}{\mu} = \dfrac{(1.66 \times 10^{-9})(4 \times 10^{6})}{800} = 8.30 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{net}} = Q_d - Q_p \approx 1.74 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 17.4\ \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students 2–4 marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Students forget to convert rpm → rev/s when computing $Q_d = \alpha N$. Always divide by 60.
> 2. **Mixing up compression ratio:** Some students invert it as $h_m / h_f$ instead of $h_f / h_m$. KTU textbook convention is $CR = h_f / h_m$.
> 3. **Neglecting the helix angle projection:** Using $\sin\theta$ instead of $\sin\theta\cos\theta$ in the drag flow term loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Assuming $\theta = 0$:** A common approximation error; the standard KTU expectation is to retain $\theta$ unless explicitly told to assume a "shallow channel" approximation.
> 5. **Forgetting assumptions in derivations:** Even if the math is correct, KTU evaluators deduct up to 2 marks if the assumptions (Newtonian, steady, laminar, no leakage, fully developed) are not stated.
> 6. **Confusing L/D with L alone:** "A 30:1 L/D screw of 60 mm diameter" means the screw length is **1800 mm**, not 30 mm.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Use this as your last-minute revision checklist before the KTU exam.**

- ✅ An extruder is a **continuous** processing machine combining solids conveying + melting + melt metering.
- ✅ A single-screw extruder has **three functional zones**: feed, compression, metering.
- ✅ The metering zone is a **Newtonian rectangular-channel flow** problem in an unwound coordinate system.
- ✅ **Master equation:** $Q = \alpha N - \beta \Delta P / \mu$, with $\alpha = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} h \sin\theta\cos\theta}{2}$ and $\beta = \dfrac{\pi D h^{3} \sin^{2}\theta}{12}$.
- ✅ **Drag flow** is forward (proportional to $N$); **pressure flow** is backward (proportional to $\Delta P$).
- ✅ **L/D ratio** controls residence time; **compression ratio** controls melting efficiency.
- ✅ The **operating point** is the intersection of the screw characteristic curve and the die characteristic curve.
- ✅ **Twin-screw extruders** are preferred for compounding, devolatilization, and reactive extrusion because of superior mixing and self-wiping action.
- ✅ **Die swell** (extrudate swell) occurs due to elastic recovery of the polymer melt on exiting the die.
- ✅ KTU exam-favorite numerical quantities: typical $D = 25$–$150$ mm; typical $N = 20$–$200$ rpm; typical $\theta \approx 17.65°$ (corresponding to a "square-pitched" screw where lead = $D$).
- ✅ **Specific energy** of single-screw extrusion ≈ 0.20–0.35 kWh/kg; twin-screw ≈ 0.15–0.25 kWh/kg.
- ✅ Always quote **assumptions** before any derivation; always quote **units** at the end of any numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extrusion** is a *bulk deformation manufacturing process* in which a cylindrical billet (workpiece) is plastically deformed by being forced under high compressive load through a *die orifice* of smaller cross-sectional area, thereby producing a continuous product of uniform, desired cross-section.

In formal KTU 2024 scheme terminology, extrusion belongs to the family of **compressive bulk forming processes** (alongside forging, rolling, and drawing). The driving force is a *ram* (or piston) that pushes the billet, while the die geometry dictates the final shape. Unlike drawing, the *die* in extrusion remains stationary and the material flows *through* it under hydrostatic compressive stress.

The generic extrusion statement is:

$$\text{Billet (large cross-section)} \xrightarrow{\text{Compressive Ram Force}} \text{Die Orifice} \rightarrow \text{Product (small cross-section)}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Toothpaste Tube

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste. When you press the bottom of the tube with your thumb, the paste *flows* out through the narrow opening at the tip in a continuous ribbon. The paste does not "fall" out — it is *pushed* (compressed) and *shaped* simultaneously. The toothpaste tube is the *container*, your thumb is the *ram*, the paste is the *billet*, and the nozzle is the *die*. Exactly this happens in industrial metal extrusion — only at pressures of **300 – 1200 MPa**, temperatures of **20 – 1300 °C**, and with materials ranging from aluminium to super-alloys.

A second powerful analogy is the **cookie press / play-dough extruder** used by children — the dough is forced through shaped dies (stars, flowers) to obtain a uniform cross-section. This perfectly captures the *continuous, constant-profile* nature of extrusion.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Favourites)

| Term | Definition (as per KTU 2024 Scheme) |
|---|---|
| **Billet** | The starting cylindrical workpiece, typically pre-heated. |
| **Container** | The thick-walled chamber that holds the billet against the die. |
| **Ram / Stem** | The plunger that transmits the press force onto the billet. |
| **Die** | The shaped tool containing one or more orifices; determines product section. |
| **Dummy Block** | A disc placed between the ram and the hot billet to protect the ram. |
| **Bearing Land** | The straight cylindrical land of the die that provides final shape and back-pressure. |
| **Extrusion Ratio (R)** | $R = A_0 / A_f$ — ratio of initial to final cross-sectional area. |
| **Extrusion Constant (K)** | $K = P / \sigma_f$ — ratio of actual ram pressure to flow stress. |
| **Break-through Pressure** | The peak pressure required to initiate plastic flow through the die. |
| **Steady-State Pressure** | The lower, sustained pressure during stable material flow. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Examiners *frequently* test the difference between **break-through pressure** (initial peak) and **steady-state pressure** (running pressure). Memorise both — this is a 3-mark favourite.

## 1.4 Why Extrusion? — The Engineering Motivation

Extrusion is preferred over other forming processes when:
- A **constant cross-section** is required over a long length (e.g., window frames, rails, tubes).
- The material has **low ductility** in tension but high ductility in compression (e.g., magnesium).
- **Brittle materials** (tungsten, molybdenum) must be shaped — extrusion works because the *compressive* stress state suppresses crack propagation.
- **Complex hollow sections** are required (e.g., aluminium heat-sink profiles) — these are impossible to obtain by rolling or forging alone.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics of Extrusion

Extrusion works on the principle of **plastic deformation under predominantly compressive stress**. The billet, constrained within the container, experiences three principal stresses:

$$\sigma_1 < 0, \quad \sigma_2 < 0, \quad \sigma_3 < 0$$

(i.e., all three are compressive). The material flows in the direction of **least resistance** — towards the die exit. This triaxial compression is what allows even brittle materials to deform without fracture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept (KTU 2024):** The *hydrostatic* (mean) component of stress governs *cracking tendency*, while the *deviatoric* component governs *plastic flow*. In extrusion, the hydrostatic component is large and negative, suppressing fracture, while the deviatoric component drives shape change.

## 2.2 Stages of the Extrusion Process (Logical Flow)

1. **Loading** — The pre-heated (or room-temperature) billet is placed inside the container.
2. **Upsetting** — The ram advances; the billet first *upsets* (shortens radially) to fill any clearance with the container walls.
3. **Break-through (Initial Upsetting → Flow Onset)** — Pressure rises sharply as the material begins to yield and *break through* the die land. This is the **peak pressure**.
4. **Steady-State Flow** — Material flows plastically through the die at a near-constant, lower pressure. The deformation zone is stationary relative to the die.
5. **Stopping / Discard Formation** — As the ram approaches the die, a thin *butt* or *discard* remains; the ram is retracted, and the discard is sheared off.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most-tested formula cluster in Module 1.** Memorise this table; questions in Part A and Part B routinely combine 2–3 of these equations.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$ | Extrusion ratio (always $\geq 1$) | $A_0$ = initial area [m²]; $A_f$ = final area [m²] |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \ln(R) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | True (logarithmic) strain in extrusion | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P_{\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \,\ln(R)$ | Ideal (frictionless) extrusion pressure | $P$ [Pa]; $\sigma_f$ = flow stress [Pa] |
| 4 | $P_{\text{real}} = \sigma_f \!\left[\ln(R) + \dfrac{2L}{D}\right]$ | Pressure with interfacial friction (forward ext.) | $L$ = billet length [m]; $D$ = billet diameter [m] |
| 5 | $F_{\text{ram}} = P \cdot A_0$ | Total ram force required | $F$ [N] |
| 6 | $K = \dfrac{P}{\sigma_f} = \ln(R) + \dfrac{2L}{D}$ | Extrusion constant (dimensionless) | — |
| 7 | $P_{\text{ind}} = \sigma_f \ln(R)$ | Indirect (backward) extrusion — friction-free | — |
| 8 | $P_{\text{hyd}} = \sigma_f \ln(R)$ | Hydrostatic extrusion — friction-free, no billet-container contact | — |
| 9 | $W = P \cdot A_0 \cdot L$ | Total deformation work (approx.) | $W$ [J] |
| 10 | $P_{\text{impact}} \approx 2\sigma_f \ln(R)$ | Impact extrusion peak pressure | — |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{ideal}}}{P_{\text{real}}}$ | Process efficiency (friction indicator) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{V \tan\phi}{L_d}$ | Strain rate inside deformation zone | $V$ = ram speed; $\phi$ = die semi-angle; $L_d$ = deformation length |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety:** All absolute values and ratios in formulas use the inline `R = A_0 / A_f` form above. If you ever write the equation `P = K · |σ_f|` in your exam, write the modulus as `\vert \sigma_f \vert` to prevent KTU answer-sheet markdown rendering errors in digital submissions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above are used daily in:

- **Aluminium architectural profiles** (window frames, door frames) — hot direct extrusion of AA6063 billets.
- **Copper busbars and cables** — high R ratios achieved via hydrostatic extrusion for oxygen-free copper.
- **Tungsten and molybdenum rods** — used for filaments and X-ray targets; extrusion exploits compressive state to shape otherwise brittle refractory metals.
- **Lead-acid battery grids** — cold impact extrusion of lead alloy.
- **Polymer profiles** (PVC pipes) — the same fundamental principles apply, but with viscoelastic constitutive laws.
- **Aerospace titanium sections** — hot extrusion of Ti-6Al-4V in inert atmosphere.

## 2.5 Factors Affecting Extrusion Pressure

The ram pressure depends on (in order of significance):

1. **Extrusion ratio $R$** — higher $R$ → exponentially higher pressure.
2. **Flow stress $\sigma_f$** — governed by material, temperature, strain, and strain rate.
3. **Friction at billet-container interface** — raises pressure by up to **30–50%** in long billets.
4. **Die geometry** (semi-angle $\phi$) — optimal $\phi \approx 45°$–$60°$ minimises redundant work.
5. **Ram speed** — higher speeds raise adiabatic heating → lower $\sigma_f$ → lower pressure (in hot extrusion).
6. **Temperature** — the single most powerful lever; every +100 °C roughly halves $\sigma_f$ for aluminium.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Extrusion Pressure (Frictionless Case)

We start from the **slab method (upper-bound analysis)**, which is the KTU board's preferred analytical method.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**
Consider a cylindrical billet of initial length $L_0$ and area $A_0$ being reduced to area $A_f$ through a frictionless die. The deformation is treated as occurring in a conical zone of semi-angle $\phi$.

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium to a slab element.**
A thin slab of area $A$ at the entry side, with the flow stress $\sigma_f$, transmits load $F$ across it. Equilibrium gives:

$$F = p \cdot A$$

where $p$ is the instantaneous pressure inside the deformation zone.

**Step 3 — Apply the constancy of volume principle.**
Since the material is incompressible (plastic deformation), volume is conserved:

$$A_0 \cdot dx_0 = A_f \cdot dx_f$$

where $dx_0$ and $dx_f$ are the incremental displacements at the entry and exit respectively.

**Step 4 — Express true strain.**
The true (logarithmic) strain in uniaxial compression-equivalent extrusion is:

$$\varepsilon = \int_{A_0}^{A_f} \dfrac{dA}{A} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln(R)$$

**Step 5 — Write the work-equivalence equation.**
The work done by the ram equals the work of plastic deformation (per unit volume $\times$ volume):

$$P_{\text{ideal}} \cdot A_0 \cdot dx_0 = \sigma_f \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_0 \cdot dx_0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the ideal pressure.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{ideal}} \cdot A_0 \cdot dx_0 &= \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R) \cdot A_0 \cdot dx_0 \\
\implies P_{\text{ideal}} &= \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental ideal-extrusion equation**, valid for frictionless direct, indirect, and hydrostatic extrusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Even in the *frictionless* ideal case, the extrusion pressure is *not* zero — it is the plastic work of the logarithmic strain. This is why high R ratios require very high pressures, regardless of friction.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Real Pressure with Friction (Forward / Direct Extrusion)

**Step 1 — Identify the friction contribution.**
In direct (forward) extrusion, the billet slides against the container wall over its entire length $L$. The frictional shear stress is:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot p_{\text{contact}} \approx \mu \cdot \sigma_f$$

(by the sticking-friction assumption used in bulk forming).

**Step 2 — Compute the friction force on the container wall.**
The lateral surface area of contact is $\pi D L$ (for a cylindrical billet of diameter $D$). The friction force is therefore:

$$F_{\text{friction}} = \tau \cdot \pi D L = \mu \, \sigma_f \, \pi D L$$

**Step 3 — Convert this to an *equivalent pressure* at the ram face.**
Divide by the billet cross-section $A_0 = \pi D^2 / 4$:

$$\Delta P_{\text{friction}} = \dfrac{\mu \, \sigma_f \, \pi D L}{\pi D^2 / 4} = \dfrac{4 \mu L \sigma_f}{D}$$

**Step 4 — Combine with the ideal pressure.**
By the principle of superposition (approximate, but standard at KTU level):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{real, forward}} &= P_{\text{ideal}} + \Delta P_{\text{friction}} \\
P_{\text{real, forward}} &= \sigma_f \ln(R) + \dfrac{4 \mu L}{D} \,\sigma_f \\
P_{\text{real, forward}} &= \sigma_f \!\left[ \ln(R) + \dfrac{4 \mu L}{D} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU simplification:** Many textbooks use the form $2L/D$ instead of $4\mu L/D$ by absorbing the friction coefficient into the geometric term, giving $P = \sigma_f[\ln(R) + 2L/D]$. Both forms are accepted in KTU examinations; the $4\mu L/D$ form is more physically faithful.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model Answer)

> **Problem (KTU style):** A cylindrical aluminium billet of diameter **80 mm** and length **250 mm** is extruded at 400 °C. The final section is a square of side **30 mm**. The flow stress at this temperature is **35 MPa**, and the Coulomb friction coefficient at the container wall is **0.08**. Calculate (a) the extrusion ratio, (b) the true strain, (c) the ideal extrusion pressure, (d) the real extrusion pressure including friction, and (e) the ram force.

**Given:**
$D_0 = 80$ mm $= 0.080$ m, $L = 0.250$ m, side $a_f = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m, $\sigma_f = 35$ MPa $= 35 \times 10^6$ Pa, $\mu = 0.08$.

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Areas and Extrusion Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \dfrac{\pi D_0^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2 \\
A_f &= a_f^2 = (0.030)^2 = 9.000 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 \\
R &= \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = \dfrac{5.027 \times 10^{-3}}{9.000 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.585
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (b) — True Strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \ln(R) = \ln(5.585) = 1.719$$

**Step (c) — Ideal Pressure:**

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R) = 35 \times 1.719 = 60.16\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step (d) — Real Pressure (with friction):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{\text{friction}} &= \dfrac{4 \mu L \sigma_f}{D_0} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.08 \times 0.250 \times 35}{0.080} \\
&= \dfrac{2.800}{0.080} = 35.00\ \text{MPa} \\
P_{\text{real}} &= P_{\text{ideal}} + \Delta P_{\text{friction}} = 60.16 + 35.00 = 95.16\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (e) — Ram Force:**

$$F_{\text{ram}} = P_{\text{real}} \cdot A_0 = 95.16 \times 10^6 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 478{,}370\ \text{N} \approx 478.4\ \text{kN}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote answers to **3 significant figures** and state units explicitly. Showing the **friction contribution as a separate $\Delta P$** term scores the full derivation marks, rather than substituting directly into a single combined formula.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Forward vs Backward vs Hydrostatic Extrusion

| Parameter | Direct (Forward) | Indirect (Backward) | Hydrostatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ram motion** | Same as product motion | Opposite to product motion | Ram pressurises fluid |
| **Billet–container friction** | Present (full length) | Eliminated (die moves) | Eliminated (no contact) |
| **Pressure** | $P = \sigma_f[\ln R + 4\mu L/D]$ | $P = \sigma_f \ln R$ | $P = \sigma_f \ln R$ |
| **Maximum attainable R** | $\sim 20$ – $40$ | $\sim 200$ – $400$ | $\sim 10^4$ (very high) |
| **Billet length limitation** | Yes (longer → higher P) | No (longer OK) | No |
| **Product length limitation** | No | Yes (limited by stem length) | No |
| **Tooling complexity** | Low | High (hollow stem) | High (high-pressure fluid system) |
| **Common application** | Architectural Al profiles | Tube extrusion, large R | Super-conductors, refractory metals |
