# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Distinguish clearly between Direct (forward) extrusion and Indirect (backward) extrusion. List two advantages of indirect extrusion over direct extrusion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct (Forward) | Indirect (Backward) |
|---|---|---|
| Ram–product motion | Same direction | Opposite direction |
| Friction on billet wall | Present over full length | Absent |
| Maximum extrusion ratio | Low to moderate | High |

**Two advantages of indirect extrusion:**
1. *Frictionless deformation* — eliminates billet–container friction, reducing ram pressure by 30–50%.
2. *Higher attainable extrusion ratios* (R up to ~400 vs ~40), enabling production of thinner sections.
3. (Bonus) *Uniform metal flow* — produces more dimensionally consistent products.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *die* motion with the *product* motion. In backward extrusion, the **die moves into the billet**, and the product emerges from the hollow stem — the die itself is the moving element. Markers deduct 1 mark for this confusion.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **Define (i) extrusion ratio, (ii) extrusion constant, and (iii) true strain in extrusion. Why does the extrusion pressure increase logarithmically with the extrusion ratio?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Extrusion ratio $R$**: $R = A_0 / A_f$, the ratio of the initial billet cross-section to the final product cross-section. It is dimensionless and always $\geq 1$.
2. **Extrusion constant $K$**: $K = P / \sigma_f$, the dimensionless ratio of actual ram pressure to the material's flow stress. It measures the *severity* of the process.
3. **True strain $\varepsilon$**: $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0 / A_f) = \ln(R)$, obtained from the constancy of volume in plastic deformation.

**Why pressure increases logarithmically with R:**
From the ideal-extrusion equation $P = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R)$, the pressure is *directly proportional* to $\ln(R)$, not to $R$ itself. As $R$ grows, the natural logarithm grows more slowly than $R$ but never reaches zero — meaning the pressure requirement is intrinsically tied to the *logarithmic* work of homogeneous deformation, which itself arises from the constancy of plastic volume.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern):** *Answer ANY ONE of Question A or Question B. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

### 🔹 Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, adapted]

> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **principle of hydrostatic extrusion**. Derive the expression for the ram pressure and explain why this process is preferred for *brittle and refractory metals*. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A copper billet of diameter **60 mm** is extruded at room temperature into a rod of diameter **15 mm**. The flow stress of copper at this condition is **400 MPa**, and the friction coefficient is **0.05**. Determine: (i) the ideal extrusion pressure, (ii) the real extrusion pressure for direct extrusion with billet length **300 mm**, and (iii) the ram force. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**Schematic (2 marks):** A labelled diagram showing the high-pressure chamber filled with incompressible fluid (castor oil or glycerine), the billet inside, the die at the exit, and the high-pressure pump connected to a ram intensifier.

**Principle (2 marks):** In hydrostatic extrusion, the billet is *not* in contact with the chamber walls. Instead, a high-pressure fluid surrounds the billet and transmits pressure uniformly (Pascal's law) to its surface. The pressure acts as a pure hydrostatic compression, eliminating both *billet–container friction* and *chill zones*. The fluid pressure pushes the billet through the die.

**Derivation (2 marks):** Applying the slab method, the work done by the fluid pressure equals the plastic work of deformation:

$$P \cdot A_0 \cdot dx = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R) \cdot A_0 \cdot dx \implies P = \sigma_f \ln(R)$$

The *same* ideal formula as frictionless direct extrusion is obtained — but with *no friction term* in practice.

**Why preferred for brittle materials (1 mark):** The triaxial compressive stress state (all three principal stresses are negative) suppresses crack nucleation and propagation (Griffith criterion: tensile stresses are required for fracture). This allows tungsten, molybdenum, beryllium, and other normally-brittle metals to be extruded successfully.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
$D_0 = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m; $D_f = 15$ mm $= 0.015$ m; $\sigma_f = 400$ MPa $= 400 \times 10^6$ Pa; $\mu = 0.05$; $L = 0.300$ m.

**Compute areas (1 mark):**

$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi (0.060)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$
$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi (0.015)^2}{4} = 1.767 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$$
$$R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = 16.00$$

**(i) Ideal pressure (2 marks):**

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \ln(R) = 400 \times 10^6 \times \ln(16) = 400 \times 10^6 \times 2.773 = 1109\ \text{MPa}$$

**(ii) Real pressure (direct extrusion, with friction) (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{real}} &= \sigma_f \!\left[\ln(R) + \dfrac{4 \mu L}{D_0}\right] \\
&= 400 \times 10^6 \times \left[2.773 + \dfrac{4 \times 0.05 \times 0.300}{0.060}\right] \\
&= 400 \times 10^6 \times [2.773 + 1.000] \\
&= 400 \times 10^6 \times 3.773 \\
&= 1509\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Ram force (1 mark):**

$$F = P_{\text{real}} \cdot A_0 = 1509 \times 10^6 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} = 4.27 \times 10^6\ \text{N} = 4266\ \text{kN}$$

---

### 🔹 Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, adapted]

> **(a)** Explain the **principle of impact extrusion** with a suitable diagram. Compare it with conventional direct extrusion and list **three industrial products** made by impact extrusion. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An aluminium billet of diameter **75 mm** is hot-extruded at **450 °C** through a die to produce a strip of cross-section **40 mm × 6 mm**. The flow stress is **25 MPa**, and the billet length is **200 mm**. Assuming direct extrusion with friction coefficient **0.1**, calculate: (i) the extrusion ratio, (ii) the true strain, (iii) the ideal pressure, and (iv) the real pressure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

**Principle (3 marks):**
Impact extrusion is a *high-speed, single-stroke* extrusion process in which a punch strikes a blank (slug) of metal placed in a die cavity at very high velocity (typically 1–2 m/s). The impact generates a brief but intense pressure pulse (≈ 2 × the conventional extrusion pressure), which forces the metal to flow *backwards* around the punch (similar to backward extrusion) and/or *forwards* through an orifice, conforming to the die shape. The entire forming operation occurs in a fraction of a second, which is why the process is suited to high-volume mass production of small, simple shapes.

**Diagram (1 mark):** A labelled schematic showing the punch, slug, die cavity, extruded wall, and finished product.

**Comparison with direct extrusion (2 marks):**

| Feature | Impact Extrusion | Direct Extrusion |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Very high (1 stroke) | Slow, continuous |
| Pressure pulse | Brief, intense | Sustained |
| Typical material | Soft (Al, Pb, Sn) | Wide range |
| Production rate | Very high (mass production) | Moderate |
| Billet shape | Slug (small) | Long cylindrical billet |

**Three industrial products (1 mark):**
1. Aluminium beverage cans (two-piece cans — body and bottom).
2. Toothpaste tubes.
3. Battery terminal connectors / lead-acid battery grids.
4. (Bonus) Fire extinguisher bodies, collapsible tubes for cosmetics.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $D_0 = 75$ mm $= 0.075$ m; strip $40 \times 6$ mm; $\sigma_f = 25$ MPa; $L = 0.200$ m; $\mu = 0.1$.

**Areas and ratio (2 marks):**

$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$
$$A_f = 0.040 \times 0.006 = 2.400 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$$
$$R = \dfrac{4.418 \times 10^{-3}}{2.400 \times 10^{-4}} = 18.41$$

**(i) Extrusion ratio: $R = 18.41$ (1 mark for the calculation)**

**(ii) True strain (1 mark):**

$$\varepsilon = \ln(18.41) = 2.913$$

**(iii) Ideal pressure (1 mark):**

$$P_{\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R) = 25 \times 10^6 \times 2.913 = 72.83\ \text{MPa}$$

**(iv) Real pressure (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{real}} &= \sigma_f \!\left[\ln(R) + \dfrac{4 \mu L}{D_0}\right] \\
&= 25 \times 10^6 \times \left[2.913 + \dfrac{4 \times 0.1 \times 0.200}{0.075}\right] \\
&= 25 \times 10^6 \times [2.913 + 1.067] \\
&= 25 \times 10^6 \times 3.980 \\
&= 99.49\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Module 1, Principles & Types:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — Pressure must be in Pa (or MPa), force in N (or kN). One mark is routinely deducted for missing units.
> 2. **Using the *wrong area* for the final section** — A *rectangular strip* (40 × 6 mm) has area $a \times b$, not $\pi d^2/4$. Many students incorrectly compute $A_f$ using a circle formula, giving a totally wrong R and a 7-mark cascade.
> 3. **Confusing $\ln(R)$ with $R$** — Pressure is *linear* in $\ln(R)$, not in $R$ itself. Showing $P = 25 \times 18.41 = 460$ MPa is a fundamental error; the correct value is ~73 MPa.
> 4. **Omitting the friction term** in direct extrusion — gives an "ideal" answer, not a "real" answer, and the question explicitly says "real pressure". Always add the $4\mu L/D$ term.
> 5. **Mixing up hydrostatic and indirect extrusion** — both eliminate friction, but for *different reasons*. Hydrostatic uses a *fluid medium*; indirect uses a *moving die*. Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **The 1-line definition:** *Extrusion = compressive bulk forming + billet pushed through a die to get a long product of uniform cross-section.*

> ✅ **The 3 master formulas** (memorise in this order):
> 1. $R = A_0 / A_f$
> 2. $P_{\text{ideal}} = \sigma_f \cdot \ln(R)$
> 3. $P_{\text{real, forward}} = \sigma_f[\ln(R) + 4\mu L / D]$

> ✅ **The 4 process stages:** Loading → Upsetting → Break-through (peak P) → Steady-state flow → Discard.

> ✅ **The 5 main types of extrusion (exam-favourite classification):**
> 1. **Direct (forward)** — ram pushes billet through stationary die; friction present.
> 2. **Indirect (backward)** — die moves into billet; no billet–container friction.
> 3. **Hydrostatic** — pressurised fluid drives the billet; no friction, no chill zones.
> 4. **Impact** — high-speed single-stroke punch; used for soft-metal mass production.
> 5. **Continuous** — billet is replenished continuously (e.g., Conform process).

> ✅ **The 3 temperature regimes:**
> - **Hot** ($T > 0.6 T_m$) — for steels, Ti, Ni superalloys.
> - **Warm** ($0.3 T_m < T < 0.6 T_m$) — for Mg, Al alloys (intermediate).
> - **Cold** ($T < 0.3 T_m$) — for Pb, Sn, soft Al, requiring high force but best surface finish.

> ✅ **Why pressure rises with R logarithmically, not linearly** — because the natural strain is $\ln(R)$, derived from volume constancy, and pressure is proportional to true strain.

> ✅ **Why indirect and hydrostatic can achieve R ~ 400** — because they *eliminate* the $4\mu L/D$ friction term that grows with billet length.

> ✅ **Why extrusion handles brittle metals** — because the triaxial *compressive* stress state prevents crack propagation (Griffith criterion requires tensile stress for fracture).

> ✅ **Industrial examples to quote in answers:**
> - Direct hot: Al window frames (AA6063).
> - Indirect: Cu tubes, Al collapsible tubes.
> - Hydrostatic: W, Mo rods; superconducting Nb-Ti billets.
> - Impact: Two-piece Al beverage cans, toothpaste tubes.

> ✅ **Key die terminology:** approach angle, bearing land, relief angle — three terms examiners test in process diagrams.

> ✅ **Exam mantra:** *Always quote R, $\ln(R)$, and the units of P (Pa or MPa) in the first line of any numerical answer.* This single habit is worth 1–2 marks in almost every KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uses of Extruders in the Food Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Uses of Extruders in the Food Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** and the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework, the term **"Food Extrusion"** is defined as a continuous thermo-mechanical process in which food material is forced to flow — under controlled pressure, shear, and temperature — through a designed **die** of specific cross-sectional geometry, thereby producing a wide spectrum of products with tailored textural, nutritional, and functional properties.

A **Food Extruder** is therefore a high-shear, high-pressure, continuous-flow reactor (or biopolymer processor) that simultaneously performs **mixing, kneading, shearing, cooking, shaping, and texturizing** operations inside a single modular barrel assembly, replacing multiple conventional unit operations (boiling, rolling, baking, drying, frying) by a single integrated process line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Extrusion is a thermophysical and thermochemical unit operation in which food biopolymers (starch, protein, fibre) are converted into viscous dough/melt, shaped, and structurally transformed through a die to yield a vast range of value-added products — from breakfast cereals and snacks to textured vegetable protein and pet food.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

To a first-time learner, an industrial food extruder can be compared to a **"Pressure Cooker + Meat Grinder + Pasta Maker combined into one machine"**:

- Imagine a **sausage maker** (mechanical forcing of dough through a small hole).
- Now add **heat and steam injection** (the pressure cooker part).
- Now add a **rotating screw that shears, mixes and cooks** the dough as it travels.
- At the end, the dough comes out in any shape you can imagine — pellets, rings, stars, flakes, puffs, noodles, sheets.

The **screw** is the "engine" that pushes the material forward against resistance. The **barrel** is the "cooking chamber." The **die** is the "shape-defining throat." The **knife** at the die face is the "precision cutter."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why extruders dominate the food industry:**
> 1. They are **continuous** (not batch) → high productivity.
> 2. They are **multi-functional** → 6+ unit operations in one machine.
> 3. They are **energy-efficient** due to high specific throughput.
> 4. They produce **uniform product shape and density** at industrial scale.
> 5. They enable **novel food textures** (puffing, expansion, layered, fibrous) impossible by any other unit operation.

---

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Barrel Temperature** | T_b | **80 °C – 200 °C** |
| **Screw Speed** | N | **100 – 600 rpm** |
| **Screw L/D Ratio** | L/D | **15:1 – 30:1** |
| **Throughput Capacity** | Q | **100 – 10,000 kg/h** |
| **Die Pressure** | P_d | **1 – 10 MPa** |
| **Specific Mechanical Energy** | SME | **50 – 500 Wh/kg** |
| **Moisture at Feed** | M_f | **10 – 30 % (w.b.)** |
| **Product Expansion Ratio** | ER | **2 – 15** |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 ESE answers, always quote **SI units** (Pa, kg/s, J/kg, K). Examiners award additional marks when units are correctly specified.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Framework of Food Extrusion

Food extrusion can be best understood as the **controlled degradation and re-organisation of biopolymer molecules** (starch granules and protein networks) inside a high-shear, high-temperature environment. From a polymer processing standpoint, the food behaves as a **non-Newtonian, shear-thinning viscoelastic melt** whose viscosity $\eta(\dot\gamma)$ is temperature and moisture dependent.

### 2.1.1 The Five Functional Zones of an Extruder (KTU High-Yield Concept)

The barrel of a single-screw food extruder is conceptually divided into **five functional zones** (most frequently asked in KTU exams):

1. **Feed Zone (Conveying Zone):** Material enters at ambient conditions; pure solids conveying takes place. No melting or cooking yet.
2. **Compression / Kneading Zone:** Mechanical energy input increases dramatically. The screw flight depth decreases (compression ratio = 1:2 to 1:5). Mixing, shear, and incipient cooking begin.
3. **Cooking / Melting Zone (Metering Zone):** Biopolymers gelatinize and melt. Temperature rises to 120–180 °C. Shear energy converts mechanical work into internal (viscous) heat.
4. **Metering / Pumping Zone:** Acts as a positive-displacement pump. Pressure builds up to 1–10 MPa. Flow becomes fully developed and predictable.
5. **Die Zone (Shaping):** Sudden pressure drop to atmospheric → instantaneous moisture flash-off → product expansion and structural setting (glass transition, $T_g$).

### 2.1.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Stage — A KTU Conceptual Map

| Stage | Why It Exists | What Happens Physically |
|---|---|---|
| Feed | To meter input | Densification, moisture redistribution |
| Kneading | To mix & cook | Viscous dissipation; starch gelatinization |
| Cooking | To gelatinize starch | Order–disorder transition; protein denaturation |
| Pumping | To pressurize melt | Pressure build-up; throughput control |
| Die | To shape & expand | Viscoelastic expansion; moisture flash-off |

---

## 2.2 Classification of Food Extruder Uses — The KTU High-Yield Master Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following classification forms the **core Module 1 syllabus content** for KTU 2024 PEFTT637. Memorise the product categories and the corresponding process parameters.

| S.No. | Application Category | Typical Products | Process Type | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ready-to-Eat Breakfast Cereals** | Corn flakes, puffed rice, wheat balls | High-shear cooking extrusion | T = 150–180 °C; SME = 100–200 Wh/kg |
| 2 | **Snack Foods** | Puffs, balls, curls, third-generation snacks | Direct expansion extrusion | Die pressure 4–8 MPa; expansion ratio 6–12 |
| 3 | **Pasta and Noodles** | Macaroni, spaghetti, instant noodles | Cold / low-temperature extrusion | T = 40–60 °C; protein-rich dough |
| 4 | **Textured Vegetable Protein (TVP)** | Meat analogues, soy chunks, nuggets | High-moisture extrusion (HME) | M = 40–60 %; T = 140–160 °C; cooling die |
| 5 | **Pet Food & Aqua Feed** | Kibble, fish pellets, sinking feed | Dry extrusion / preconditioning | Bulk density 350–500 g/L |
| 6 | **Confectionery & Sweet Masses** | Liquorice, toffees, chewing gum | Cold forming extrusion | T = 50–90 °C |
| 7 | **Pre-cooked Flours & Bakery** | Tortilla, pre-gelatinized flour, flat bread | Steam-jet cooking extrusion | Starch pre-gelatinization |
| 8 | **Modified Starch Production** | Pregelatinized, dextrinized, cold-water-soluble starches | Thermomechanical conversion | DE control via SME |
| 9 | **Croutons, Crackers & Coatings** | Bread croutons, batter coatings | Low-moisture, high-shear extrusion | Crunchy matrix |
| 10 | **Brewing Adjuncts & Beverages** | Malt substitutes, cereal-based coffee | Cooking extrusion | Dextrinization for fermentable sugars |
| 11 | **Baby Foods & Weaning Foods** | Instant cereal mixes, pre-cooked weaning powders | Hygienic twin-screw extrusion | Nutrient retention; low SME |
| 12 | **Functional Foods** | High-fibre, protein-enriched, probiotic carriers | Gentle cooking extrusion | Bioactive retention |

---

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **directly testable in Part B (14-mark) questions** of KTU ESE 2024. Every engineering derivation in food extrusion traces back to one of these roots.

| # | Formula | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mass Throughput** | $Q = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | kg/s | Mass of material discharged per second |
| 2 | **Volumetric Throughput** | $Q_v = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(D_s^2 - D_c^2)\,N\,\tan\phi$ | m³/s | Drag-flow in feed zone (Newtonian approximation) |
| 3 | **Specific Mechanical Energy** | $SME = \dfrac{\omega \cdot T}{\dot m}$ | J/kg (or Wh/kg) | Mechanical energy input per kg of product |
| 4 | **Shaft Power** | $P_{shaft} = 2\pi N \cdot T$ | W | Power required to drive the screw |
| 5 | **Viscous Heat Generation** | $\dot Q_{visc} = \tau \cdot \dot\gamma \cdot V$ | W | Rate of viscous dissipation |
| 6 | **Screw Power (Newtonian)** | $P_{screw} = \pi^3\,D_s^3\,N^2\,L\,\eta\,\sin^2\phi$ | W | Standard screw power (Bird et al.) |
| 7 | **Die Pressure Drop (Capillary)** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2L\,\tau_w}{R}$ | Pa | Hagen–Poiseuille for power-law fluid |
| 8 | **Apparent Shear Rate** | $\dot\gamma_a = \dfrac{32\,Q}{\pi D^3}$ | s⁻¹ | Wall shear rate in die |
| 9 | **Apparent Viscosity** | $\eta_a = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\dot\gamma_a}$ | Pa·s | Power-law consistency check |
| 10 | **Residence Time (Mean)** | $\bar t = \dfrac{V_{barrel}}{Q_v}$ | s | Average time material spends in barrel |
| 11 | **Expansion Ratio** | $ER = \dfrac{\rho_{feed}}{\rho_{product}}$ | — | Volumetric puffing of extrudate |
| 12 | **Moisture Flash (Isothermal)** | $m_{steam} = m_{liquid}\left(1 - \dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_{die}}\right)$ | kg/kg | Instantaneous water vaporization |
| 13 | **Moisture Mass Balance** | $\dot m_f\,w_f = \dot m_p\,w_p + \dot m_{steam}$ | kg/s | Water in feed = water in product + flashed steam |
| 14 | **Energy Mass Balance** | $\dot m_f\,h_f + \dot Q_{visc} = \dot m_p\,h_p + \dot m_{steam}\,h_{fg}$ | W | Steady-state energy conservation |
| 15 | **Power-Law Fluid** | $\tau = K\,\dot\gamma^{n}$ | Pa | Ostwald-de Waele rheology |
| 16 | **Capillary Die Viscosity** | $\eta_{app} = \dfrac{R\,\Delta P}{2L\,\dot\gamma_a}$ | Pa·s | Bagley-corrected apparent viscosity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip (High-Yield):** In 14-mark derivation questions, the most commonly asked formulas are **SME (Eq. 3), Hagen–Poiseuille die flow (Eq. 7), and the moisture flash (Eq. 12)**. Memorise these three cold.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above equations are not academic abstractions — they are **used daily in process industries** such as:

- **Frito-Lay, ITC Foods, Haldiram's, Bikaji** → snack-food extrusion lines in India
- **Nestlé, Mondelez, General Mills, Kellogg's** → global breakfast cereal manufacturing
- **Mars, Purina, Royal Canin** → pet food twin-screw extrusion
- **Cargill, ADM, Bühler** → pre-cooked flours and brewing adjuncts
- **Beyond Meat, Impossible Foods, Vezlay** → textured vegetable protein (meat analogues)

> The **specific mechanical energy (SME)** input is the single most important design variable — it determines **starch conversion, protein denaturation, expansion, texture, and color** of the final product. KTU examiners emphasise this in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) — The Most-Asked KTU Derivation

> **Problem:** A single-screw food extruder is running at a screw speed of **N = 300 rpm**. The drive motor delivers a torque of **T = 80 N·m**. The mass throughput is **ṁ = 200 kg/h**. Compute the **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** in **Wh/kg** and in **J/kg**.

### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation

The mechanical energy input to the material is the work done by the screw shaft per unit mass. For a rotating shaft:

$$P_{shaft} = 2\pi\,N\,T$$

where $N$ is in revolutions per second (Hz) and $T$ is torque in N·m, giving $P_{shaft}$ in watts (J/s).

The **Specific Mechanical Energy** is the shaft power divided by the mass flow rate:

$$SME = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\dot m} = \dfrac{2\pi\,N\,T}{\dot m}$$

### Step 2 — Convert units to SI

Convert screw speed to Hz:

$$N = 300\;\text{rpm} = \dfrac{300}{60}\;\text{Hz} = 5\;\text{rev/s}$$

Convert mass flow rate to SI:

$$\dot m = 200\;\text{kg/h} = \dfrac{200}{3600}\;\text{kg/s} = 0.0556\;\text{kg/s}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the shaft power

$$P_{shaft} = 2\pi\,(5)\,(80) = 800\pi\;\text{W} = 2513.27\;\text{W}$$

### Step 4 — Compute SME in J/kg

$$SME = \dfrac{2513.27\;\text{W}}{0.0556\;\text{kg/s}} = 45,200\;\text{J/kg}$$

### Step 5 — Convert SME to Wh/kg

$$SME = \dfrac{45,200\;\text{J/kg}}{3600\;\text{s/h}} = 12.56\;\text{Wh/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $SME \approx 12.6\;\text{Wh/kg}$ — this is a **low-SME** extrusion, suitable for **cold or mild processing** such as pasta, cold-formed confectionery, or pre-gelatinized starch at very low temperatures. KTU answers must state the conclusion explicitly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Throughput in the Feed Zone (Drag Flow)

> **Problem:** A single-screw extruder has a screw diameter $D_s = 60\;\text{mm}$, core (root) diameter $D_c = 36\;\text{mm}$, helix angle $\phi = 20°$, and screw speed $N = 200\;\text{rpm}$. Compute the **volumetric drag-flow throughput** in the feed zone, assuming Newtonian solids conveying.

### Step 1 — Governing equation (Newtonian drag flow)

For a single-screw extruder conveying a Newtonian material as if it were a viscous fluid adhering to the screw:

$$Q_{drag} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(D_s^2 - D_c^2)\,N\,\tan\phi$$

This expression arises from the **relative axial motion** of the screw flight surface dragging the material in the barrel. The factor $\tan\phi$ projects the rotational velocity into axial motion.

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (in SI units)

$$D_s^2 = (0.060)^2 = 3.6\times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^2$$
$$D_c^2 = (0.036)^2 = 1.296\times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^2$$
$$D_s^2 - D_c^2 = 2.304\times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^2$$

Screw speed:

$$N = 200\;\text{rpm} = \dfrac{200}{60} = 3.333\;\text{rev/s}$$

Helix projection:

$$\tan 20° = 0.3640$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the drag flow

$$Q_{drag} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(2.304\times 10^{-3})\,(3.333)\,(0.3640)$$

$$Q_{drag} = (0.7854)\,(2.304\times 10^{-3})\,(3.333)\,(0.3640)$$

$$Q_{drag} = 2.198\times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to practical units

$$Q_{drag} = 2.198\times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 2.198\;\text{L/s} = 131.9\;\text{L/min} = 7.91\;\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** This is the **upper bound** of the throughput (drag flow only). The **net (actual) throughput** is lower because the back-flow $Q_{pressure}$ subtracts from it: $Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure}$. KTU examiners appreciate the recognition of the "drag-flow / pressure-flow" split.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Die Pressure Drop (Hagen–Poiseuille) for Power-Law Foods

> **Problem:** Molten starch melt (a power-law fluid with $K = 5{,}000\;\text{Pa·s}^n$ and $n = 0.45$) flows through a circular die of radius $R = 4\;\text{mm}$ and length $L = 40\;\text{mm}$ at a volumetric flow rate $Q_v = 1.5\times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Calculate the die pressure drop.

### Step 1 — Apparent shear rate (Rabinowitsch flow)

$$\dot\gamma_a = \dfrac{32\,Q_v}{\pi\,D^3} = \dfrac{32\,Q_v}{\pi\,(2R)^3}$$

$$\dot\gamma_a = \dfrac{32 \times 1.5\times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times (0.008)^3} = \dfrac{4.8\times 10^{-5}}{1.608\times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\dot\gamma_a = 2.985\times 10^{4}\;\text{s}^{-1}$$

### Step 2 — Wall shear stress for a power-law fluid

$$\tau_w = K\,(\dot\gamma_a)^{n} = 5000 \times (2.985\times 10^{4})^{0.45}$$

Compute the exponent:

$$(2.985\times 10^{4})^{0.45} = e^{0.45\,\ln(2.985\times 10^4)} = e^{0.45 \times 10.305} = e^{4.637} = 103.3$$

Thus:

$$\tau_w = 5000 \times 103.3 = 5.165\times 10^{5}\;\text{Pa}$$

### Step 3 — Pressure drop across the die (Hagen–Poiseuille for power-law)

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{2\,L\,\tau_w}{R} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.040 \times 5.165\times 10^{5}}{0.004}$$

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{41{,}320}{0.004} = 1.033\times 10^{7}\;\text{Pa} \approx 10.33\;\text{MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $\Delta P \approx 10.3\;\text{MPa}$. This is the typical die pressure for **direct-expansion snack extrusion**, perfectly aligned with the syllabus range of **1–10 MPa** stated in Section 1.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Moisture Flash During Die Discharge (Energy Basis)

> **Problem:** A corn-based snack melt exits the die at 8 MPa pressure and 160 °C, with moisture content of 22 % (wet basis). The ambient pressure is 0.1 MPa. Compute the **fraction of moisture that flashes to steam** at the die exit.

### Step 1 — Effective steam partial pressure inside the die

The water in the superheated melt exerts its saturation vapour pressure. For a first-order isothermal flash estimate:

$$\dot m_{steam} = \dot m_{water}\left(1 - \dfrac{P_{atm}}{P_{die}}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Compute flash fraction

$$\dfrac{\dot m_{steam}}{\dot m_{water}} = 1 - \dfrac{0.1}{8.0} = 1 - 0.0125 = 0.9875$$

### Step 3 — Convert feed moisture to absolute (dry basis)

If feed has 22 % moisture (w.b.) and 1 kg of feed contains:

$$m_{water} = 0.22\;\text{kg},\quad m_{dry} = 0.78\;\text{kg}$$

Flashed steam per kg of feed:

$$m_{steam} = 0.22 \times 0.9875 = 0.217\;\text{kg per kg feed}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** About **21.7 % of the total mass** of the snack extrudate is **instantaneously vaporised water** — this is precisely the steam that causes the **puffing, expansion, and cellular (porous) structure** of the snack. This single mechanism is the foundation of **direct-expansion extrusion technology** for the food industry.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question, Module 1]**
**"Define food extrusion. List any four major uses of extruders in the food industry."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Food extrusion is a **continuous thermomechanical process** in which moistened food material is forced to flow through a die under controlled temperature, pressure and shear conditions to obtain a product of desired shape, texture and functional properties.
>
> **Four Major Uses (2 Marks):**
> 1. Production of **ready-to-eat breakfast cereals** (corn flakes, puffed rice, wheat balls).
> 2. Production of **expanded snack foods** (puffs, curls, balls, third-generation snacks).
> 3. Production of **pasta, noodles, and flat breads** (tortillas, pre-cooked flour).
> 4. Production of **textured vegetable protein (TVP)** and meat analogues using high-moisture extrusion.

*(Award 3 full marks if all four are valid; definition must include the word "continuous" and "thermomechanical".)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question, Module 1]**
**"Differentiate between cold extrusion and cooking extrusion in the food industry."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cold Extrusion | Cooking Extrusion |
|---|---|---|
| Barrel Temperature | Below gelatinization (< 70 °C) | Above gelatinization (120–200 °C) |
| Product State | Dense, ungelatinized | Porous, expanded, cooked |
| Typical Products | Pasta, cold-formed confectionery, raw dough shapes | Snacks, breakfast cereals, TVP |
| Moisture Content | High (30–50 % w.b.) | Moderate (10–30 % w.b.) |
| SME Input | Low (< 50 Wh/kg) | High (100–500 Wh/kg) |

*(3 marks for 3 valid differentiating points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation (ESE):** Part B questions must be answered choosing **either OR (a)/(b)** of the same module. Below are two fully independent alternatives to mirror KTU's standard pattern.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — KTU ESE Model Paper, Module 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question, Module 1]**

> **(a)** "Discuss in detail the major uses of extruders in the food industry. Classify them with suitable examples." **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** "A single-screw food extruder is running at a screw speed of 360 rpm with a motor torque of 120 N·m. The throughput is 300 kg/h of corn-based snack feed. Calculate: (i) the shaft power in kW; (ii) the specific mechanical energy (SME) in J/kg and in Wh/kg; and (iii) comment on the suitability of this SME value for direct-expansion snack production." **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Introduction (1 Mark):** Food extrusion is a versatile, continuous thermomechanical process that has revolutionized modern food processing. It integrates mixing, kneading, cooking, shaping and texturizing into one machine, replacing multiple conventional unit operations.

**Classification of Uses (5 Marks):**

The uses of extruders in the food industry can be broadly classified as follows:

1. **Cereals and Snack Products (1.5 Marks):**
   - **Ready-to-Eat Breakfast Cereals:** Corn flakes, puffed wheat, multigrain flakes, bran flakes.
   - **Direct-Expanded Snacks:** Puffs, curls, balls, tubes, rings (e.g., extruded corn curls, "Kurkure" in India, "Cheese Puffs" globally).
   - **Process:** High-temperature (150–180 °C), low-moisture (12–15 %), high-shear cooking extrusion. The hot melt, upon exiting the die, undergoes **flash evaporation** of moisture, leading to puffy, porous structure.

2. **Pasta, Noodles, and Flat Breads (1.5 Marks):**
   - **Pasta Products:** Macaroni, spaghetti, fusilli — produced by **cold extrusion** of durum wheat semolina.
   - **Instant Noodles:** Pre-gelatinized wheat dough extruded and dried for rapid rehydration.
   - **Tortillas & Flat Breads:** Pre-cooked maize/wheat flour extruded into sheets.

3. **Textured Vegetable Protein (TVP) and Meat Analogues (1 Mark):**
   - **Soy chunks, nuggets, and granules** made by **high-moisture extrusion (HME)** of defatted soy flour.
   - The cooling die imparts **fibrous, meat-like texture**.
   - Modern applications: Plant-based burgers (Beyond Meat, Vezlay).

4. **Pet Food and Aqua Feed (0.5 Marks):**
   - Dry extruded **kibble, pellets, and floating fish feed**.
   - Twin-screw extruders dominate this segment due to better mixing of protein, fat, and vitamins.

5. **Confectionery, Pre-cooked Flours, and Functional Foods (0.5 Marks):**
   - **Cold-formed confectionery** (liquorice, toffees) and **pre-gelatinized starches** for instant soups and baby foods.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The food extrusion industry has evolved from a single-purpose pasta maker into a versatile platform serving cereals, snacks, protein products, feeds, and functional ingredients — a fact that makes extruders indispensable in modern food manufacturing.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**

- Screw speed, $N = 360\;\text{rpm} = 6\;\text{Hz}$
- Torque, $T = 120\;\text{N·m}$
- Throughput, $\dot m = 300\;\text{kg/h} = 0.0833\;\text{kg/s}$

**(i) Shaft Power in kW (3 Marks):**

$$P_{shaft} = 2\pi N T = 2\pi \times 6 \times 120 = 1440\pi\;\text{W}$$

$$P_{shaft} = 4523.9\;\text{W} = 4.524\;\text{kW}$$

**(ii) SME in J/kg and Wh/kg (3 Marks):**

$$SME = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\dot m} = \dfrac{4523.9\;\text{W}}{0.0833\;\text{kg/s}} = 54{,}310\;\text{J/kg}$$

Converting to Wh/kg:

$$SME = \dfrac{54{,}310}{3600} = 15.09\;\text{Wh/kg}$$

**(iii) Comment on Suitability (1 Mark):**

A SME of ~15 Wh/kg is **low-to-moderate** in food extrusion terms. For **direct-expansion snack production**, the typical required SME range is **100–200 Wh/kg**. Therefore, the given operating conditions are **insufficient for high-expansion snack production**, and are more suitable for **cold extrusion** or **low-expansion pre-cooked flour** products. To increase SME, the operator should **increase screw speed, increase screw compression ratio, or add preconditioning steam injection**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — KTU ESE Model Paper, Module 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question, Module 1]**

> **(a)** "Explain the working principle of a single-screw food extruder with a neat functional block diagram. Highlight the role of specific mechanical energy (SME) in determining product quality." **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** "A twin-screw food extruder processes snack dough at a feed rate of 150 kg/h with a moisture content of 18 % (wet basis). The die exit pressure is 6 MPa and the product exits at 150 °C. If the throughput must be increased to 250 kg/h, calculate the new die pressure assuming the screw speed is increased by 50 % and torque by 30 %." **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Working Principle (4 Marks):**

A single-screw food extruder consists of a **drive motor**, a **screw** rotating inside a **heated barrel**, a **die** at the discharge end, and a **cutter** at the die face. The barrel is heated externally (and sometimes by steam jackets) and is divided into functional zones:

1. **Feed Zone:** The granular food material (cereal flour, starch, protein meal) is fed via a volumetric feeder and conveyed forward by the relative motion of the rotating screw flight.
2. **Kneading/Compression Zone:** The flight depth decreases; the material is compressed, sheared, and mixed. Viscous dissipation begins to heat the melt.
3. **Cooking Zone:** The temperature exceeds 100 °C; starch granules **gelatinize**, proteins **denature**, and water acts as a plasticizer, transforming the mass into a **viscoelastic dough/melt**.
4. **Metering Zone:** The fully developed melt is pressurized to 1–10 MPa.
5. **Die Zone:** The melt is forced through a precisely shaped die. Upon exiting, the sudden pressure drop causes **flash evaporation** of superheated moisture, leading to **product expansion (puffing)**, **solidification**, and formation of the final **porous, crunchy structure**. A rotating knife cuts the extrudate to desired lengths.

**Role of SME (3 Marks):**

The **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** is defined as the mechanical energy input from the screw shaft per kilogram of product:

$$SME = \dfrac{\omega\,T}{\dot m}$$

SME is the **single most important process variable** in food extrusion because it directly controls:

- **Starch conversion** (gelatinization + dextrinization): higher SME → more breakdown of starch molecules.
- **Protein denaturation and texturization**: critical for TVP and meat analogues.
- **Expansion ratio** of the final product.
- **Bulk density**: high SME → low bulk density (very puffed).
- **Color and flavor**: Maillard browning, caramelization, and toasting are SME-dependent.
- **Nutrient retention**: vitamins and probiotics are SME-sensitive.

A typical classification:

| SME Range (Wh/kg) | Product Class |
|---|---|
| 50–100 | Pre-cooked flour, low-density products |
| 100–200 | Snack puffs, breakfast cereals |
| 200–500 | Highly expanded snacks, modified starch |

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given Data (Initial):**

- $\dot m_1 = 150\;\text{kg/h}$
- $w = 0.18$ (moisture w.b.)
- $P_{die,1} = 6\;\text{MPa}$
- $N_2 = 1.5\,N_1$ (screw speed increased by 50 %)
- $T_2 = 1.3\,T_1$ (torque increased by 30 %)
- $\dot m_2 = 250\;\text{kg/h}$

**Step 1 — Initial Shaft Power (2 Marks):**

$$P_1 = 2\pi N_1 T_1$$

**Step 2 — Final Shaft Power (2 Marks):**

$$P_2 = 2\pi (1.5 N_1)(1.3 T_1) = 1.95 \times 2\pi N_1 T_1 = 1.95 P_1$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hagen–Poiseuille for die pressure (1 Mark):**

For a power-law food melt, the die pressure drop is proportional to the **square** of the apparent shear rate (or approximately to $Q^2$). However, for simplicity in first-order design, die pressure is taken to scale with the volumetric flow rate:

$$P_{die,2} \approx P_{die,1} \times \left(\dfrac{\dot m_2}{\dot m_1}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute new die pressure (1.5 Marks):**

$$P_{die,2} = 6\;\text{MPa} \times \left(\dfrac{250}{150}\right)^2 = 6 \times (1.667)^2$$

$$P_{die,2} = 6 \times 2.778 = 16.67\;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Comment (0.5 Mark):**

A die pressure of **16.67 MPa** is significantly higher than the typical industrial range (1–10 MPa) and approaches the structural limit of standard food extrusion dies. The student should recommend **die geometry redesign (larger exit diameter) or multi-orifice dies** to maintain feasible operating pressures.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (most common):** Failing to convert **rpm → Hz** and **kg/h → kg/s** is the #1 reason for losing 2–3 marks in numerical problems. Always do it first.
> 2. **Missing the "Conclusion" Step:** Numerical answers without an engineering comment ("this SME is suitable/unsuitable for snack expansion") are penalized. Always end with a **practical interpretation**.
> 3. **Ignoring Mass Balance:** When asked to compute moisture flash, students often forget to convert moisture content from **wet basis to dry basis** correctly.
> 4. **Diagram Neglect:** In 7-mark theory questions, the absence of a **labelled block diagram or process flow** typically costs 1–2 marks even if the descriptive text is correct.
> 5. **Out-of-Range Answers:** If your computed SME, pressure, or temperature falls outside the syllabus-quoted range, state it explicitly and discuss the reason — examiners reward diagnostic thinking.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Food extrusion** is a **continuous thermomechanical** unit operation combining **mixing, kneading, cooking, shaping, and texturizing** in one machine.
- [x] Major extruder types: **Single-screw** (simpler, cheaper) and **Twin-screw** (co-rotating or counter-rotating; better for high-moisture, high-fat feeds).
- [x] Five functional zones: **Feed → Kneading → Cooking → Pumping → Die**.
- [x] **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** is the master variable controlling texture, expansion, and ingredient transformation.
- [x] Use **cold extrusion** for pasta, confectionery, and dense products (T < 70 °C, SME < 50 Wh/kg).
- [x] Use **hot cooking extrusion** for snacks, cereals, and TVP (T = 120–200 °C, SME = 100–500 Wh/kg).
- [x] **High-moisture extrusion (HME)** at moisture 40–60 % produces **fibrous meat analogues** (TVP, plant-based meat).
- [x] **Direct-expansion snacks** rely on **instantaneous moisture flash** at the die exit, governed by $P_{die}$ vs $P_{atm}$.
- [x] Key formula: $SME = \omega T / \dot m$ and $P_{shaft} = 2\pi N T$.
- [x] Hagen–Poiseuille for power-law foods: $\Delta P = 2L\,\tau_w / R$, $\tau_w = K\dot\gamma_a^n$.
- [x] Expansion ratio $ER = \rho_{feed} / \rho_{product}$ is the **headline quality metric** for snack extrusion.
- [x] Industrial applications span **cereals, snacks, pasta, TVP, pet food, confectionery, pre-cooked flours, modified starch, brewing adjuncts, baby foods, and functional foods**.
- [x] KTU 14-mark answers must always include a **structured diagram, a numerical example, and a concluding engineering statement**.
- [x] Always convert units to SI before computation; always state practical interpretations in the answer's final paragraph.

---

> **End of KTU 2024 Module 1 Notes — Uses of Extruders in the Food Industry**
> *Prepared as per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 (NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme)*

topics.title: Pre-conditioning of Raw Materials Used in Extrusion Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pre-conditioning of Raw Materials Used in Extrusion Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pre-conditioning** refers to the systematic sequence of physical, thermal, and chemical operations applied to feed materials *prior* to their introduction into the extruder barrel, with the objective of stabilizing feed characteristics, optimizing moisture content, ensuring uniform particle size distribution, and achieving homogeneous blending of multi-component formulations. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEFTT637 - Advanced Extrusion Technology), pre-conditioning is recognized as the **critical preparatory interface** between raw material storage and the high-shear thermo-mechanical conversion zone of the extruder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Framework:** Pre-conditioning encompasses **moisture adjustment, particle size reduction, pre-heating, ingredient blending, and binder/lubricant incorporation** — all aimed at maximizing extruder throughput, product quality consistency, and energy efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** Industry-standard feed moisture for most single-screw food/feed extruders is maintained in the range of **25–35 % (wet basis)**, while twin-screw cooking extruders typically operate at **10–22 %** depending on the formulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bakery & Pasta Logic"

Imagine you are a home baker preparing pasta dough. Before pushing the dough through the pasta machine (the extruder), you would:
1. **Weigh and sift** the flour (size reduction & uniformity)
2. **Hydrate gradually** while mixing (moisture conditioning & blending)
3. **Rest the dough** covered with a damp cloth (pre-heating/tempering)
4. **Add a tiny drop of oil** to ease flow (lubricant addition)

Pre-conditioning of extrusion raw materials is the **industrial, scientifically controlled equivalent** of this exact sequence. Just as the baker ensures the dough is at the right hydration, temperature, and consistency *before* it meets the die, the extrusion engineer ensures feed material is at the right **moisture %, temperature (°C), particle size (mm), and composition (%)** before it enters the extruder's compression zone.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** *"Pre-conditioning = The 4 T's"* — **T**emperature, **T**exture (size), **T**otal moisture, and **T**horoughness of mix.

## 1.3 Classification of Pre-conditioning Operations

Pre-conditioning operations can be broadly classified into the following functional categories relevant to the KTU PEFTT637 syllabus:

| S.No | Operation Type | Primary Function | Typical Equipment |
|:----:|:---------------|:-----------------|:------------------|
| 1 | **Moisture Conditioning** | Hydrate feed to target % | Steam conditioner, water bath |
| 2 | **Size Reduction** | Reduce Dp to desired range | Hammer mill, roller mill |
| 3 | **Pre-heating / Tempering** | Raise feed temperature | Steam jacketed conditioner |
| 4 | **Blending / Mixing** | Homogenize multi-component feed | Ribbon blender, paddle mixer |
| 5 | **Binder/Lubricant Addition** | Improve flow & die release | Inline dosing pump |
| 6 | **Agglomeration** | Improve bulk density | Disc pelletizer, agglomerator |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Students often confuse *Pre-conditioning* with *Pre-cooking*. Pre-conditioning **does not** gelatinize starch fully; it only prepares the material thermally and mechanically so that the extruder's short residence time (~20–60 s) can complete the cook. Pre-cooking is done **inside** the extruder barrel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" of Pre-conditioning — Engineering Justification

Raw materials arriving at the extruder feed hopper are typically **heterogeneous, cold, dry, and non-uniform**. Direct feeding without pre-conditioning leads to:

- **Surging & pulsating flow** at the die exit (poor product dimensional stability)
- **Increased specific mechanical energy (SME)** consumption — wastes motor power
- **Incomplete starch gelatinization** — reduces product digestibility & expansion
- **Die blockages** from oversized particles
- **Product color/flavor defects** due to uneven moisture
- **Reduced throughput** as the extruder must absorb conditioning load

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Moisture Conditioning (Hydration)

Water (or steam) is added to raise moisture content to the target level. The relationship between wet-basis (w.b.) and dry-basis (d.b.) moisture content is critical for KTU numerical problems.

### Step 2 — Size Reduction (Comminution)

Particles are reduced to a target geometric mean diameter (dgw) using comminution laws. **Bond's Law** is the most exam-relevant.

### Step 3 — Pre-heating / Steam Conditioning

Live steam injection raises the feed temperature (typically to **80–95 °C**) and partially pre-gelatinizes starches — reducing the cook load on the extruder.

### Step 4 — Homogenization (Mixing)

Multi-component feeds (e.g., flour + vitamin/mineral premix + fat coating) are blended to a **coefficient of variation (CV) ≤ 5 %** for trace ingredients.

### Step 5 — Holding / Retention

A retention time of **60–180 seconds** in the conditioner allows moisture migration from the particle surface into the interior (diffusion-limited process).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains the **complete high-yield formula set** for Module 1 numerical questions on Pre-conditioning.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|:-:|:-------------|:---------|:-------------------|:------------|
| 1 | **Moisture Content (w.b.)** | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100$ | $m_w$ = mass of water (kg); $m_d$ = mass of dry solid (kg) | Express feed hydration |
| 2 | **Moisture Content (d.b.)** | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100$ | Same as above | Higher values; preferred in drying literature |
| 3 | **Conversion (wb ↔ db)** | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$ | $M_{wb}$ in % | KTU frequently tested |
| 4 | **Water to Add (mass basis)** | $m_{add} = m_s \left( \dfrac{M_t - M_i}{100 - M_t} \right)$ | $m_s$ = dry solid mass (kg); $M_t$ = target %; $M_i$ = initial % | Conditioning calculation |
| 5 | **Bond's Law (Energy)** | $W = W_i \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_p}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_f}} \right)$ | $W$ = specific energy (kWh/ton); $W_i$ = Work Index (kWh/ton); $d_p$, $d_f$ = product, feed size (µm) | Size reduction |
| 6 | **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** | $SME = \dfrac{\tau \cdot N}{m}$ | $\tau$ = torque (N·m); $N$ = screw speed (rpm); $m$ = throughput (kg/s) | Extruder energy input |
| 7 | **Mixing Homogeneity (CV)** | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100$ | $\sigma$ = std. deviation; $\bar{x}$ = mean | Quality control |
| 8 | **Steam Mass for Heating** | $m_{steam} = \dfrac{m_s c_p (T_t - T_i)}{\lambda_s + c_{pw}(T_s - T_t)}$ | $c_p$ = specific heat (kJ/kg·K); $T$ = temp (°C); $\lambda_s$ = latent heat (kJ/kg) | Pre-heating |
| 9 | **Fick's Diffusion (Moisture)** | $\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 M}{\partial x^2}$ | $D$ = diffusivity (m²/s); $x$ = depth (m); $t$ = time (s) | Moisture migration |
| 10 | **Throughput (Conditioner)** | $Q = \rho \cdot V \cdot A$ | $\rho$ = bulk density (kg/m³); $V$ = velocity (m/s); $A$ = cross-section (m²) | Retention time design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Index ($W_i$) Reference Values for KTU Calculations:**
> - Corn (ground): $W_i \approx 7.5$ kWh/ton
> - Wheat (hard): $W_i \approx 8.9$ kWh/ton
> - Soybean meal: $W_i \approx 6.8$ kWh/ton
> - Limestone: $W_i \approx 11.6$ kWh/ton

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Industry:** Soybean meal pre-conditioning before wet extrusion eliminates anti-nutritional factors (trypsin inhibitors) and improves protein digestibility.
- **Pet Food:** Steam conditioning at **90 °C for 90 s** before extrusion produces kibble with controlled expansion (Density: **350–500 g/L**).
- **Aquafeed:** Pre-conditioning reduces the requirement of high SME in the extruder, preserving heat-labile nutrients like vitamins and pigments.
- **Plastics/Metal:** Pre-conditioning in metal extrusion uses **billet pre-heating** to specific homologous temperatures ($T/T_m$) — often **0.5–0.8**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Water Required for Moisture Conditioning

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A feed formulation contains 1000 kg of dry solids with initial moisture of 12 % (w.b.). The target moisture for extrusion pre-conditioning is 28 % (w.b.). Calculate the mass of water to be added.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert initial moisture from wet basis to dry basis:**

Using the conversion formula:
$$M_{db,i} = \frac{M_{wb,i}}{100 - M_{wb,i}} \times 100$$

Substituting $M_{wb,i} = 12$ %:
$$M_{db,i} = \frac{12}{100 - 12} \times 100 = \frac{12}{88} \times 100 = 13.636\ \%$$

**Step 2 — Convert target moisture from wet basis to dry basis:**

Using $M_{wb,t} = 28$ %:
$$M_{db,t} = \frac{28}{100 - 28} \times 100 = \frac{28}{72} \times 100 = 38.889\ \%$$

**Step 3 — Express the mass balance equation:**

The water mass balance states that the total water in the conditioned product equals the water originally present plus the water added:
$$m_{w,total} = m_{w,initial} + m_{w,add}$$

**Step 4 — Compute initial water mass:**

$$m_{w,initial} = \frac{M_{db,i}}{100} \times m_d = \frac{13.636}{100} \times 1000 = 136.36\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Compute total water required at target moisture:**

$$m_{w,total} = \frac{M_{db,t}}{100} \times m_d = \frac{38.889}{100} \times 1000 = 388.89\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate water to add:**

$$m_{w,add} = m_{w,total} - m_{w,initial} = 388.89 - 136.36 = 252.53\ \text{kg}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{m_{w,add} \approx 252.5\ \text{kg of water}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation Check:** Total conditioned mass = $1000 + 252.53 = 1252.53$ kg. Verify moisture w.b.:
> $\frac{388.89}{1252.53} \times 100 = 31.05\ \%$ ... Slightly off due to initial mass baseline assumption.
> Correcting with full feed basis (initial wet mass = 1136.36 kg):
> $M_{wb,t} = \frac{388.89}{1389.17} \times 100 = 28.00\ \%$ ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Required Using Bond's Law

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
Corn is ground in a hammer mill from an initial particle size of 3000 µm to a product size of 500 µm. Given the Bond Work Index for corn $W_i = 7.5$ kWh/ton, calculate the specific energy consumption per ton and the total energy for processing 5 tons.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State Bond's Law explicitly:**

$$W = W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_f}} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given particle sizes:**

$$d_f = 3000\ \mu\text{m},\quad d_p = 500\ \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the inverse square roots:**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_p}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{500}} = \frac{1}{22.361} = 0.04472\ \mu\text{m}^{-1/2}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_f}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3000}} = \frac{1}{54.772} = 0.01826\ \mu\text{m}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the difference:**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_f}} = 0.04472 - 0.01826 = 0.02646\ \mu\text{m}^{-1/2}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by Work Index:**

$$W = 7.5 \times 0.02646 = 0.1985\ \text{kWh/ton}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate total energy for 5 tons:**

$$E_{total} = W \times m = 0.1985 \times 5 = 0.9925\ \text{kWh}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{W = 0.1985\ \text{kWh/ton};\quad E_{total} \approx 0.99\ \text{kWh for 5 tons}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steam Mass Required for Pre-heating

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
1000 kg of corn meal (initial temperature 25 °C, initial moisture 12 % w.b.) is to be conditioned to 85 °C using saturated steam at 1.2 bar (saturation temp ≈ 104.7 °C, latent heat ≈ 2240 kJ/kg). Target moisture is 28 % w.b. Calculate the steam mass required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute dry solids mass and water masses:**

From Section 3.1 calculations:
$$m_d = 1000\ \text{kg},\quad m_{w,initial} = 136.36\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — State the energy balance equation:**

Sensible heat required for the solid + initial water = Heat provided by steam (latent + cooling water + heating water):

$$m_s c_{ps} (T_t - T_i) + m_{w,i} c_{pw} (T_t - T_i) = m_{steam} \left[ \lambda_s + c_{pw}(T_s - T_t) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute standard specific heats:**

Using $c_{ps}$ (corn) = 1.65 kJ/kg·K, $c_{pw}$ = 4.186 kJ/kg·K:
$$\Delta T = 85 - 25 = 60\ \text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the LHS (heat demand):**

$$Q_{demand} = (1000 \times 1.65 \times 60) + (136.36 \times 4.186 \times 60)$$

$$Q_{demand} = 99000 + 34246 = 133246\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the per-kg heat contribution of steam:**

$$q_{steam} = \lambda_s + c_{pw}(T_s - T_t) = 2240 + 4.186 \times (104.7 - 85)$$

$$q_{steam} = 2240 + 4.186 \times 19.7 = 2240 + 82.46 = 2322.46\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6 — Compute mass of steam:**

$$m_{steam} = \frac{Q_{demand}}{q_{steam}} = \frac{133246}{2322.46} = 57.37\ \text{kg}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{m_{steam} \approx 57.4\ \text{kg of saturated steam at 1.2 bar}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pre-conditioning Methods

| Parameter | Cold Conditioning (Water) | Steam Conditioning | Dry Pre-heating (Hot Air) |
|:----------|:--------------------------|:-------------------|:--------------------------|
| Temperature achieved | 25–40 °C | 80–100 °C | 50–80 °C |
| Starch pre-gelatinization | Negligible | Partial (~10–30 %) | Negligible |
| Energy efficiency | High | Moderate | Low (high losses) |
| Microbial load reduction | None | Significant | Minimal |
| Typical retention time | 5–30 min | 60–180 s | 30–120 s |
| Best suited for | High-moisture extrusion | Pet food, aquafeed | Sensitive ingredients |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Pre-conditioning Line Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RawMaterialHopper] --> B[GrinderHammerMill]
    B --> C[ParticleClassifierScreen]
    C --> D[WeighScaleLossInWeighFeeder]
    D --> E[LiquidDosingWaterFat]
    E --> F[SteamInjectionManifold]
    F --> G[RibbonBlender]
    G --> H[SteamConditionerDDC]
    H --> I[RetentionHoldingTube]
    I --> J[ExtruderFeedingInlet]
    J --> K[ExtruderBarrel]

    classDef process fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K process
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**

- **Block A–C (Size Reduction Stage):** Raw material enters the hammer mill and is classified to the target geometric mean diameter (typically **250–600 µm** for most extrusion feeds).
- **Block D (Proportioning):** Loss-in-weight feeders ensure continuous mass-flow control with **±0.5 %** accuracy.
- **Block E–F (Hydration + Heating):** Liquid dosing and steam injection occur in parallel at the conditioner inlet.
- **Block G–I (Homogenization & Retention):** Ribbon blender and DDC (Differential Diameter Conditioner) provide mixing with **60–180 s** retention.
- **Block J–K (Hand-off):** The conditioned feed is metered into the extruder feed throat.

    S5 -- No --> S6[Inject Live Steam]

    S8 -- No --> S9[Adjust Steam Flow Rate]
    S9 --> S6
