# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define pre-conditioning of raw materials used in the extrusion process. List any four operations involved in pre-conditioning.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

Pre-conditioning refers to the series of preparatory operations — including moisture adjustment, particle size reduction, pre-heating, and homogeneous blending — carried out on the feed material **before** it enters the extruder barrel. (2 marks)

Four key operations are:
1. Moisture conditioning (water/steam addition)
2. Size reduction (grinding/milling)
3. Pre-heating (steam injection)
4. Mixing/blending of multi-component formulations (1 mark for listing)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**State the significance of steam conditioning in the extrusion process. What is the typical moisture range maintained for pet food extrusion?**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

Steam conditioning partially pre-gelatinizes starch, improves feed plasticity, reduces SME demand on the extruder, and enhances die flow stability. (2 marks)

Typical moisture range for pet food extrusion is **25–30 % (wet basis)** with steam temperature around **90–95 °C**. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**"Explain in detail the various operations involved in pre-conditioning of raw materials used in extrusion processing. Discuss the role of steam conditioning with reference to a Differential Diameter Conditioner (DDC)."**

#### Part (a) — Describe the various operations involved in pre-conditioning. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

Pre-conditioning consists of the following sequential operations:

**1. Particle Size Reduction (2 marks):**
Raw materials such as cereals (corn, wheat, rice), oilseeds (soybean, canola), and protein meals are reduced to a target particle size using hammer mills or roller mills. The Bond Work Index ($W_i$) characterizes the energy required. Typical target particle size for extrusion is **250–600 µm**.

**2. Moisture Conditioning (1.5 marks):**
Water or steam is added to raise moisture to the target level (typically 25–35 % w.b. for single-screw extruders; 10–22 % for twin-screw cookers). The mass of water required is computed using the wet-basis/dry-basis conversion formula:
$$m_{w,add} = m_s \left( \frac{M_t - M_i}{100 - M_t} \right)$$

**3. Mixing and Blending (1.5 marks):**
Multi-component formulations (flour + vitamin premix + fat + binder) are homogenized in ribbon or paddle mixers. Quality is assessed using the **Coefficient of Variation (CV)**, with the industry standard being **CV ≤ 5 %** for trace ingredients.

**4. Pre-heating / Steam Conditioning (1.5 marks):**
Saturated steam (1–3 bar) is injected to raise the feed temperature to 80–95 °C. This pre-gelatinizes starch partially, reduces SME demand, and improves die flow. Typical retention: **60–180 s**.

**5. Holding/Retention (0.5 marks):**
Allows moisture migration from surface to particle interior via diffusion-limited process.

---

#### Part (b) — Discuss the role of the Differential Diameter Conditioner (DDC) in steam conditioning. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **Differential Diameter Conditioner (DDC)** is a high-efficiency steam conditioner used in modern extrusion feed lines. Its key features and roles are:

**1. Geometry and Design (2 marks):**
The DDC consists of a horizontal cylindrical shell with a rotating shaft carrying paddles. The differential diameter (larger inlet section, smaller outlet section) creates two distinct retention zones — a **mixing zone** and a **holding zone** — providing total retention of **60–180 seconds**.

**2. Steam Injection (1.5 marks):**
Live steam is injected through a perforated manifold at controlled pressure (typically 1–3 bar). Steam provides both **latent heat** (raising temperature to 85–95 °C) and **moisture addition** (typically 2–5 % moisture gain from steam alone).

**3. Closed-Loop Moisture Control (2 marks):**
Modern DDCs integrate an **NIR moisture sensor** in the discharge, feeding real-time data to a PLC. The PLC modulates water/steam dosing valves to maintain target moisture within **±0.5 %**.

**4. Energy and Quality Benefits (1.5 marks):**
- Reduces SME by **15–25 %** because part of the cook load is offloaded.
- Improves product expansion, density uniformity, and gelatinization.
- Eliminates anti-nutritional factors in soybean meal (e.g., trypsin inhibitors).
- Capacity range: **1–30 tons/hour**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (14 Marks — Alternative)

**"A feed formulation of 2000 kg contains corn meal with initial moisture of 10 % (wet basis). It is to be conditioned for extrusion to a target moisture of 30 % (wet basis) and pre-heated to 90 °C using steam at 2 bar (saturation temp 120 °C, latent heat 2201 kJ/kg). (a) Calculate the mass of water to be added and (b) the mass of steam required. Assume $c_{p,\text{corn}} = 1.65$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{p,\text{water}} = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K, and initial temperature = 25 °C."**

#### Part (a) — Calculate the mass of water to be added. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert initial moisture to dry basis:
$$M_{db,i} = \frac{10}{100 - 10} \times 100 = 11.11\ \%$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute dry solid mass in 2000 kg feed:
$$m_d = 2000 \times (1 - 0.10) = 1800\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Convert target moisture to dry basis:
$$M_{db,t} = \frac{30}{100 - 30} \times 100 = 42.86\ \%$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute water required in conditioned product:
$$m_{w,target} = 1800 \times \frac{42.86}{100} = 771.43\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute initial water already present:
$$m_{w,initial} = 2000 - 1800 = 200\ \text{kg}$$

**Step 6 (1.5 marks):** Compute water to be added:
$$m_{w,add} = 771.43 - 200 = 571.43\ \text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{w,add} \approx 571.4\ \text{kg}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Calculate the mass of steam required for pre-heating. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Compute heat required to raise solids from 25 °C to 90 °C:
$$Q_s = m_d \cdot c_{p,corn} \cdot \Delta T = 1800 \times 1.65 \times (90 - 25)$$
$$Q_s = 1800 \times 1.65 \times 65 = 193{,}050\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Compute heat required to raise initial water (200 kg) from 25 °C to 90 °C:
$$Q_w = m_{w,i} \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T = 200 \times 4.186 \times 65 = 54{,}418\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute total heat demand:
$$Q_{total} = 193{,}050 + 54{,}418 = 247{,}468\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute per-kg heat delivered by steam (saturated at 120 °C, condensing and cooling to 90 °C):
$$q_{steam} = \lambda_s + c_{pw}(T_s - T_t) = 2201 + 4.186 \times (120 - 90)$$
$$q_{steam} = 2201 + 125.58 = 2326.58\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Compute steam mass:
$$m_{steam} = \frac{Q_{total}}{q_{steam}} = \frac{247{,}468}{2326.58} = 106.36\ \text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{steam} \approx 106.4\ \text{kg}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Moisture basis confusion** — Always verify whether the problem states **wet basis (w.b.)** or **dry basis (d.b.)**. Mixing them up is the **#1 cause** of full-mark loss (typically -3 to -7 marks).
> 2. **Dry solid vs. total feed mass** — The Bond's Law and water-addition formulas use **dry solid mass ($m_d$)**, NOT total feed mass. Many students substitute 2000 kg directly and lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Steam energy assumption** — Always include **both latent heat AND sensible cooling of condensate** unless explicitly told to assume isothermal condensation.
> 4. **Units of Bond's $W_i$** — Default unit is **kWh/ton**, not kJ/kg. Convert if necessary.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 (Pre-conditioning):**

- **Definition:** Pre-conditioning = preparatory operations **before** extrusion (NOT inside extruder).
- **4 T's:** Temperature, Texture, Total moisture, Thoroughness of mix.
- **Typical moisture range:**
  - Single-screw extruder: **25–35 % w.b.**
  - Twin-screw cooker: **10–22 % w.b.**
- **Typical steam conditioning temperature:** **80–95 °C** with retention of **60–180 s**.
- **DDC** = Differential Diameter Conditioner with NIR-based closed-loop moisture control (±0.5 %).
- **Bond's Law** is the primary size-reduction energy formula:
  $$W = W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_f}} \right)$$
- **Moisture conversions:**
  $$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{100 - M_{wb}} \times 100$$
- **Water addition formula (dry solids basis):**
  $$m_{w,add} = m_s \left( \frac{M_t - M_i}{100 - M_t} \right)$$
- **Steam energy equation:**
  $$m_{steam} = \frac{m_s c_p \Delta T + m_{w,i} c_{pw} \Delta T}{\lambda_s + c_{pw}(T_s - T_t)}$$
- **Bond Work Index reference:** Corn ≈ **7.5**, Wheat ≈ **8.9**, Soybean meal ≈ **6.8** kWh/ton.
- **Mixing Quality Metric:** **CV ≤ 5 %** for trace ingredients (vitamins, minerals).
- **Key equipment:** Hammer mill, ribbon blender, DDC, loss-in-weight feeder.
- **Benefits of pre-conditioning:**
  - Reduces SME by **15–25 %**
  - Improves expansion index & product uniformity
  - Reduces anti-nutritional factors in soybean
  - Enables tighter density control (pet food: **350–500 g/L**)
- **Common Exam Pitfall:** Do **NOT** confuse *pre-conditioning* (preparatory) with *pre-cooking* (in-barrel gelatinization).
- **Remember:** Pre-conditioning sets the **input entropy** of the extrusion process — the lower the input variability, the higher the output quality.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extruder Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Extruder Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Extruder Selection** is the systematic engineering process of evaluating and choosing the most suitable extrusion machinery (single-screw, twin-screw, or ram extruder) and its auxiliary components based on a multi-parameter optimization framework. The selection criteria typically encompass: **volumetric throughput rate (Q)**, **specific energy consumption (SEC)**, **melt temperature uniformity**, **residence time distribution (RTD)**, **pressure development capability (ΔP)**, **material rheological compatibility**, **product dimensional tolerance**, **capital and operational expenditure (CAPEX/OPEX)**, and **production flexibility**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, extruder selection is treated as a **design-decision matrix problem** wherein a manufacturing engineer must match polymer rheology (viscosity curves, shear sensitivity), product geometry (profile, pipe, sheet, film, blown), throughput target (kg/hr), and quality specifications (tolerances, surface finish, density) to the machine's geometric (screw diameter $D$, length-to-diameter ratio $L/D$, compression ratio $CR$), thermal (barrel zone count, heater wattage), and kinematic (screw speed $N$, torque capacity) capabilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Extruder selection is not merely the purchase of a machine — it is the *integrated matching* of **Material + Machine + Process + Product (the 4M Paradigm)**. A correct selection must be justified on quantitative technical grounds, not just economic ones.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are choosing a **water pump** to fill a swimming pool:
- A small fountain pump (low flow, low pressure) cannot do the job efficiently.
- A fire-fighting pump (high flow, high pressure) is overkill and wastes energy and money.
- You need to match the **pool's volume (throughput)**, **pipe diameter constraints (die geometry)**, **water viscosity (polymer melt flow index)**, and **fill time deadline (production rate)** to the pump's capacity.

An extruder is essentially a **"polymer pump and melter"** combined into one machine. Just like the pump analogy, selecting the wrong size extruder leads to:
- **Oversized machine:** Wasted energy, poor melt homogeneity, high screw RPM (degradation), high CAPEX.
- **Undersized machine:** Bottleneck in production, over-pressure trips, motor overload, poor mixing.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Extruder Selection

The following **bold-marked** metrics form the spine of any extruder selection decision:

- **Throughput (Q):** Mass output per unit time, typically in **kg/hr**.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Energy per unit mass, in **kWh/kg** (typical: 0.2–0.5 kWh/kg for single-screw).
- **Screw Diameter (D):** The dominant scaling parameter, typically **20 mm to 300 mm**.
- **L/D Ratio:** Length-to-diameter ratio (**20:1 to 40:1** for modern single-screw extruders).
- **Screw Speed (N):** Rotational speed, typically **10 to 300 rpm**.
- **Melt Pressure (P):** Die entry pressure, typically **50 to 700 bar**.
- **Apparent Shear Rate ($\dot{\gamma}_a$):** Approximated as $\pi D N / h$ where $h$ is the channel depth.
- **Motor Power (P_motor):** Typically **5 kW to 5000 kW** in industrial extruders.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rheological Constant:** For shear-thinning polymers (most thermoplastics), the **Power Law Index (n)** typically ranges from **0.2 to 0.5**. This single exponent controls how throughput scales with screw speed, die pressure, and screw geometry.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 4M Selection Paradigm (Why and How)

The selection of an extruder proceeds through a **hierarchical decision tree**, governed by four interacting pillars:

#### M1. **Material Considerations (Polymer Rheology)**
- **Melt Flow Index (MFI/MFR):** Determines baseline processability. High MFI → easy processing, low pressure; Low MFI → high viscosity, requires more robust screw.
- **Shear Sensitivity:** PVC, PP, HDPE degrade differently. PVC demands *bimetallic barrels* and *deep-flight screws*; HDPE tolerates *high compression ratios*.
- **Thermal Sensitivity:** Heat-sensitive polymers (PVC, POM) need *short L/D (20–24)* and *cooling-intensive screws*.
- **Abrasiveness:** Glass-fiber reinforced polymers demand *bimetallic liners* and *hardened screws* (stellite, nitrided).

#### M2. **Machine Considerations (Hardware Specs)**
- **Single-Screw vs. Twin-Screw vs. Ram Extruder** decision.
- **Screw Geometry:** Feed, compression, and metering zone lengths; compression ratio; flight helix angle (typically 17.78° for square-pitch).
- **Barrel Configuration:** Smooth bore, grooved feed, barrier flight.
- **Drive Train:** Motor power, gearbox torque rating, screw RPM range.

#### M3. **Process Considerations (Operating Window)**
- **Throughput Target:** Determines $D$ and $N$ via empirical scaling laws.
- **Melt Temperature Window:** Dictates barrel zone count (typically 3–5 zones).
- **Pressure Stability:** Requires screw with adequate metering depth.
- **Venting/Degassing:** Necessary for moisture-sensitive or recycled feedstock.

#### M4. **Product Considerations (Downstream Requirements)**
- **Profile/Geometry:** Thin films need *wide dies and high melt strength*; thick profiles need *low-shear screws*.
- **Tolerance Class:** Tight tolerances (medical, optical) demand *gear pump assisted* and *closed-loop pressure control*.
- **Surface Quality:** High-gloss surfaces require *melt homogeneity* (use *mixing sections*: Maddock, Saxton, pineapple).
- **Downstream Equipment:** Calibration, cooling, haul-off, cutting, winding must synchronize with extruder output.

### 2.2 Why Selection is Hierarchical

A student must understand that extruder selection is **not a single-variable optimization**. It is a **constrained multi-objective problem**:

- Maximize throughput $Q$
- Minimize specific energy SEC
- Minimize melt temperature $T_{melt}$
- Minimize residence time variance (good RTD)
- Respect motor torque limit $\tau_{max}$
- Respect die pressure limit $P_{die,max}$
- Respect product tolerance $\Delta x_{tol}$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Bank for Extruder Selection**

| **#** | **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Throughput (Newtonian, simplified) | $Q = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$ | m³/s | Drag-induced flow; $d$ = channel depth, $\phi$ = helix angle |
| 2 | Throughput (Power-law fluid) | $Q = \frac{\pi D N W d^2}}{2} f(n,\phi)$ | m³/s | $f(n,\phi)$ is shape factor $\in [0.3, 0.7]$ |
| 3 | Apparent Shear Rate | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{\pi D N}{h}$ | s⁻¹ | Used to read viscosity from rheometer curve |
| 4 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{P_{motor} \eta_{mech}}{Q}$ | kWh/kg | Typical: 0.2–0.5 kWh/kg |
| 5 | Die Pressure (Power-law) | $P_{die} = \dfrac{2 L \tau_w}{R}$ | Pa | For pipe dies; $\tau_w = K \dot{\gamma}_w^n$ |
| 6 | Motor Power (Theoretical) | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{T \cdot 2\pi N}{60}$ | W | $T$ = torque in N·m |
| 7 | Screw Scaling Law (Throughput) | $Q \propto D^{2.0} \text{ to } D^{2.5}$ | — | Empirical; $D^{2.5}$ is common for metering zone |
| 8 | Screw Scaling Law (Power) | $P \propto D^{2.5} \text{ to } D^{3.0}$ | — | Power grows faster than throughput |
| 9 | Compression Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{h_f}{h_m}$ | dimensionless | Typical 2:1 to 4:1 |
| 10 | Residence Time (mean) | $\bar{t} = \dfrac{V_{free}}{Q_{vol}}$ | s | $V_{free}$ = free volume in channels |
| 11 | Melt Temperature Rise (Adiabatic) | $\Delta T = \dfrac{SEC}{c_p}$ | °C | $c_p$ = specific heat of polymer |
| 12 | Output per Screw Revolution | $q = \dfrac{Q}{N}$ | cm³/rev | Linear scaling benchmark |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants for Quick Reference:**
> - Polymer melt density (PE): $\rho \approx$ **750–950 kg/m³**
> - Specific heat (PE melt): $c_p \approx$ **2300 J/(kg·K)**
> - Thermal conductivity (PE): $k \approx$ **0.24 W/(m·K)**
> - Mechanical efficiency: $\eta_{mech} \approx$ **0.85–0.95**
> - Helix angle (standard): $\phi =$ **17.78°** (square pitch)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Capacity Planning:** The $Q \propto D^{2.5}$ law allows quick estimation: doubling screw diameter yields ~5.66× throughput.
- **Energy Auditing:** SEC is a key KPI in ISO 50001 energy management for plastics plants.
- **Material Substitution:** When changing from LDPE to LLDPE, SEC can change by **20–30%** due to higher shear viscosity.
- **Process Scale-Up:** Scaling from a lab extruder ($D = 25$ mm) to production ($D = 90$ mm) requires careful re-evaluation of all geometric ratios.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Throughput of a Single-Screw Extruder (Newtonian, Simplified Drag Flow)

**Starting Premise:** Consider melt in the screw channel as a shallow rectangular trough of width $W = \pi D \sin\phi - e$ (where $e$ is flight width), depth $h$, and the barrel moving relative to the screw at tangential velocity $V_b = \pi D N$.

**Step 1 — Coordinate Transformation:**
Resolve the barrel velocity into the down-channel direction:
$$V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\phi$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity (Couette-like flow):**
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Integrate across the channel depth $h$ assuming no pressure gradient contribution (pure drag flow):
$$u(y) = \frac{V_{bz} \cdot y}{h}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric Flow Rate (Drag Component):**
Average velocity is $V_{bz}/2$. Multiply by cross-sectional area $W \cdot h$:
$$Q_d = \frac{1}{2} V_{bz} W h = \frac{\pi D N \cos\phi \cdot W h}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $W = \pi D \sin\phi$ (ignoring flight width):**
$$\boxed{Q_d = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}}$$

This is the **classical drag-flow equation**. In practice, total throughput is reduced by the **pressure flow** (back-leakage) component:
$$Q = Q_d - Q_p = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \frac{W h^3 \Delta P \sin^2\phi}{12 \mu L_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Selection of a *deeper metering channel* ($h$ larger) increases $Q_d$ linearly, but also increases back-leakage cubically. This is why $CR$ is bounded.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Screw Scaling Laws (Empirical)

**Premise:** Industrial experience shows that when scaling from a reference extruder (subscript 1) to a new size (subscript 2) at *geometric similarity* (same $L/D$, $h/D$, $\phi$):

**Step 1 — Throughput scaling:** Holding screw speed proportional to $1/D$ keeps shear rate constant:
$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{D_1}{D_2} \Rightarrow Q_2 = Q_1 \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^3 \cdot \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = Q_1 \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — But empirical observation gives exponent 2.0 to 2.5:**
This deviation from $D^2$ is due to improved melting efficiency at larger diameters (better surface-to-volume ratio). A widely accepted empirical fit:
$$\boxed{Q_2 = Q_1 \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{2.0 \text{ to } 2.5}}$$

**Step 3 — Power scaling:** Motor power must overcome viscous dissipation, which scales as:
$$P \propto \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma} \cdot V \propto \mu \dot{\gamma}^2 D^3$$

With $\dot{\gamma} \propto N/D \propto 1/D^2$ at constant $\dot{\gamma}$:
$$P_2 = P_1 \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^3$$

Empirically adjusted to 2.5 to 3.0:
$$\boxed{P_2 = P_1 \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{2.5 \text{ to } 3.0}}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:**
Given: Reference extruder: $D_1 = 45$ mm, $Q_1 = 80$ kg/hr, $P_1 = 30$ kW. Scale up to $D_2 = 90$ mm.

Using exponent 2.4 for throughput and 2.8 for power:
$$Q_2 = 80 \times (90/45)^{2.4} = 80 \times 2^{2.4} = 80 \times 5.28 = 422.4 \text{ kg/hr}$$
$$P_2 = 30 \times (90/45)^{2.8} = 30 \times 2^{2.8} = 30 \times 6.96 = 208.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Conclusion:** Doubling screw diameter increases throughput ~5.3× but requires ~7× power. This is why energy-efficient selection is critical.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

**Step 1 — Definition:**
$$SEC = \frac{\text{Energy input to melt per hour}}{\text{Mass output per hour}} = \frac{P_{dissipated}}{Q_{mass}}$$

**Step 2 — P_{dissipated} in terms of motor input:**
$$P_{dissipated} = P_{motor} \cdot \eta_{mech}$$

Therefore:
$$SEC = \frac{P_{motor} \cdot \eta_{mech}}{Q_{mass}}$$

**Step 3 — Express in kWh/kg:**
If $P_{motor}$ is in kW and $Q_{mass}$ in kg/hr:
$$SEC = \frac{P_{motor} \text{ [kW]}}{Q_{mass} \text{ [kg/hr]}} = \text{kWh/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute melt temperature rise (adiabatic):**
All dissipated energy raises melt temperature:
$$\Delta T = \frac{SEC \times 3600}{c_p}$$

For $SEC = 0.3$ kWh/kg and $c_p = 2300$ J/(kg·K):
$$\Delta T = \frac{0.3 \times 3600}{2300} = \frac{1080}{2300} = 0.47 \text{ °C per kWh/kg}$$

So 0.3 kWh/kg produces only 0.47 °C rise — sounds small, but with **10× larger extruders** running hours on end, this becomes significant.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection Decision Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to justify extruder selection in 14-mark KTU questions.**

| **Selection Criterion** | **Single-Screw (SS)** | **Twin-Screw Co-rotating** | **Twin-Screw Counter-rotating** | **Ram Extruder** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Throughput | Medium–High | High | Medium | Low |
| Mixing Quality | Poor–Moderate | Excellent | Good | Poor |
| Shear / Degradation | Moderate | High (for sensitive materials) | Moderate | Very Low |
| Cost (CAPEX) | Low | High | High | Low |
| Material Versatility | Low (dedicated) | Very High | High | Low |
| Pressure Capability | High (≥500 bar) | Medium | High | Very High |
| Typical Application | Pipe, sheet, film, profile | Compounding, masterbatch, PVC, wood-plastic | Devolatilization, reactive extrusion, PVC | UHMWPE, PTFE, large billets |
| Energy Efficiency | Good (0.20–0.30 kWh/kg) | Higher (0.30–0.50 kWh/kg) | Medium | Low |

### 3.5 Selection Algorithm (KTU-Style 14-Mark Answer Skeleton)

**Step 1 — Define Product:** Geometry, tolerance, material, annual volume.
**Step 2 — Estimate Required Q:** Annual volume / available production hours / utilization factor.
**Step 3 — Identify Material Rheology:** Viscosity curve, MFI, thermal stability.
**Step 4 — Match Screw Diameter:** Use $Q \propto D^{2.5}$ to estimate $D$.
**Step 5 — Compute Motor Power:** Use $P \propto D^{2.8}$ to estimate kW.
**Step 6 — Validate Shear Rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_a = \pi D N / h$ must lie in polymer's stable processing window.
**Step 7 — Check Residence Time:** $\bar{t} = V_{free}/Q_{vol}$ must be sufficient for melting and homogenization.
**Step 8 — Evaluate Barrel/Screw Materials:** Bimetallic if abrasive/reinforced.
**Step 9 — Economic Analysis:** CAPEX + 10-year OPEX (energy + maintenance + labor).
**Step 10 — Final Selection:** Document with comparative table.
