## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"List any four criteria used for the selection of an extruder for polymer processing."**

**Model Answer:**
The four primary criteria for extruder selection are:
1. **Material Characteristics:** Melt flow index (MFI), viscosity curve, thermal sensitivity, and abrasiveness of the polymer (e.g., glass-filled or neat).
2. **Throughput Requirement:** The desired mass output (kg/hr) determined by the annual production volume and available working hours.
3. **Product Geometry and Tolerance:** Whether the product is pipe, sheet, profile, film, or pellet, and the dimensional tolerance class required.
4. **Energy and Cost Economics:** Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in kWh/kg, capital cost (CAPEX), and operating cost (OPEX) over the machine's life.

> [!NOTE]
> A 3-mark question expects **3–4 crisp points** with one-line justifications. Avoid writing paragraphs.

#### **Question A2:** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"What is the significance of the L/D ratio in extruder selection? Mention typical values for modern single-screw extruders."**

**Model Answer:**
The **Length-to-Diameter (L/D) ratio** is a critical selection parameter that determines the extruder's ability to:
- **Melt the polymer completely** through sufficient shear and residence time.
- **Homogenize the melt** by allowing time for conductive and viscous dissipation.
- **Develop pressure** for the die by providing adequate metering length.
- **Vent volatiles** if the L/D permits a decompression zone (typical L/D ≥ 30 for venting).

**Typical L/D Values:**
- **20:1 to 24:1** — for heat-sensitive polymers (e.g., PVC).
- **25:1 to 30:1** — general purpose (PE, PP, PS).
- **30:1 to 40:1** — high-performance, devolatilization, or compounding duties.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

#### **Question B1A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **4M Paradigm (Material–Machine–Method–Product)** in the context of extruder selection. Justify the role of each factor with one example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturer wants to scale up production from a 45 mm screw extruder (output = 80 kg/hr, motor power = 30 kW) to a 90 mm screw extruder. Using empirical scaling laws ($Q \propto D^{2.4}$, $P \propto D^{2.8}$), estimate the new throughput and motor power. Comment on the energy implications. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a):**

The 4M Paradigm is a structured framework for extruder selection:

**1. Material (Resin Properties):** The polymer's rheology dictates screw geometry. *Example:* PVC (heat-sensitive, high viscosity) requires a *bimetallic barrel* and *L/D of 22:1* to avoid thermal degradation.

**2. Machine (Hardware):** The extruder's mechanical and thermal capability must match the process. *Example:* A *co-rotating twin-screw* is selected for compounding glass-fiber-filled nylon to ensure distributive mixing.

**3. Method (Process Parameters):** Operating conditions like screw speed, temperature profile, and throughput must be within the machine's window. *Example:* Setting screw speed too high can cause melt fracture in LLDPE — a method–material interaction.

**4. Product (Downstream Requirements):** The end-product dictates die and downstream configuration. *Example:* A thin BOPP film requires a *coathanger die* and *chill roll*, while a thick HDPE pipe requires a *spider die* and *vacuum calibration*.

##### **Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Apply Throughput Scaling Law:**
$$Q_2 = Q_1 \times \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{2.4}$$
$$Q_2 = 80 \times \left(\frac{90}{45}\right)^{2.4} = 80 \times (2)^{2.4}$$
$$Q_2 = 80 \times 5.278 = 422.2 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Motor Power Scaling Law:**
$$P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{2.8}$$
$$P_2 = 30 \times (2)^{2.8} = 30 \times 6.964 = 208.9 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute SEC Before and After:**
$$SEC_1 = \frac{30}{80} = 0.375 \text{ kWh/kg}$$
$$SEC_2 = \frac{208.9}{422.2} = 0.495 \text{ kWh/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Energy Implications:**
Although throughput increases **5.28×**, power consumption increases **6.96×**. As a result, **SEC increases by 32%**. This is because larger extruders have *relatively higher viscous dissipation* in the deeper channels. **Engineering recommendation:** To restore energy efficiency, the screw should be designed with a *barrier flight* or *grooved feed zone*, which can reduce SEC by 10–15%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Skipping the SEC comparison step** — KTU examiners explicitly look for the *engineering insight* that larger ≠ more efficient. You lose 2 marks if you only compute Q and P.
> 2. **Using a single exponent (e.g., D²)** for both Q and P — KTU expects you to state that *empirical exponents differ* (2.4 vs 2.8) and justify why.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — always write *kg/hr* and *kW* explicitly.
> 4. **Not commenting on energy implication** — the question explicitly asks, so a missing comment costs 1–2 marks.

#### **Question B1B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Compare the suitability of **single-screw**, **twin-screw co-rotating**, and **ram extruders** for the following applications, with justification:
  (i) Compounding 30% glass-fiber-filled PP masterbatch.
  (ii) Extrusion of UHMWPE rods.
  (iii) Production of LDPE packaging film at 500 kg/hr. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a sketch, describe the **function of the metering zone** in a single-screw extruder. Derive the **drag-flow throughput equation** for a Newtonian fluid in the metering channel. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution (a):**

**(i) Compounding 30% GF-filled PP masterbatch:** **Twin-screw co-rotating extruder** is most suitable. Justification: Excellent *distributive and dispersive mixing* is required to wet out glass fibers and disperse them uniformly. The modular screw design allows kneading and reverse elements. Single-screw gives poor fiber dispersion. Ram extruder cannot provide continuous mixing.

**(ii) UHMWPE rods:** **Ram extruder** is the only viable option. Justification: UHMWPE has *extremely high melt viscosity* (essentially non-flowing melt) and degrades catastrophically under screw shear. A ram extruder uses *plunger-driven* low-shear plug flow, avoiding molecular degradation. Single and twin-screw extruders cannot process UHMWPE.

**(iii) LDPE film at 500 kg/hr:** **Large single-screw extruder** (e.g., 90–120 mm) is suitable. Justification: LDPE is *shear-sensitive but not abrasively demanding*. A single-screw with barrier flight provides high throughput at low SEC. Twin-screw would be over-specified and more expensive. Ram extruder is too slow (low continuous throughput).

##### **Model Solution (b):**

**Function of the Metering Zone:**
The metering zone is the *final and shortest* section of the screw (length ~ 20–25% of total screw length) with a **constant, shallow channel depth ($h_m$)**. Its functions are:
1. **Generate pressure** at the die end through viscous drag and resistance.
2. **Homogenize the melt** by ensuring uniform temperature and shear history.
3. **Meter the output** at a stable, predictable rate (Q ≈ constant).

**Derivation of Drag-Flow Throughput Equation (Newtonian Fluid):**

**Step 1 — Channel Geometry:** Unwound channel has width $W = \pi D \sin\phi - e \approx \pi D \sin\phi$, depth $h_m$, and length along the channel $L_m = L/\sin\phi$.

**Step 2 — Barrel Velocity Component:** The barrel moves tangentially at $V_b = \pi D N$. Its component along the channel (down-channel) is:
$$V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\phi$$

**Step 3 — Velocity Profile in the Channel:** For pure drag flow (zero pressure gradient), the Navier–Stokes equation reduces to:
$$\mu \frac{d^2 u}{dy^2} = 0$$
with boundary conditions $u(0) = 0$ (screw root stationary) and $u(h_m) = V_{bz}$ (barrel at velocity $V_{bz}$). Integrating:
$$u(y) = \frac{V_{bz} \cdot y}{h_m}$$

**Step 4 — Average Velocity:**
$$\bar{u} = \frac{1}{h_m}\int_0^{h_m} u(y) dy = \frac{V_{bz}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric Flow Rate:**
$$Q_d = \bar{u} \cdot W \cdot h_m = \frac{V_{bz} \cdot W \cdot h_m}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $V_{bz}$ and $W$:**
$$Q_d = \frac{\pi D N \cos\phi \cdot \pi D \sin\phi \cdot h_m}{2}$$

$$\boxed{Q_d = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h_m \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}}$$

Using $\sin\phi \cos\phi = \dfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\phi$ and with $\phi = 17.78°$:
$$Q_d = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h_m}{2} \cdot \frac{\sin 35.56°}{2} = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h_m}{2} \cdot 0.2906$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss on Question B1B:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the Newtonian assumption** — KTU requires explicit mention of constant $\mu$ or "Newtonian".
> 2. **Skipping boundary conditions** — writing $u(0) = 0$ and $u(h_m) = V_{bz}$ is *mandatory* for full marks.
> 3. **Not unwinding the screw** — students often forget to justify that the channel is treated as a flat trough with $W = \pi D \sin\phi$.
> 4. **Mixing up drag flow and pressure flow** — KTU often has a follow-up on pressure flow; be ready to write $Q = Q_d - Q_p$.
> 5. **Sketch without labels** — unlabeled sketches lose 1–2 marks. Always label $h_m$, $L_m$, $\phi$, $W$, and the rotation direction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 15 High-Yield Points for Last-Minute Revision:**

- [ ] **Extruder selection is a 4M problem:** Material + Machine + Method + Product must all be satisfied.
- [ ] **Throughput scales as** $Q \propto D^{2.4}$ to $D^{2.5}$ (empirical, not $D^2$).
- [ ] **Motor power scales as** $P \propto D^{2.8}$ to $D^{3.0}$ — power grows *faster* than throughput.
- [ ] **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is the key efficiency metric: $SEC = P_{motor}/Q$ in kWh/kg.
- [ ] **SEC for single-screw:** 0.20–0.30 kWh/kg; **twin-screw:** 0.30–0.50 kWh/kg.
- [ ] **L/D ratio** modern range: **20:1** (heat-sensitive) → **30–40:1** (general/compounding).
- [ ] **Compression Ratio (CR)** typical: **2:1 to 4:1**.
- [ ] **Standard helix angle** for square-pitch screws: **17.78°**.
- [ ] **Drag flow equation:** $Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N h_m \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$.
- [ ] **Apparent shear rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_a = \pi D N / h$ — must match rheometer data window.
- [ ] **Twin-screw co-rotating** → best for compounding, masterbatch, PVC, wood-plastic composites.
- [ ] **Twin-screw counter-rotating** → high pressure, devolatilization, reactive extrusion.
- [ ] **Ram extruder** → mandatory for **UHMWPE, PTFE, and other non-flowing polymers**.
- [ ] **Bimetallic barrel** → required for abrasive (glass-filled), corrosive (PVC), or high-temperature polymers.
- [ ] **Selection always** ends with **economic analysis (CAPEX + 10-yr OPEX)**, not just technical match.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Recall Mnemonic — "S-CALE-D":**
> **S** — Screw diameter (D) is the master scaling parameter.
> **C** — Compression ratio (CR = h_f / h_m).
> **A** — Apparent shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}_a = \pi D N / h$).
> **L** — L/D ratio (length to diameter).
> **E** — Energy (SEC in kWh/kg).
> **D** — Drive train (motor power and torque).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Operation for Different Food Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Design and Operation for Different Food Applications ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Food Extrusion Design** refers to the systematic engineering of extruder components — screw geometry, barrel configuration, die assembly, and ancillary feeding/cutting systems — to satisfy the rheological, thermal, and structural requirements of a target food matrix. **Operation** encompasses the controlled manipulation of process variables (moisture content, barrel temperature profile, screw speed, feed rate, and die pressure) to consistently produce a finished product with predetermined physico-chemical, textural, and nutritional attributes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based framework, students must be able to *select and justify* extruder designs (single vs. twin-screw, co-rotating vs. counter-rotating) and operational windows for distinct food categories — namely **snacks, breakfast cereals, pasta, texturized vegetable protein (TVP), pet food, and breadings/crumb coatings**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Screw-Press Bakery" Imagine a giant industrial baker's piping bag, but instead of squeezing cake frosting at room temperature, imagine forcing a cooked dough through a shaped nozzle under **high temperature (120–200 °C)** and **high pressure (20–150 bar)** while a rotating auger continuously mixes, shears, and cooks the material. That is extrusion cooking. - **The bag** = **Barrel** (heated, jacketed) - **The auger** = **Screw** (with variable pitch, flight, and kneading elements) - **The nozzle tip** = **Die** (defines final shape — curls, pellets, sheets) - **The baker's hand pressure** = **Drive motor torque & screw speed** - **The dough's dryness** = **Feed moisture content (typically 10–30 % w/w)** > [!NOTE] > **Why does food matter?** Different foods behave differently: *corn grits* are hard and abrasive (need wear-resistant barrel liners), *high-fat doughs* are sticky (need anti-adhesion coatings), *high-protein mixes* need high specific mechanical energy (SME) for texturization. Hence **design is product-specific**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Cook temperature range:** 120 °C – 200 °C - **Screw speed range:** 100 – 600 rpm - **Feed moisture:** 10 % – 45 % (wb) - **Throughput:** 50 – 20,000 kg/h - **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME):** 100 – 1500 W·h/kg - **L/D ratio:** typically 20 : 1 to 40 : 1 ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Design Philosophy — The 4 Pillars 1. **Feed Characterization** - Starch-based (cereals, snacks): Require gelatinization + expansion. - Protein-based (TVP, pet food): Require texturization via high shear. - Fat-rich formulations (>15 %): Require cooling extruders to prevent slippage. - High-fiber/high-moisture (>30 %): Require long-barrel cooking extruders. 2. **Screw Geometry Selection** | Element Type | Function | Application | |---|---|---| | Feed screw (deep flight) | Conveying bulk solids | Inlet zone | | Conveying screw | Controlled transport | Cooking zone | | Kneading discs (offset) | Mixing, shear, disruption | High-shear zone | | Reverse pitch elements | Pressure generation, mixing | Melt seal | | Leaning (paddle) elements | Gentle mixing | Low-shear zones for delicate products | | Die-end screw | Final pressurization | Before die | 3. **Barrel & Die Temperature Zoning** The barrel is segmented into independently controlled zones. Typical zoning for **expanded snacks**: - Zone 1 (Feed): **30–60 °C** (pre-conditioning) - Zone 2 (Mixing/Kneading): **80–120 °C** - Zone 3 (Cooking): **140–170 °C** - Zone 4 (Metering): **150–180 °C** - Die: **150–200 °C** 4. **Die Geometry** - **Open die:** Used for elastic melts (e.g., pasta) → low expansion. - **Restricted/land die:** Creates back-pressure for explosive puffing. - **Co-extrusion die:** For filled products (cream-filled snacks). > [!NOTE] > **Die Land Length (L) to Hole Diameter (D) ratio** is the single most important design lever for expansion control. Typical **L/D = 2 to 10** for puffed snacks; **L/D < 1** for elastic, non-expanded products. ### 2.2 Operation — Key Process Variables | Variable | Symbol | Range | Effect | |---|---|---|---| | Feed rate | $\dot{m}$ | 50 – 20 000 kg/h | Throughput, SME | | Screw speed | $N$ | 100 – 600 rpm | SME, shear, residence time | | Moisture content | $MC$ | 10 – 45 % | Expansion, viscosity | | Barrel temperature | $T$ | 30 – 200 °C | Starch gelatinization, protein denaturation | | Die pressure | $P$ | 20 – 150 bar | Expansion ratio | | Screw L/D ratio | $L/D$ | 20 – 40 | Cook intensity | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{SME} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot \omega}{\dot{m}}$ | Specific Mechanical Energy | W·h/kg | | 2 | $Q = \rho \cdot N \cdot V_{d} \cdot \eta_{f}$ | Throughput (drag flow) | kg/h | | 3 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^{3}}$ | Die pressure (slit die, Newtonian) | Pa | | 4 | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{die}}}{\rho_{\text{product}}}$ | Expansion Ratio | dimensionless | | 5 | $\tau_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{free}}}{\dot{V}}$ | Mean Residence Time | s | | 6 | $\text{SI} = \dfrac{N^{2} L}{D}$ | Shear Intensity Index | 1/s | | 7 | $\tau_{w} = \mu \dot{\gamma}_{w}$ | Wall shear stress | Pa | | 8 | $P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Motor power required | W | | 9 | $N_{c} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{steam}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}}}$ | Steam-to-feed ratio | dimensionless | | 10 | $V_{d} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2}) \cdot t - V_{f}$ | Displaced volume per screw turn | m³/rev | Where: - $\tau$ = torque (N·m), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $\dot{m}$ = mass flow (kg/s) - $\rho$ = density, $V_{d}$ = displaced volume, $\eta_{f}$ = fill efficiency (0.3 – 0.5) - $\mu$ = viscosity, $L$ = die land length, $w, h$ = slit die dimensions - $D, d$ = screw OD and root diameter; $t$ = pitch; $V_{f}$ = flight volume - $T$ = torque at motor shaft > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Condition for Pressure Flow in Extruder:** Pressure flow must NEVER exceed drag flow or the extruder will "choke" (zero net output). Design rule: **$Q_{p} < 0.5 Q_{d}$**. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pet food industry:** Twin-screw extruders dominate because precise moisture + high protein processing demands accurate SME control (typically 80–150 W·h/kg). - **Breakfast cereal (flaked):** Low SME (40–80 W·h/kg) cooking + post-extrusion flaking rolls. - **Third-generation (3G) snacks:** Ultra-low density (≤ 0.1 g/cc) — needs high SME and die L/D > 5. - **Pasta:** Cold extrusion (T < 50 °C), high pressure, low SME — the opposite of snack extrusion. - **Texturized Soy Protein (TVP):** Uses "high-moisture extrusion" (MC 30–60 %) to create fibrous, meat-like texture. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) The **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** quantifies the mechanical work input per unit mass of product and is the master design variable for extrusion. Let us derive it from first principles. **Step 1 — Mechanical Power Delivered by the Screw Shaft** The motor transfers rotational power to the screw shaft. The instantaneous power is the product of torque and angular velocity: $$ P_{\text{shaft}} = \tau \cdot \omega $$ Substituting $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ where $N$ is in revolutions per minute: $$ P_{\text{shaft}} = \tau \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}] $$ **Step 2 — Convert Rotational Speed to Energy per Unit Mass** Dividing mechanical power by the mass throughput $\dot{m}$ gives energy supplied per kilogram of product. However, this is in W·s/kg = J/kg. Since $1 \text{ W·h} = 3600 \text{ J}$, we divide by 3600 to obtain SME in W·h/kg: $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot \omega}{\dot{m}} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}}{\dot{m} \cdot 3600} $$ **Step 3 — Final Compact Form** $$ \boxed{\text{SME} = \dfrac{2 \pi N \tau}{216000 \, \dot{m}}} \quad [\text{W·h/kg}] $$ > **Conversion logic check:** $N$ in rpm, $\tau$ in N·m, $\dot{m}$ in kg/s. Result is W·h/kg. KTU examiners test the *constant 216 000* frequently. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Drag-Flow Throughput The drag-flow model treats the extruder screw channel as an unwound, inclined plane. Melt is dragged forward by the relative motion of the barrel wall. **Step 1 — Geometry of Unwound Channel** Consider a single-flight screw of outer diameter $D$, root diameter $d$, pitch $t$, and channel depth $H = (D - d)/2$. When unwound, the channel length per turn is approximately $\pi D / \cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the helix angle. **Step 2 — Relative Velocity of Barrel Wall** The barrel surface moves relative to the screw at velocity: $$ v_{b} = \pi D N \cos\theta $$ **Step 3 — Drag Flow Rate (Newtonian Assumption)** For a Newtonian fluid in a shallow channel of width $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e$ (where $e$ is flight width) and depth $H$: $$ Q_{d} = \dfrac{1}{2} v_{b} W H $$ Substituting $v_{b} = \pi D N \cos\theta$: $$ \boxed{Q_{d} = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi D N \cos\theta \cdot W \cdot H} $$ **Step 4 — Include Fill Efficiency** Real extruders are not 100 % filled due to conveying inefficiencies and starve feeding. Introduce $\eta_{f} \in [0.3, 0.5]$: $$ Q = \eta_{f} \cdot Q_{d} = \eta_{f} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \pi D N \cos\theta \cdot W \cdot H $$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Drop Across a Slit Die When a Newtonian melt passes through a rectangular slit die of land length $L$, width $w$, and gap $h$, the Hagen–Poiseuille relation gives: **Step 1 — Pressure-Driven Flow Between Parallel Plates** For fully developed laminar flow between stationary plates separated by distance $h$: $$ \dot{V} = \dfrac{w h^{3} \Delta P}{12 \mu L} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for Pressure Drop** Rearranging for $\Delta P$: $$ \Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L \dot{V}}{w h^{3}} $$ **Step 3 — Apply to Extrusion** For an extruder, $\dot{V} = \dot{m}/\rho$: $$ \boxed{\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L \dot{m}}{\rho \, w \, h^{3}}} $$ > **Insight:** The cubic dependence on gap $h$ explains why die designers drastically alter pressure by minor gap adjustments. Halving the gap increases pressure drop by **8×**. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Expansion Ratio (ER) The **Expansion Ratio** is the ratio of cross-sectional area (or diameter) of the extrudate post-die to that at the die exit: $$ \text{ER} = \dfrac{D_{e}^{2}}{D_{d}^{2}} = \dfrac{A_{e}}{A_{d}} $$ In terms of densities (mass conservation across the die with negligible moisture loss): $$ \boxed{\text{ER} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{die}}}{\rho_{\text{product}}}} $$ **Step 5 — Combine with Die Design Parameter** A widely used empirical correlation from literature (and frequently cited in KTU ESE): $$ \text{ER} \propto \left(\dfrac{T_{\text{die}} - T_{\text{glass}}}{\dot{m}/A_{d}}\right)^{0.4} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\text{SME}}{MC}\right)^{0.3} $$ This shows **ER increases with higher die temperature, higher SME, lower moisture content, and lower die throughput**. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Product × Design/Operation Recommendations | Food Product | Screw Type | L/D | SME (W·h/kg) | Moisture (%) | Die L/D | Die T (°C) | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Direct-expanded snacks | Twin, co-rotating | 24–32 | 120–200 | 12–15 | 4–8 | 160–180 | | 3G Pellets (half-products) | Twin, co-rotating | 32–40 | 60–100 | 18–24 | 1–2 | 100–130 | | Breakfast cereals (puffed) | Single or twin | 16–20 | 60–110 | 14–18 | 2–4 | 150–170 | | Pet food (dry kibbles) | Twin, co-rotating | 24–30 | 80–150 | 20–28 | 2–4 | 130–160 | | TVP (high moisture) | Twin, co-rotating | 30–40 | 150–400 | 30–60 | 10–20 | 140–160 | | Pasta | Single or twin | 10–14 | <30 | 28–32 | 5–15 | <50 (cold) | | Bread crumbs | Twin, co-rotating | 24–30 | 80–130 | 22–28 | 3–5 | 140–160 | | Crackers / biscuits | Single | 12–16 | 40–70 | 18–22 | 2–4 | 80–110 | | Aquafeed (fish feed) | Twin, co-rotating | 32–40 | 100–200 | 22–30 | 4–8 | 130–160 | | Infant food / weaning | Twin, co-rotating | 28–36 | 60–120 | 18–24 | 1–3 | 130–150 | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generalized Food Extrusion Line ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Material
Reception] --> B[Grinding
and Sifting] B --> C[Preconditioner
Steam + Water + Liquid] C --> D[Feeder
Loss-in-Weight] D --> E[Extruder Barrel
Zone 1 to Zone n] E --> F[Die Assembly
Land + Orifice] F --> G[Knife Cutter
Variable Speed] G --> H[Drying Oven
or Toaster] H --> I[Coating Drum
Seasoning] I --> J[Cooling Conveyor] J --> K[Packaging] E --> L[Steam Lock
and Venting] L --> M[Vacuum Vent
for Moisture] M --> N[Condensate Trap] ``` ### 4.2 Screw Configuration Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Zone
Deep Flight Screw] --> B[Kneading Zone
Disc Offset 30deg] B --> C[Kneading Zone
Disc Offset 60deg] C --> D[Kneading Zone
Disc Offset 90deg] D --> E[Reverse Pitch
Element Seal] E --> F[Metering Zone
Shallow Flight] F --> G[Conveying Screw
Final Pressurization] G --> H[Die Adapter] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting Extruder Configuration ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Target Food Product] --> B{Protein Content?} B -- High GT 40 percent --> C{Target Texture?} B -- Low LT 20 percent --> D{Expansion Required?} C -- Fibrous --> E[High Moisture Twin Screw
L over D = 30 to 40] C -- Crispy --> F[Low Moisture Twin Screw
L over D = 20 to 30] D -- High --> G{Throughput?} D -- Low --> H[Single Screw Cooking Extruder] G -- High GT 1000 kg/h --> I[Twin Screw Co-Rotating
High Shear] G -- Low --> J[Single Screw with
Conditioning] ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Twin-Screw Process Stages ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Zone1["FEED ZONE - Solid Conveying"] Z1A[Hopper] --> Z1B[Feed Screw Element] end subgraph Zone2["MIXING and KNEADING ZONE - Hydration and Shear"] Z2A[Kneading Discs 30 deg] --> Z2B[Kneading Discs 60 deg] --> Z2C[Kneading Discs 90 deg] end subgraph Zone3["COOKING ZONE - Melt and Gelatinization"] Z3A[Reverse Pitch Seal] --> Z3B[Long Conveying Screw] end subgraph Zone4["METERING and DIE ZONE - Pressure Build-up"] Z4A[Shallow Flight Screw] --> Z4B[Die Plate] end Zone1 --> Zone2 --> Zone3 --> Zone4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Note:** Node IDs are alphanumeric (zone1A, zone2A, etc.) and labels contain only uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and spaces — no markdown formatting inside node text — to ensure successful compilation across all renderers. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]** *Define Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) in extrusion. Mention its significance in product design.* **Model Answer:** Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) is defined as the mechanical energy input per unit mass of product, given by: $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi N \tau}{216000 \, \dot{m}} \quad [\text{W·h/kg}] $$ **Significance in product design:** - SME controls **starch gelatinization** degree, **protein texturization**, and **product expansion**. - **Low SME (< 60 W·h/kg)** → dense, elastic products (pasta, some cereals). - **Medium SME (80–150 W·h/kg)** → expanded snacks, pet food. - **High SME (> 200 W·h/kg)** → texturized vegetable protein, highly expanded 3G snacks. - It is the **single most important design variable** for matching extruder geometry to product target. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Differentiate between cold extrusion and hot extrusion with suitable food examples.* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Cold Extrusion | Hot Extrusion | |---|---|---| | Barrel temperature | < 50 °C | 120 – 200 °C | | Moisture | 28 – 32 % | 10 – 25 % | | SME | < 30 W·h/kg | 80 – 400 W·h/kg | | Pressure | High (50 – 150 bar) | Moderate (20 – 80 bar) | | Cooking effect | None | Starch gelatinization | | Expansion | Negligible | Significant puffing | | Examples | Pasta, certain noodles | Snacks, TVP, pet food | | Screw type | Smooth, low-shear | High-shear kneading | **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model — Module 1 Choice]** **(a)** Describe in detail the **design considerations for screw configuration** in a co-rotating twin-screw extruder for the production of **direct-expanded corn snacks**. Justify the choice of L/D ratio, kneading element angle, and reverse pitch elements. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — L/D Ratio Selection (2 marks)** For direct-expanded snacks, residence time must be sufficient to achieve complete starch gelatinization while avoiding over-cooking. Industry-standard design selects **L/D = 24–32**. - Too low (<20): Incomplete gelatinization, hard texture. - Too high (>40): Over-cooked, burnt notes, low throughput. **Step 2 — Kneading Disc Angle Selection (3 marks)** Kneading discs are arranged in stagger pattern: - **30° offset:** Low shear, used in initial mixing zones where gentle hydration occurs. - **60° offset:** Medium shear, used for homogenization. - **90° offset (neutral):** Maximum shear, used to break down starch granules and induce gelatinization at the transition zone. A short 90° staggered block of 3–5 discs is typically placed before the reverse element. **Step 3 — Reverse Pitch Element Justification (2 marks)** A reverse pitch element (left-handed flight) creates a **melt seal** by forcing material backward. This: - Generates the high die pressure (40–80 bar) required for explosive expansion at the die exit. - Increases **fill ratio** → higher SME input. - Provides residence time for complete cook. **Conclusion:** A typical configuration is **feed screws → 30° kneading (3 discs) → 60° kneading (4 discs) → 90° kneading (4 discs) → reverse pitch (1 element) → metering screws → die adapter**. **(Full 7 marks)** --- **(b)** A co-rotating twin-screw extruder is operated at **N = 400 rpm** with measured motor torque **$\tau$ = 250 N·m** and feed rate **$\dot{m}$ = 800 kg/h**. Calculate the Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) and comment on its suitability for producing **expanded snack pellets** (target SME: 100–180 W·h/kg). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $N = 400$ rpm - $\tau = 250$ N·m - $\dot{m} = 800$ kg/h = $800/3600 = 0.2222$ kg/s **Step 1 — Convert $\dot{m}$ to kg/s (already done above).** **Step 2 — Apply the SME formula:** $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{2 \pi N \tau}{216000 \, \dot{m}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute values:** $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 400 \times 250}{216000 \times 0.2222} $$ **Step 4 — Numerator computation:** $$ 2 \pi \times 400 \times 250 = 628318.5 \text{ (units: N·m·rad·min⁻¹)} $$ **Step 5 — Denominator computation:** $$ 216000 \times 0.2222 = 48000.0 \text{ (units: dimensionless constant × kg/s)} $$ **Step 6 — Final SME:** $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{628318.5}{48000.0} = 13.09 \text{ W·h/kg} $$ **Step 7 — Comment:** The calculated SME of **13.09 W·h/kg** is **far below** the target range of 100–180 W·h/kg. The product would be **under-cooked**, with poor expansion, dense texture, and incomplete gelatinization. **Step 8 — Corrective action:** Increase screw speed, increase fill (use reverse pitch element), or reduce feed rate. If N is increased to 1200 rpm (max) keeping $\tau = 350$ N·m, SME rises to: $$ \text{SME}_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{2\pi(1200)(350)}{216000 \times 0.2222} = 55 \text{ W·h/kg} $$ Still insufficient. The feed rate must be reduced to ~ 200 kg/h to enter the target range. **(Full 7 marks)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model — Module 1 Choice Alternative]** **(a)** Explain the **operating parameter window** (temperature, moisture, screw speed, SME) for **high-moisture extrusion** of **texturized vegetable protein (TVP)**. Why is the design fundamentally different from snack extrusion? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):** High-moisture extrusion (HME) operates at moisture contents of **30–60 %** (vs. 12–18 % for snacks) to produce fibrous, meat-analog texture from plant proteins. **Step 2 — Operating Window (4 marks):** | Parameter | TVP Range | Snack Range | |---|---|---| | Moisture | 30 – 60 % | 12 – 18 % | | Barrel T | 120 – 160 °C | 140 – 180 °C | | Screw speed | 150 – 300 rpm | 300 – 600 rpm | | SME | 150 – 400 W·h/kg | 100 – 200 W·h/kg | | Die L/D | 10 – 20 (long cooling die) | 4 – 8 | | Die T | 100 – 140 °C (cooled!) | 160 – 180 °C | **Step 3 — Fundamental Differences (2 marks):** 1. **Protein vs. Starch Focus:** TVP requires protein **denaturation + cross-linking + alignment** under high moisture. Snacks require starch **gelatinization + expansion** under low moisture. 2. **Cooled Die:** Long, water-jacketed cooling die (L/D = 10–20) is essential in HME to solidify the fibrous structure before exit. In snacks, the die is hot to maintain melt viscosity for expansion. 3. **Lower Screw Speed:** Prevents excessive mechanical degradation of protein fibrils which would destroy the fibrous texture. 4. **Higher SME:** Required to unfold and re-orient protein molecules along the flow direction, creating anisotropic, meat-like bite. --- **(b)** A **slit die** of width $w = 30$ mm, gap $h = 4$ mm, land length $L = 80$ mm is fitted to an extruder. The melt viscosity at the die wall is $\mu = 5000$ Pa·s, density $\rho = 1400$ kg/m³, and throughput $\dot{m} = 250$ kg/h. Calculate the die pressure drop $\Delta P$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $w = 30$ mm = 0.030 m - $h = 4$ mm = 0.004 m - $L = 80$ mm = 0.080 m - $\mu = 5000$ Pa·s - $\rho = 1400$ kg/m³ - $\dot{m} = 250$ kg/h = $250/3600 = 0.0694$ kg/s **Step 1 — Convert mass flow to volumetric flow:** $$ \dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{0.0694}{1400} = 4.96 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 2 — Apply slit-die pressure drop equation (Newtonian):** $$ \Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L \dot{V}}{w h^{3}} $$ **Step 3 — Compute denominator:** $$ w h^{3} = 0.030 \times (0.004)^{3} = 0.030 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-8} = 1.92 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^4 $$ **Step 4 — Substitute:** $$ \Delta P = \dfrac{12 \times 5000 \times 0.080 \times 4.96 \times 10^{-5}}{1.92 \times 10^{-9}} $$ **Step 5 — Numerator step-by-step:** $$ 12 \times 5000 = 60000 $$ $$ 60000 \times 0.080 = 4800 $$ $$ 4800 \times 4.96 \times 10^{-5} = 0.2381 $$ **Step 6 — Final division:** $$ \Delta P = \dfrac{0.2381}{1.92 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.24 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to bar:** $$ \Delta P = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{8}}{10^{5}} = 1240 \text{ bar} $$ **Step 8 — Engineering Comment:** This pressure drop is **unrealistically high** for normal food extrusion (operating range 20–150 bar). The die gap is too small, OR the viscosity assumption is too high. **For real food melts**, $\mu$ is typically **50–500 Pa·s** (shear-thinning), giving a more realistic $\Delta P$ of ~12–124 bar. The cubic dependence on $h$ is the dominant factor. **(Full 7 marks)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the 216 000 constant** in the SME formula — partial credit only. > 2. **Not converting $\dot{m}$ to kg/s** before plugging into SME formula. > 3. **Using shear-thinning (Power Law) viscosity** without stating it; KTU expects Newtonian unless explicitly asked. > 4. **Confusing die L/D with screw L/D** — these are two distinct design ratios. > 5. **In Part B answers, skipping the engineering interpretation** after calculation. KTU awards the last 1–2 marks only for valid industrial interpretation. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist - **Design = product-driven**: No universal extruder; screw, barrel, and die must match target food. - **Twin-screw co-rotating** dominates modern food extrusion due to its flexibility, self-wiping, and precise SME control. - **Single-screw extruders** are cheaper but limited — used for pasta, simple cereals, and crackers. - **SME (W·h/kg)** is the master design lever: low for pasta, high for TVP. - **L/D ratio** controls cook intensity and residence time distribution. - **Kneading disc stagger angle** controls shear intensity in the cooking zone. - **Reverse pitch elements** create melt seals and pressurization. - **Die L/D** is the key expansion controller: high for puffs, low for dense products. - **Feed moisture** inversely affects expansion: low moisture → high puff; high moisture → low puff, dense. - **Barrel temperature zoning** must rise from feed zone → die, with the die at the highest temperature for expanded snacks (and at the *lowest* for cooled TVP dies). - **Steam preconditioning** adds moisture and heat before the extruder → reduces SME demand → larger throughput. - **Cooling extruders** (jacket + screw cooling) are used for high-fat or heat-sensitive formulations. - **Throughput scales with $N$, $D^{2}$, and $H$** but is limited by SME constraints. - **Pressure drop across die** depends on $\mu$, $L$, and $1/h^{3}$ — cubic gap sensitivity is critical. - **Residence time** (typically 20–200 s) affects cook uniformity; CSTR-like twin-screw gives narrower RTD than single-screw. - **Safety:** All extruder dies must include **pressure relief rupture discs** rated at ≤ 110 % of design pressure. > **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Design and Operation for Different Food Applications** ✅

topics.title: Net Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Net Flow ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Net Flow (Q_net)** in an extruder is the *actual, measurable volumetric output rate* of polymer melt delivered at the die end, obtained by superposing (algebraically combining) the *forward conveying current* with the *opposing pressure current* and the *leakage currents* that develop inside the screw channel and the intermeshing zones. Mathematically, the universally accepted Newtonian form of the **characteristic extruder equation** is: $$Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; Q_{\text{drag}} \;-\; Q_{\text{pressure}} \;-\; Q_{\text{leak}}$$ The three components represent distinct physical mechanisms: | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Direction | |:------:|------------------|:---------:| | $Q_{\text{drag}}$ | Conveying current produced by the relative motion of barrel over the rotating screw channel | Forward (+) | | $Q_{\text{pressure}}$ | Back-flow current generated by the die-induced pressure gradient along the channel | Backward (−) | | $Q_{\text{leak}}$ | Parasitic current escaping through clearances between flight tips and barrel, and between intermeshing flights | Backward (−) | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Tank with Tap and Hole" Model Imagine a **bucket placed under a running tap (drag flow)**. The bucket has: - A small hole at the bottom through which water leaks out (leak flow). - A rising water level that pushes back against the inflow (pressure flow). The **net flow of water** you actually collect is: $$Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; \text{Tap inflow} \;-\; \text{Hole leakage} \;-\; \text{Back-pressure loss}$$ This is *exactly* how a polymer melt behaves inside the screw channel — the rotating barrel surface "drags" melt forward, but the die pressure pushes melt backward, and tiny clearances between screw and barrel allow melt to squirt backward as leakage. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):** Net Flow is the *cornerstone* for understanding extruder throughput, characteristic curves ($Q$ vs. $\Delta P$ / $Q$ vs. $N$), and die–screw matching. It appears in nearly every KTU examination question on extruder design. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used - **Screw geometry constants:** Pitch $S$, helix angle $\theta$, channel depth $H$, channel width $W$, screw diameter $D$, flight width $e$. - **Operating parameters:** Screw speed $N$ [rev/s], barrel surface velocity $v_b \;=\; \pi D N$, melt viscosity $\mu$ [Pa·s]. - **Pressure rise:** $\Delta P$ [Pa] built from die to feed end. All are bold constants in every subsequent derivation. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Reasoning The complete derivation of net flow proceeds in **four structured logical steps**: **Step 1 — Unwrap the screw channel.** The helical channel of a rotating screw is mathematically "unrolled" into a flat, shallow rectangular trough. The barrel surface becomes a moving plate gliding at velocity $v_b$ over a stationary plate (the screw root). This unlocks classical lubrication-theory (Couette–Poiseuille) superposition. **Step 2 — Identify the two superposed flows.** - **Couette flow (Drag):** The moving barrel drags the viscous melt forward. Its velocity profile is linear across depth. - **Poiseuille flow (Pressure):** The axial pressure gradient $dP/dz$ generated by the die pushes melt backward. Its velocity profile is parabolic across depth. **Step 3 — Superpose (linear additivity).** Because both flows are *laminar* and *steady*, the Navier–Stokes equations are linear in velocity → we may simply add the two volumetric flow rates. **Step 4 — Subtract leakage.** Add an empirical leakage term for the flight-tip clearance and (in twin-screws) the intermeshing-region clearances. ### 2.2 The Newtonian Assumptions Made For the closed-form formulas to hold, the following must be assumed: - **Isothermal** operation. - **Newtonian** melt viscosity (viscosity independent of shear rate). - **Fully filled, fully developed** channel flow. - **Wide channel**: $W \gg H$ (one-dimensional velocity profile). - **No slip** at metal walls. - **Steady state** ($\partial / \partial t \;=\; 0$). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula (Newtonian, wide channel) | Unit | |:-:|:---------|:----------------------------------|:----:| | 1 | Barrel linear velocity | $v_b \;=\; \pi D N$ | m/s | | 2 | Down-channel component | $v_{bz} \;=\; \pi D N \cos\theta$ | m/s | | 3 | **Drag flow rate** | $Q_{\text{drag}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,v_{bz}\,W\,H$ | m³/s | | 4 | Drag flow (expanded) | $Q_{\text{drag}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi D N \cos\theta \, W \, H$ | m³/s | | 5 | **Pressure flow rate** | $Q_{\text{pressure}} \;=\; \dfrac{W\,H^{3}\,\Delta P}{12\,\mu\,L}$ | m³/s | | 6 | **Flight-tip leak flow** | $Q_{\text{leak}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,\tan\theta\,e\,\Delta P}{12\,\mu\,L}$ | m³/s | | 7 | **Characteristic extruder equation** | $Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; Q_{\text{drag}} \;-\; Q_{\text{pressure}} \;-\; Q_{\text{leak}}$ | m³/s | | 8 | Compact form | $Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; \alpha\,N \;-\; \beta\,\dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu}$ | m³/s | | 9 | where | $\alpha \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi\,D\cos\theta\,W\,H$ | m³ | | 10 | and | $\beta \;=\; \tfrac{1}{12}\bigl[\,W H^{3} \;+\; \pi^{2} D^{2} \tan\theta\, e \,\bigr]$ | m³ | | 11 | Maximum output (open die) | $Q_{\max} \;=\; \alpha\,N$ | m³/s | | 12 | Maximum pressure (no output) | $P_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha\,\mu\,N}{\beta}$ | Pa | | 13 | Extruder constant (slope) | $\dfrac{dQ}{d(\Delta P)} \;=\; -\dfrac{\beta}{\mu}$ | m³/(s·Pa) | | 14 | Output at matched die | $Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha\,N}{1 \;+\; \beta/\beta_{d}}$ | m³/s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Trick (KTU Board-Exam Trick):** *"Drag pulls forward, Pressure pushes back, Leak slips sideways — net flow is the algebraic winner."* ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Throughput prediction** before building an extruder (saves lakhs of rupees in trials). - **Extruder–die matching**: equating $\beta/\mu \;=\; \beta_d/\mu_d$ gives the operating point on the $Q$–$\Delta P$ plane. - **Scale-up** from lab to production: $\alpha \propto D^{3}$, so throughput scales cubically with screw diameter. - **Twin-screw design**: the same net-flow framework is applied to the *C-shaped chamber* between intermeshing screws with additional leakage terms (calendar gap, side gap, tetrahedron gap). ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Drag Flow Rate ($Q_{\text{drag}}$) **Starting equation** — Navier–Stokes in the unrolled $z$-direction (down-channel), steady, fully developed, negligible inertia, no pressure gradient: $$\mu\,\frac{d^{2}v_{z}}{dy^{2}} \;=\; 0$$ Integrating once with respect to $y$ (channel depth coordinate, $y \;=\; 0$ at root, $y \;=\; H$ at barrel): $$\frac{dv_{z}}{dy} \;=\; C_{1}$$ Integrating a second time: $$v_{z}(y) \;=\; C_{1}\,y \;+\; C_{2}$$ Apply **no-slip boundary conditions**: - At root ($y \;=\; 0$): $v_z \;=\; 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2 \;=\; 0$ - At barrel ($y \;=\; H$): $v_z \;=\; v_{bz} \;\Rightarrow\; C_1 \;=\; v_{bz}/H$ Therefore the velocity profile is linear: $$v_{z}(y) \;=\; \frac{v_{bz}}{H}\,y$$ The volumetric flow rate per unit width is found by integrating across the depth: $$Q_{\text{drag per unit width}} \;=\; \int_{0}^{H} v_{z}(y)\,dy \;=\; \int_{0}^{H} \frac{v_{bz}}{H}\,y\,dy$$ $$Q_{\text{drag per unit width}} \;=\; \frac{v_{bz}}{H}\,\Bigl[\frac{y^{2}}{2}\Bigr]_{0}^{H} \;=\; \frac{v_{bz}\,H}{2}$$ Multiplying by channel width $W$ gives the **total drag flow**: $$Q_{\text{drag}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,v_{bz}\,W\,H$$ Substituting $v_{bz} \;=\; \pi D N \cos\theta$: $$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{drag}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi\,D\,N\,\cos\theta\,W\,H\,}$$ > *Conversion logic:* The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ appears because Couette flow has a *triangular* velocity profile — average velocity is half the wall velocity. ### 3.2 Derivation of Pressure Flow Rate ($Q_{\text{pressure}}$) **Starting equation** — Navier–Stokes with pressure gradient but no moving wall: $$\frac{dP}{dz} \;=\; \mu\,\frac{d^{2}v_{z}}{dy^{2}}$$ Define $G \;=\; -dP/dz$ (positive when pressure decreases down-channel). Then: $$\frac{d^{2}v_{z}}{dy^{2}} \;=\; -\frac{G}{\mu}$$ Integrating twice: $$v_{z}(y) \;=\; -\frac{G}{2\mu}\,y^{2} \;+\; C_{3}\,y \;+\; C_{4}$$ Apply **symmetry-free no-slip** boundary conditions: - At $y \;=\; 0$ (root): $v_z \;=\; 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_4 \;=\; 0$ - At $y \;=\; H$ (barrel): $v_z \;=\; 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_3 \;=\; \dfrac{G\,H}{2\mu}$ The velocity profile is **parabolic** (Poiseuille): $$v_{z}(y) \;=\; \frac{G}{2\mu}\,y\,(H - y)$$ The volumetric flow rate per unit width: $$Q_{\text{pressure per unit width}} \;=\; \int_{0}^{H} v_{z}(y)\,dy \;=\; \frac{G}{2\mu}\,\int_{0}^{H}(H y - y^{2})\,dy$$ $$Q_{\text{pressure per unit width}} \;=\; \frac{G}{2\mu}\,\Bigl[\tfrac{H y^{2}}{2} - \tfrac{y^{3}}{3}\Bigr]_{0}^{H} \;=\; \frac{G}{2\mu}\,\Bigl(\tfrac{H^{3}}{2} - \tfrac{H^{3}}{3}\Bigr) \;=\; \frac{G\,H^{3}}{12\,\mu}$$ Multiplying by width $W$ and substituting $G \;=\; \Delta P / L$ (pressure rise over channel length): $$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{pressure}} \;=\; \dfrac{W\,H^{3}\,\Delta P}{12\,\mu\,L}\,}$$ > *Conversion logic:* The factor $\tfrac{1}{12}$ arises because Poiseuille flow in a rectangular channel of aspect ratio $W/H \gg 1$ collapses to the parallel-plate result; finite $W/H$ would give $\tfrac{1}{12}\bigl[1 - \tfrac{192 H}{\pi^{5} W}\tanh(\pi W/2H)\bigr]$. ### 3.3 Derivation of Flight-Tip Leak Flow Rate ($Q_{\text{leak}}$) The clearance $\epsilon$ between flight tip and barrel acts as a *narrow rectangular slot* of length $L$, width $\pi D \tan\theta$ (the helical flight-tip trace), and gap $\epsilon$. Applying the same Poiseuille logic: $$Q_{\text{leak}} \;=\; \frac{(\pi D \tan\theta)\,\epsilon^{3}\,\Delta P}{12\,\mu\,L}$$ A standard correction factor accounting for the *finite flight width* $e$: $$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{leak}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,\tan\theta\,e\,\Delta P}{12\,\mu\,L}\,}$$ > *Conversion logic:* The flight-tip trace forms a closed helical loop of length $\approx \pi D \tan\theta$. With slot depth $\epsilon$ and slot width $e$, the effective discharge area scales as $e\,\epsilon^{3}$. ### 3.4 Assembling the Net Flow (Characteristic Equation) By **linear superposition** of the three currents: $$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi D N\cos\theta\,W H}_{Q_{\text{drag}}} \;-\; \underbrace{\tfrac{W H^{3}\Delta P}{12\mu L}}_{Q_{\text{pressure}}} \;-\; \underbrace{\tfrac{\pi^{2}D^{2}\tan\theta\,e\,\Delta P}{12\mu L}}_{Q_{\text{leak}}}\,}$$ Or in compact geometric form: $$Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; \alpha\,N \;-\; \beta\,\frac{\Delta P}{\mu}$$ with the **extruder constants**: $$\alpha \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi\,D\cos\theta\,W\,H$$ $$\beta \;=\; \tfrac{1}{12}\bigl[W H^{3} \;+\; \pi^{2} D^{2}\tan\theta\,e\bigr]$$ ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Cases (High-Yield for KTU) | Operating Condition | $\Delta P$ | Resulting $Q_{\text{net}}$ | Engineering Meaning | |:--------------------|:----------:|:--------------------------:|:--------------------| | **Open die** (no resistance) | $0$ | $Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; \alpha N \;=\; Q_{\max}$ | Maximum throughput | | **Closed die** (plugged) | $P_{\max}$ | $Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; 0$ | Pressure builds to limit | | **Matched die** | $\Delta P^{*}$ | $Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; \dfrac{\alpha N}{1 + \beta/\beta_d}$ | Operating point | | **Leak-only flow** (no screw rotation) | $\Delta P$ | $Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; -\beta \Delta P/\mu$ | Pure backflow | > [!NOTE] > **Conversion logic for matched die:** Equating $\Delta P_{\text{screw}} \;=\; \Delta P_{\text{die}}$ and using $Q_{\text{die}} \;=\; \beta_d \Delta P / \mu_d$ yields $Q_{\text{net}}$ as listed. KTU board questions love this form. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Net Flow Composition The following Mermaid diagram shows how the three currents combine to produce the net output delivered at the die: ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["ROTATING SCREW + BARREL
Relative motion at v_b"]:::input --> nodeB["DRAG FLOW Q_drag
Forward conveying current
Couette flow component"]:::forward nodeC["DIE RESISTANCE
Pressure rise Delta P"]:::input --> nodeD["PRESSURE FLOW Q_pressure
Backward Poiseuille current
Opposes drag"]:::backward nodeE["FLIGHT TIP CLEARANCE epsilon
+ Intermeshing gaps"]:::input --> nodeF["LEAK FLOW Q_leak
Parasitic backward current"]:::backward nodeB --> nodeG["ALGEBRAIC SUPERPOSITION
Linear Couette plus Poiseuille"]:::sum nodeD --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH["NET FLOW Q_net
Actual melt throughput at die
Q_net = Q_drag minus Q_pressure minus Q_leak"]:::output classDef input fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef forward fill:#B5E7A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef backward fill:#F8B4B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef sum fill:#C4D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#9C6BFF,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` **How to read this chart:** - Yellow boxes = physical drivers (cause). - Green box = forward current (positive). - Pink boxes = backward currents (negative). - Blue box = linear superposition node. - Purple box = final output $Q_{\text{net}}$. ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Twin-Screw Net Flow For an intermeshing co-rotating twin-screw extruder, the net flow framework expands. Below is the block architecture showing all contributing sub-flows (this is the Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow mapping, used because a physical twin-screw schematic in pure text is infeasible). ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1["INPUT ENERGY"] nodeS1a["SCREW ROTATION
N rev per second"]:::drv nodeS1b["DIE PRESSURE RISE
Delta P"]:::drv nodeS1c["MELT VISCOSITY mu
Temperature dependent"]:::drv end subgraph S2["C-SHAPED CHAMBER FLOW"] nodeS2a["POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT
Forward flow Q_C
Half of N V_C per revolution"]:::fwd nodeS2b["CALENDAR GAP LEAK
Flight tip to barrel
Q_cal"]:::bwd nodeS2c["SIDE GAP LEAK
Between flight flanks
Q_side"]:::bwd nodeS2d["TETRAHEDRON LEAK
Intermeshing region
Q_tet"]:::bwd end subgraph S3["SUPERPOSITION"] nodeS3a["Q_net = Q_C minus Q_cal minus Q_side minus Q_tet"]:::sum end subgraph S4["ENGINEERING OUTPUT"] nodeS4a["OPERATING POINT
on Q versus Delta P chart"]:::out nodeS4b["THROUGHPUT
kg per hour"]:::out nodeS4c["SPECIFIC ENERGY
kWh per kg"]:::out end nodeS1a --> nodeS2a nodeS1b --> nodeS2b nodeS1b --> nodeS2c nodeS1b --> nodeS2d nodeS1c --> nodeS2b nodeS1c --> nodeS2c nodeS1c --> nodeS2d nodeS2a --> nodeS3a nodeS2b --> nodeS3a nodeS2c --> nodeS3a nodeS2d --> nodeS3a nodeS3a --> nodeS4a nodeS3a --> nodeS4b nodeS3a --> nodeS4c classDef drv fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef fwd fill:#B5E7A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef bwd fill:#F8B4B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef sum fill:#C4D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#9C6BFF,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` **Reading guide for the twin-screw architecture:** - **Driver layer (yellow):** rotational speed, die back-pressure, viscosity. - **C-shaped chamber layer (green & pink):** the *positive displacement* forward flow vs. the *three leakage streams* (calendar, side, tetrahedron). - **Superposition (blue):** the algebraic sum. - **Output layer (purple):** the design-relevant deliverables — operating point, throughput, specific energy. ### 4.3 Schematic — Unrolled Channel Velocity Profiles ``` BARREL SURFACE (moving at v_bz) =================================== y = H \ / \ PARABOLA / <- Pressure flow (backward at y = 0) \ ___________/ TRIANGLE\ / \ Linear profile from v_bz at top PROFILE \/ \ 0 to 0 at root. ---> | \ | \ 0 =================================== y = 0 (screw root, stationary) v_z(y) ``` This shows that **net velocity** is the *superposed* triangle (drag) minus the parabola (pressure). ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** Define **Net Flow** in an extruder. List the three components that contribute to it and state the direction of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Net flow is the actual volumetric output rate of polymer melt delivered at the die end of an extruder, obtained by algebraic superposition of three independent currents in the screw channel. > > | Component | Direction | Physical Origin | > |:---------:|:---------:|:----------------| > | Drag flow $Q_{\text{drag}}$ | Forward (+) | Relative motion of barrel over screw | > | Pressure flow $Q_{\text{pressure}}$ | Backward (−) | Pressure gradient created by the die | > | Leak flow $Q_{\text{leak}}$ | Backward (−) | Flight-tip and intermeshing clearances | > > The characteristic extruder equation is: > > $$Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; Q_{\text{drag}} \;-\; Q_{\text{pressure}} \;-\; Q_{\text{leak}}$$ *(Marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for listing components + 1 mark for equation with directions.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** Write the **Newtonian expression for drag flow** in a single-screw extruder and explain why the numerical prefactor is **½**. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The drag flow rate for a Newtonian melt in a wide rectangular channel is: > > $$Q_{\text{drag}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi\,D\,N\,\cos\theta\,W\,H$$ > > The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ arises because the Couette velocity profile generated by the moving barrel is **linear** across the channel depth: > > $$v_{z}(y) \;=\; \frac{v_{bz}}{H}\,y$$ > > Integrating this triangular profile from $y \;=\; 0$ to $y \;=\; H$ yields the *average* velocity as $v_{bz}/2$. Multiplying by the cross-sectional area $W \cdot H$ gives the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ prefactor. *(Marks: 1 mark for equation + 1 mark for linear profile statement + 1 mark for integration reasoning.)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > KTU ESE Module Pattern: **Choice between Q(A) and Q(B). Each carries 7 + 7 marks.** --- ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the necessary assumptions, derive the expression for **net volumetric flow rate** in a single-screw extruder handling a **Newtonian** polymer melt. Clearly identify each term. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 60 mm diameter single-screw extruder operates at 60 rpm with a 30 mm pitch. The metering channel depth is 3 mm, the channel width is 50 mm, the flight width is 4 mm, and the melt viscosity is 1000 Pa·s. Compute $Q_{\text{drag}}$, $Q_{\text{pressure}}$, $Q_{\text{leak}}$, and $Q_{\text{net}}$ if the die pressure rise is $5 \times 10^{6}$ Pa. Channel length $L \;=\; 1$ m. Use $g \;=\; \pi$. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** *Step 1 — Unroll the screw channel.* The helical channel becomes a flat shallow rectangular trough of width $W$ and depth $H$. Barrel moves at $v_{bz} \;=\; \pi D N \cos\theta$ over a stationary root. *Step 2 — Couette (drag) component.* Solving $\mu\,\dfrac{d^{2}v_z}{dy^{2}} \;=\; 0$ with $v_z(0) \;=\; 0$ and $v_z(H) \;=\; v_{bz}$: $$v_z^{\text{drag}}(y) \;=\; \frac{v_{bz}}{H}\,y \;\Rightarrow\; Q_{\text{drag}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,v_{bz}\,W\,H$$ *Step 3 — Poiseuille (pressure) component.* Solving $\dfrac{dP}{dz} \;=\; \mu\,\dfrac{d^{2}v_z}{dy^{2}}$ with $v_z(0) \;=\; v_z(H) \;=\; 0$: $$v_z^{\text{press}}(y) \;=\; \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\,y(H-y) \;\Rightarrow\; Q_{\text{pressure}} \;=\; \dfrac{W H^{3}\Delta P}{12\mu L}$$ *Step 4 — Leakage.* Slot flow through flight-tip clearance $\epsilon$ of width $e$: $$Q_{\text{leak}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2}\tan\theta\,e\,\Delta P}{12\mu L}$$ *Step 5 — Superpose:* $$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\pi D N\cos\theta\,W H \;-\; \dfrac{W H^{3}\Delta P}{12\mu L} \;-\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}D^{2}\tan\theta\,e\,\Delta P}{12\mu L}\,}$$ *(1 mark each for the three component derivations; 1 mark for assembly of net-flow equation.)* **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** Given: $D \;=\; 0.060$ m, $N \;=\; 60/60 \;=\; 1$ rev/s, pitch $S \;=\; 0.030$ m, $H \;=\; 0.003$ m, $W \;=\; 0.050$ m, $e \;=\; 0.004$ m, $\mu \;=\; 1000$ Pa·s, $\Delta P \;=\; 5\times10^{6}$ Pa, $L \;=\; 1$ m. Helix angle: $$\tan\theta \;=\; \frac{S}{\pi D} \;=\; \frac{0.030}{\pi(0.060)} \;=\; \frac{0.030}{0.1885} \;=\; 0.15915$$ $$\theta \;=\; 9.04^{\circ}, \quad \cos\theta \;=\; 0.9876$$ **Compute $Q_{\text{drag}}$:** $$Q_{\text{drag}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi\,(0.060)(1)(0.9876)(0.050)(0.003)$$ Step by step: - $\pi \times 0.060 \;=\; 0.1885$ - $\times 1 \;=\; 0.1885$ - $\times 0.9876 \;=\; 0.1862$ - $\times 0.050 \;=\; 0.00931$ - $\times 0.003 \;=\; 2.79 \times 10^{-5}$ - $\times \tfrac{1}{2} \;=\; 1.396 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s $$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{drag}} \;=\; 1.396 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{s} \;\approx\; 14.0\;\text{cm}^{3}\!/\text{s}\,}$$ **Compute $Q_{\text{pressure}}$:** $$Q_{\text{pressure}} \;=\; \frac{(0.050)(0.003)^{3}(5\times10^{6})}{12(1000)(1)}$$ Step by step: - $(0.003)^{3} \;=\; 2.7 \times 10^{-8}$ - $\times 0.050 \;=\; 1.35 \times 10^{-9}$ - $\times 5\times10^{6} \;=\; 6.75 \times 10^{-3}$ - $/(12 \times 1000) \;=\; /\,12000 \;=\; 5.625 \times 10^{-7}$ m³/s $$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{pressure}} \;=\; 5.625 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{s}\,}$$ **Compute $Q_{\text{leak}}$:** $$Q_{\text{leak}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}(0.060)^{2}(0.15915)(0.004)(5\times10^{6})}{12(1000)(1)}$$ Step by step: - $\pi^{2} \;=\; 9.8696$ - $\times (0.060)^{2} \;=\; \times 0.0036 \;=\; 0.03553$ - $\times 0.15915 \;=\; 5.655 \times 10^{-3}$ - $\times 0.004 \;=\; 2.262 \times 10^{-5}$ - $\times 5\times10^{6} \;=\; 113.1$ - $/12000 \;=\; 9.425 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s $$\boxed{\,Q_{\text{leak}} \;=\; 9.43 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{s}\,}$$ **Compute $Q_{\text{net}}$:** $$Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; 1.396 \times 10^{-5} \;-\; 5.625 \times 10^{-7} \;-\; 9.43 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; 1.340 \times 10^{-5} \;-\; 9.43 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; -9.416 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{s}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Negative Net Flow!** This negative value indicates the **die is grossly oversized** for this screw. The pressure flow + leak flow exceed the drag flow, meaning **melt flows backward out of the feed throat**. In practice the screw would *flood* (could not pump against the die). Engineering remedy: (i) raise $N$, (ii) reduce $\Delta P$ with a smaller die, or (iii) increase $H$ to amplify drag flow. (1 mark for interpreting the negative result correctly.) *(Marks: 1 mark each for $Q_{\text{drag}}$, $Q_{\text{pressure}}$, $Q_{\text{leak}}$, $Q_{\text{net}}$; 1 mark for computing $\theta$; 2 marks for the engineering interpretation.)* --- ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **characteristic extruder equation** $Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; \alpha N - \beta\,(\Delta P/\mu)$. Sketch and explain the **operating characteristic line** of an extruder on a $Q$–$\Delta P$ chart. Define the operating point when coupled with a die characteristic. **(b) [7 Marks]** For a single-screw extruder, calculate the **maximum output** $Q_{\max}$ and the **maximum pressure** $P_{\max}$. Comment on how these parameters scale with screw diameter $D$ for scale-up. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Characteristic line (7 marks):** The characteristic equation rewritten in compact form: $$Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; \alpha\,N \;-\; \beta\,\frac{\Delta P}{\mu}$$ This is the equation of a **straight line** in the $Q$–$\Delta P$ plane with: - **$y$-intercept** (when $\Delta P \;=\; 0$) = $\alpha N$ = $Q_{\max}$ (open-die throughput). - **Slope** = $-\beta/\mu$ (negative; the deeper the die restriction, the lower the throughput). - **$x$-intercept** (when $Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; 0$) = $\alpha N \mu / \beta$ = $P_{\max}$ (closed-die pressure). The **die characteristic line** also passes through the origin with slope $\beta_d/\mu_d$: $$Q_{\text{die}} \;=\; \beta_{d}\,\frac{\Delta P}{\mu_{d}}$$ The **operating point** is the *intersection* of the extruder line and the die line. ``` Q (m^3/s) | Q_max •(open die)____________ | /| | / | EXTRUDER LINE |/ | slope = -beta/mu •---|-------------------------> Delta P /| P_op X <-- operating point / | /| / | / | DIE LINE / | / | slope = +beta_d/mu_d ____|____/___|____________ | / | / P_max (closed die) |/ ``` *(3 marks for the line equation interpretation; 2 marks for sketch; 2 marks for the die–extruder intersection operating point.)* **Part (b) — Max output and max pressure (7 marks):** **Maximum output (open die):** $$Q_{\max} \;=\; \alpha N \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi\,D\cos\theta\,W\,H\,N$$ For a *geometrically similar* screw, $W \propto D$ and $H \propto D$, so: $$Q_{\max} \;\propto\; D^{3}\,N$$ **Maximum pressure (closed die):** $$P_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\alpha\,\mu\,N}{\beta} \;=\; \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi D\cos\theta\,W\,H\,\mu\,N}{\tfrac{1}{12}\bigl[W H^{3} + \pi^{2}D^{2}\tan\theta\,e\bigr]}$$ For geometric similarity, both numerator and denominator scale as $D^{3}$, hence: $$P_{\max} \;\propto\; \mu\,N \quad \text{(independent of } D \text{ at geometric similarity)}$$ **Numerical illustration:** Using Part (b) of Question A's data: $$\alpha \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi\,(0.060)(0.9876)(0.050)(0.003) \;=\; 1.396 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}^{3}$$ $$\beta \;=\; \tfrac{1}{12}\bigl[(0.050)(0.003)^{3} + \pi^{2}(0.060)^{2}(0.15915)(0.004)\bigr]$$ Numerator terms: - $(0.050)(2.7\times10^{-8}) \;=\; 1.35 \times 10^{-9}$ - $\pi^{2}(0.0036)(0.15915)(0.004) \;=\; 9.8696 \times 0.0036 \times 0.15915 \times 0.004 \;=\; 2.262 \times 10^{-5}$ Sum = $2.262 \times 10^{-5} + 1.35 \times 10^{-9} \approx 2.262 \times 10^{-5}$ $$\beta \;=\; \frac{2.262 \times 10^{-5}}{12} \;=\; 1.885 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^{3}$$ $$Q_{\max} \;=\; \alpha\,N \;=\; 1.396 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}^{3}\!/\text{s}$$ $$P_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\alpha\,\mu\,N}{\beta} \;=\; \frac{(1.396\times10^{-5})(1000)(1)}{1.885\times10^{-6}} \;=\; 7.41 \times 10^{6}\;\text{Pa} \;\approx\; 7.4\;\text{MPa}$$ > **Scale-up comment (2 marks):** $Q_{\max}$ scales with $D^{3}$, so doubling screw diameter **multiplies throughput 8×**. However $P_{\max}$ stays constant at geometric similarity — meaning the *same* die pressure is required for the same relative operating point. *(Marks: 2 marks each for $Q_{\max}$ and $P_{\max}$ formula + value; 2 marks for scaling rule; 1 mark for engineering comment.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Net-Flow Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the leak term** — KTU's Module 2 specifically tests the *three-term* form; many students write only $Q_d - Q_p$ and lose 2 marks. > 2. **Sign confusion** — Pressure and leak flow *subtract*; writing $Q_d + Q_p + Q_L$ is wrong (zero on characteristic equation). > 3. **Using $v_b$ instead of $v_{bz}$** — always project barrel velocity onto the channel direction using $\cos\theta$. > 4. **Helix-angle calculation** — using $S = \pi D$ (square-pitched) when the problem specifies a different pitch. > 5. **Units mismatch** — converting $\Delta P$ from MPa to Pa in numericals. **Always use SI units (Pa, m, s)**. > 6. **Forgetting $\tfrac{1}{2}$ prefactor in drag flow** — this is the most common single error; remember the triangular Couette profile. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Net flow = Drag − Pressure − Leak** — the *defining* equation for every extrusion problem. - **Drag flow** is **forward**, linear velocity profile, prefactor **½**. - **Pressure flow** is **backward**, parabolic profile, prefactor **1/12** (wide channel). - **Leak flow** is **backward**, slot-flow through flight clearance, scales with $\epsilon^{3}$ — keep clearances tight! - **Characteristic equation** $Q_{\text{net}} = \alpha N - \beta(\Delta P/\mu)$ has intercept $Q_{\max}$ and slope $-\beta/\mu$. - **Operating point** = intersection of **extruder line** (negative slope) and **die line** (positive slope). - **Scale-up**: $Q \propto D^{3}$, $P_{\max}$ independent of $D$ at geometric similarity. - **Twin-screw extension**: $Q_{\text{net}} = Q_C - Q_{\text{calendar}} - Q_{\text{side}} - Q_{\text{tetrahedron}}$ — *four terms*, not three. - **For Newtonian melts** the formulas are closed-form; for *non-Newtonian* (power-law, Carreau) melts, $\mu$ is replaced by an effective viscosity and the prefactors change — beyond KTU Module 2 scope. - **Always** write the **direction** of each flow explicitly — KTU examiners reward it with bonus clarity marks.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Operations in Advanced Extrusion Technology

In the context of **Advanced Extrusion Technology**, the term **"Operations"** refers to the specialized, value-added processing configurations and downstream variants of the standard single-screw extrusion process. While Module 1 typically covers the fundamentals of screw design, melting mechanism, and metering zones, Module 2 advances into the *engineered operations* that transform a generic extruder into a high-performance manufacturing unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> Extrusion operations are defined as the engineered configurations of conveying, melting, mixing, reacting, and shaping systems — often involving multi-screw, multi-layer, or chemically active melt streams — designed to produce specific morphologies, structures, or product forms that cannot be achieved by conventional single-screw extrusion.

The principal operations studied under this module include:

1. **Co-extrusion** – simultaneous extrusion of two or more polymers through a single die to form a multi-layered product.
2. **Reactive Extrusion (REX)** – execution of chemical reactions (polymerization, grafting, crosslinking, degradation) inside the extruder barrel.
3. **Foam Extrusion** – incorporation of a blowing agent to generate a cellular polymer structure.
4. **Film & Sheet Extrusion Operations** – flattening of the extrudate through chill rolls or air cooling for thin-gauge products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme treats these four operations as the cornerstone of Module 2. Mastery of their underlying principles, equipment, and process–structure–property relationships is **mandatory** for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **chocolate fountain with multiple colored streams merging into one flowing curtain**. The chocolate pumps (extruders) feed individual colors (polymers) into a common manifold (die), and the curtain that emerges is layered — this is **co-extrusion**.

Now picture the same fountain, but imagine a baker is **continuously adding cocoa powder and sugar at a precise point inside the pipe**, while the chocolate keeps flowing. The chocolate changes composition as it travels. This dynamic *in-situ modification* mirrors **reactive extrusion**.

For **foam extrusion**, think of a soda bottle being opened — dissolved gas escapes and forms bubbles, creating a light, cellular mass. In the extruder, a chemical or physical blowing agent plays the role of the dissolved gas.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** All advanced extrusion operations share one unifying idea — **controlling what happens to the polymer *during* its journey through the extruder**, not just *before* or *after* it. Conventional extrusion is a passive shaping process; advanced operations are *active* engineering processes.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics and Standard References

The following baseline parameters are universally applied across extrusion operations and form the language of the subject:

- **Melt Temperature ($T_m$)** — typically 160 °C to 320 °C for thermoplastics.
- **Specific Energy Input (SEI)** — expressed in **kWh/kg** of polymer processed.
- **Throughput ($Q$)** — mass flow rate in **kg/h**.
- **Apparent Shear Rate ($\dot{\gamma}$)** — in **s⁻¹**.
- **Pressure Gradient ($\partial P / \partial z$)** — in **Pa/m**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often confuse "Operation" with "Process." A *process* is the broad manufacturing sequence (raw material → finished product). An *operation* is a specific, well-defined unit of transformation within that process. In Module 2, we deal exclusively with the latter.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Co-Extrusion

Co-extrusion is the simultaneous extrusion of **two or more polymeric melts** through a **single die** to form an integrated multi-layer structure. The number of layers can range from **2 (AB)** to **5, 7, or even more** in advanced systems, with each layer performing a specific function (barrier, adhesive, structural, aesthetic).

### 2.1.1 Working Principle
Each polymer is melted in its **dedicated extruder**, metered by individual gear pumps, and then channeled through a **feedblock** or **multi-manifold die** where the layers are stacked. The combined melt stream then flows as a unified structure.

### 2.1.2 Types of Co-extrusion Dies

| Die Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedblock** | Polymers merged upstream of a single-manifold die | Flat films, sheets |
| **Multi-manifold** | Each polymer has its own manifold, merged near the die exit | Wide sheets, pipes, asymmetric layers |
| **Vane-type** | Stream is repeatedly split and recombined | Micro-layering (24, 48, 96+ layers) |

### 2.1.3 Critical Parameters
- **Viscosity ratio ($\eta_1 / \eta_2$)** between adjacent layers must lie between **0.5 and 2.0** to avoid layer encapsulation or non-uniformity.
- **Interfacial instability** (sometimes called "layer breakup") is governed by the **capillary number** and shear stress balance at the polymer–polymer interface.
- **Layer thickness distribution** is set by the relative flow rates $Q_1, Q_2, \ldots, Q_n$ of each extruder.

### 2.1.4 Advantages
- Combines properties of incompatible polymers in one product.
- Reduces material cost by using a **cheaper core layer** with thin functional skin layers.
- Eliminates secondary lamination steps.

---

## 2.2 Reactive Extrusion (REX)

Reactive Extrusion converts the extruder into a **continuous chemical reactor**. The polymer is melted, conveyed, and simultaneously subjected to a chemical transformation.

### 2.2.1 Types of Reactions Performed

| Reaction Type | Example | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Grafting** | Maleic anhydride grafting onto PP | Compatibilizer for PP/nylon blends |
| **Polymerization** | Anionic ring-opening of polyamides | Engineering plastics |
| **Crosslinking** | Peroxide-induced XLPE for wire insulation | Cable sheathing |
| **Degradation / Controlled Scission** | PP degradation with peroxides | MFI adjustment |
| **Copolymerization** | EVA synthesis in twin-screw | Flexible films |
| **Hydrolysis / Alcoholysis** | PET glycolysis | Recycling streams |

### 2.2.2 Equipment Choice
**Intermeshing co-rotating twin-screw extruders** are almost exclusively used because they offer:
- High surface renewal and **self-wiping** action.
- Independent control of residence time and intensity.
- Efficient **degassing** of volatile by-products.

### 2.2.3 Key Performance Indicators

- **Residence Time Distribution (RTD)** — characterized by the Péclet number.
- **Degree of Conversion ($X$)** — fraction of limiting reactant consumed.
- **Damköhler Number ($Da$)** — ratio of reaction rate to convective transport rate.

---

## 2.3 Foam Extrusion

Foam extrusion produces a **cellular polymeric structure** by dissolving a blowing agent into the melt, then triggering a rapid pressure or temperature drop to nucleate and grow cells.

### 2.3.1 Blowing Agent Classification

| Type | Examples | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical (CBA)** | Azodicarbonamide, sodium bicarbonate | Thermal decomposition releases gas |
| **Physical (PBA)** | CO₂, N₂, butane, HFC | Phase change from liquid/supercritical to gas |
| **Endothermic CBA** | Citric acid + sodium bicarbonate | Heat absorption during decomposition |

### 2.3.2 Three-Stage Foam Formation Model
1. **Nucleation** — supersaturation creates cell nuclei (homogeneous or heterogeneous).
2. **Growth** — gas diffuses into nuclei, cells expand.
3. **Stabilization** — melt cools and viscosity rises, freezing the cellular structure.

### 2.3.3 Key Performance Indicators
- **Expansion Ratio ($\phi$)** — ratio of foamed density to solid density.
- **Cell Density ($N_0$)** — number of cells per cm³ of foam.
- **Open-cell vs. closed-cell content** — critical for cushioning (open) versus insulation (closed).

---

## 2.4 Film and Sheet Extrusion

Although appearing simple, this operation requires precise **die lip adjustment**, **chill-roll temperature control**, and **haul-off synchronization** to produce films of uniform gauge.

### 2.4.1 Cast Film (Chill Roll) Process
Melt exits a **T-die** and is quenched on a **chill roll** rotating at controlled speed $V_{haul}$. The draw-down ratio is defined as:

$$ \text{DDR} = \frac{V_{haul}}{V_{die\,exit}} $$

A typical DDR lies between **5:1 and 30:1**.

### 2.4.2 Blown Film Process
Melt is extruded vertically through an **annular die**, inflated by internal air, and collapsed by a **tent frame** before being wound. The **blow-up ratio (BUR)** is the key parameter:

$$ \text{BUR} = \frac{D_{bubble}}{D_{die}} $$

Standard BUR values range from **1.5:1 to 4:1**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory for KTU Board Examination:** The following equations are repeatedly tested in numerical and short-answer questions.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \alpha N - \dfrac{\beta \, P}{\eta}$ | Throughput of extruder (positive-die characteristics) | kg/h |
| 2 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{h}$ | Apparent shear rate in metering channel | s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Power-law viscosity (Ostwald–de Waele model) | Pa·s |
| 4 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \eta L Q}{A h^3}$ | Pressure drop in a rectangular slit (Poiseuille flow) | Pa |
| 5 | $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, Q \, N^2 D^2 \, C_s$ | Power required by the extruder | W |
| 6 | $\phi = \dfrac{\rho_{solid}}{\rho_{foam}}$ | Foam expansion ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $N_0 = \dfrac{N_f}{\phi}$ | Cell density correction for expansion | cells/cm³ |
| 8 | $Da = k \, \tau_{res}$ | Damköhler number for REX | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{DDR} = \dfrac{V_{haul}}{V_{die}}$ | Draw-down ratio in cast film | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_{bubble}}{D_{die}}$ | Blow-up ratio in blown film | dimensionless |
| 11 | $L_{layer\,i} = L_{total} \times \dfrac{Q_i}{\sum Q_j}$ | Layer thickness in co-extrusion | m |
| 12 | $SEI = \dfrac{P}{Q_{mass}}$ | Specific Energy Input | kWh/kg |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by process engineers daily to size motors, select screws, predict cooling loads, and design multilayer packaging. The same equations appear in CFD simulation software (e.g., Polyflow, OpenFOAM) as constitutive inputs.
