# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Screw Throughput Equation (Newtonian Melt in Metering Zone)

The extruder throughput $Q$ in the metering zone of a single-screw extruder, treating the melt as Newtonian, can be derived by combining **drag flow** and **pressure flow** contributions.

### Step 1 — Define the Geometry
Let the metering channel have:
- Depth $h$ (perpendicular to barrel wall)
- Width $W$ (along the flight land)
- Helix angle $\theta$
- Screw radius $R$, root radius $R_i$, so $D = 2R$ and $e = R - R_i$

### Step 2 — Drag Flow Component
The barrel wall moves relative to the channel at velocity $V_b = \pi D N \cos\theta$. Unwinding the channel along the helix, this becomes a Couette flow with shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_{drag} = V_b / h$.

The volumetric drag flow rate is:

$$ Q_d = \frac{1}{2} \, V_b \, W \, h = \frac{1}{2} \, (\pi D N \cos\theta) \, W \, h $$

### Step 3 — Pressure Flow Component
Superimpose the Poiseuille flow driven by the pressure gradient $\partial P / \partial z$ along the channel length $L_m$:

$$ Q_p = -\frac{W h^3}{12 \eta} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} $$

The total pressure difference across the metering zone is $P$. Using $L_m$ as the axial length and noting that $\partial z = L_m$, we write $Q_p$ as:

$$ Q_p = -\frac{W h^3 \, P}{12 \eta \, L_m} $$

### Step 4 — Combine Contributions
The net throughput is the algebraic sum:

$$ Q = Q_d + Q_p $$

$$ Q = \frac{1}{2} \, \pi D N \cos\theta \, W \, h \; - \; \frac{W h^3 \, P}{12 \eta \, L_m} $$

### Step 5 — Group the Constants
Defining the geometric constants:

$$ \alpha = \frac{1}{2} \, \pi D \cos\theta \, W \, h \quad \text{and} \quad \beta = \frac{W h^3}{12 L_m} $$

The final compact form of the **screw characteristic equation** becomes:

$$ \boxed{ Q = \alpha N \; - \; \frac{\beta \, P}{\eta} } $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the same equation appearing in the Formula Sheet (Row 1). The **negative sign** indicates that an opposing die pressure reduces the throughput. KTU examiners frequently test this derivation for full 7-mark questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Layer Thickness in Co-extrusion (Feedblock Model)

Consider an **A/B/A** (skin/core/skin) co-extrusion with three layers. The total flow rate entering the feedblock is:

$$ Q_{total} = Q_A + Q_B + Q_A = 2Q_A + Q_B $$

After leaving the feedblock, the layer thickness is directly proportional to the flow rate of that layer (assuming equal channel width and density).

### Step 1 — Skin Layer Thickness

$$ L_A = L_{total} \times \frac{Q_A}{Q_{total}} $$

### Step 2 — Core Layer Thickness

$$ L_B = L_{total} \times \frac{Q_B}{Q_{total}} $$

### Step 3 — Combine

Substituting $Q_{total} = 2Q_A + Q_B$:

$$ L_B = L_{total} \times \frac{Q_B}{2 Q_A + Q_B} $$

$$ L_A = L_{total} \times \frac{Q_A}{2 Q_A + Q_B} $$

### Step 4 — Special Case (Equal Volumes of Skin and Core)
When $Q_A = Q_B$:

$$ L_A = \frac{L_{total}}{3}, \quad L_B = \frac{L_{total}}{3} $$

Wait — let me recompute. With $Q_A = Q_B$ and total $Q = 2Q_A + Q_B = 3Q_A$:

$$ L_A = L_{total} \times \frac{Q_A}{3 Q_A} = \frac{L_{total}}{3} $$

$$ L_B = L_{total} \times \frac{Q_A}{3 Q_A} = \frac{L_{total}}{3} $$

Total skin contribution: $2 \times L_{total}/3 = 2L_{total}/3$ ✓  
Core contribution: $L_{total}/3$ ✓  
Sum: $L_{total}$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Foam Expansion Ratio in Physical Blowing Agent Extrusion

The **expansion ratio** $\phi$ is defined as the ratio of the solid (unfoamed) polymer density $\rho_s$ to the foamed polymer density $\rho_f$.

### Step 1 — Mass Conservation
For a fixed mass of polymer $m$:

$$ m = \rho_s V_s = \rho_f V_f $$

### Step 2 — Definition of Expansion Ratio

$$ \phi = \frac{V_f}{V_s} = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_f} $$

### Step 3 — Express in Terms of Blowing Agent Loading
If $m_g$ grams of gas dissolves in mass $m_p$ of polymer, the foamed volume includes the gas volume $V_g$ (ideal gas) at the foaming conditions:

$$ V_g = \frac{n_g R T}{P} = \frac{m_g}{M_g} \cdot \frac{R T}{P} $$

The total foam volume is $V_f = V_s + V_g$ (assuming gas escapes isothermally and isothermally), giving:

$$ \phi = 1 + \frac{V_g}{V_s} = 1 + \frac{\rho_s \, m_g \, R T}{M_g \, m_p \, P} $$

> [!TIP]
> This last form is **excellent for KTU numericals** because it directly links the *amount of blowing agent dosed* to the *final foam density* — a typical 7-mark question pattern.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Operation

| Criterion | Co-extrusion | Reactive Extrusion | Foam Extrusion | Film / Sheet |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary equipment** | Multiple extruders + die | Twin-screw extruder | Single/twin-screw with cooling | Single-screw + die |
| **Number of polymer streams** | ≥ 2 | 1 (+ reagents) | 1 (+ blowing agent) | 1 |
| **Key output property** | Layered structure | Modified chemistry | Cellular morphology | Gauge uniformity |
| **Reactor analogy** | None | Continuous stirred tank | Pressure-quench unit | Heat exchanger |
| **Major challenge** | Interfacial instability | Residence time control | Cell collapse | Neck-in, draw resonance |
| **Typical throughput** | 50 – 1500 kg/h | 5 – 500 kg/h | 20 – 800 kg/h | 50 – 2000 kg/h |

## 4.3 Process Flow: Foam Extrusion (Physical Blowing Agent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    POLY[Polymer Pellets Feed] --> ME[Melt Extruder]
    ME --> MIX[Static Mixer Melt Homogenization]
    GAS[Supercritical CO2 Supply] --> INJ[Injection Port High Pressure]
    INJ --> MIX
    MIX --> COOL[Cooling Zone Temperature Drop]
    COOL --> DIE[Die Sudden Pressure Drop]
    DIE --> NUC[Cell Nucleation and Growth]
    NUC --> SHAPE[Shaping and Calibration Unit]
    SHAPE --> CUT[Sawing and Stacking]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept Embedded in Diagram:** The **pressure drop** in the die is what triggers nucleation. If the cooling zone is too cold, viscosity becomes too high and the gas cannot expand; if too warm, the cells coalesce and collapse. This balance is the heart of foam extrusion.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**
**Q:** Define *reactive extrusion*. List **any two** types of chemical reactions that can be performed in a reactive extruder.

**Model Answer:**
Reactive extrusion (REX) is a continuous process in which one or more chemical reactions (polymerization, grafting, crosslinking, degradation) are carried out on the polymer melt inside an extruder barrel, typically a co-rotating twin-screw extruder. The extruder thus functions simultaneously as a **conveyor, mixer, heat exchanger, and chemical reactor**.

Two types of reactions:
1. **Grafting** – e.g., grafting of maleic anhydride onto polypropylene to produce a compatibilizer.
2. **Crosslinking** – e.g., peroxide-induced crosslinking of polyethylene (XLPE) for wire and cable insulation.

> [!TIP]
> *Examiner's Note:* Always state the equipment type (twin-screw) and a *specific example reaction*. Generic answers like "chemical modification" fetch only partial credit.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** What is the *blow-up ratio (BUR)* in blown film extrusion? State its typical range and explain how it affects film properties.

**Model Answer:**
The blow-up ratio (BUR) is the ratio of the **final bubble diameter** to the **die diameter**:

$$ \text{BUR} = \frac{D_{bubble}}{D_{die}} $$

Typical BUR values lie between **1.5:1 and 4:1** for conventional LDPE/LLDPE films.

**Effect on properties:**
- Increasing BUR increases **transverse direction (TD) orientation**, improving TD tensile strength and tear resistance.
- Higher BUR generally reduces the tendency for **draw resonance** and neck-in.
- Excessive BUR (above 4:1) may cause **bubble instability** and non-uniform gauge.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer either **Question A OR Question B** from each internal-choice block.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Co-extrusion & Film Operations

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a three-layer A/B/A co-extrusion line**. Discuss the function of each unit and explain the role of the *feedblock* and *multi-manifold die*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A three-layer A/B/A co-extrusion line consists of **three extruders**, three **gear pumps**, a **feedblock**, a **multi-layer die**, a **chill roll stack**, a **thickness gauge**, and a **winder**.

- *Extruders 1 and 3* melt the **skin (A) polymer** — typically a structural or barrier grade.
- *Extruder 2* melts the **core (B) polymer** — usually a cheaper, general-purpose grade.
- *Gear pumps* deliver **precise, pulsation-free flow rates** to the feedblock.
- The *feedblock* stacks the melts in the sequence A → B → A using internal flow channels.
- The *multi-manifold die* flattens and shapes the combined stream into a sheet or film.
- The *chill roll* rapidly solidifies the extrudate, locking the layer structure.
- The *thickness gauge* (often a beta or X-ray scanner) sends feedback to the PLC.
- The PLC adjusts gear-pump speeds and chill-roll RPM to maintain **layer-thickness tolerance** within ±2 %.

The **feedblock** is preferred for symmetric layer distribution; the **multi-manifold die** is preferred for wide sheets or asymmetric layer constructions.

**(b)** A three-layer co-extruded sheet has a total thickness of **1.2 mm**. The flow rates are $Q_A = 6 \, \text{kg/h}$ (skin), $Q_B = 12 \, \text{kg/h}$ (core), and $Q_A = 6 \, \text{kg/h}$ (other skin). Assuming equal densities, calculate the thickness of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Flow Rate**

$$ Q_{total} = 2 Q_A + Q_B = 2(6) + 12 = 24 \, \text{kg/h} $$

**Step 2 — Fraction of Each Layer (Mass fraction = Thickness fraction for equal densities)**

$$ f_{skin} = \frac{Q_A}{Q_{total}} = \frac{6}{24} = 0.25 $$

$$ f_{core} = \frac{Q_B}{Q_{total}} = \frac{12}{24} = 0.50 $$

**Step 3 — Thickness of Each Skin Layer**

$$ L_{skin} = 0.25 \times 1.2 \, \text{mm} = 0.30 \, \text{mm} $$

**Step 4 — Thickness of Core Layer**

$$ L_{core} = 0.50 \times 1.2 \, \text{mm} = 0.60 \, \text{mm} $$

**Final Layer Structure (A/B/A):** 0.30 mm / 0.60 mm / 0.30 mm ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students calculate the skin layer as 0.60 mm (forgetting the two skins sum to 0.60 mm, *not* 0.30 mm). The correct individual skin thickness is 0.30 mm. **Read the question carefully** — "thickness of each skin layer" means per-skin, not combined.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Reactive & Foam Extrusion

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of foam extrusion** with a labeled process flow diagram. Differentiate between **chemical blowing agents (CBA)** and **physical blowing agents (PBA)**. State two examples of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Foam extrusion is a process in which a **blowing agent** is dissolved in the polymer melt inside the extruder, and a **sudden pressure drop** (usually at the die exit) triggers **cell nucleation, growth, and stabilization** to produce a cellular polymer structure with significantly reduced density.

**Process Flow:**
*Polymer pellets → Melt extruder → Static mixer (for homogenization) → Blowing-agent injection → Cooling zone → Die (pressure drop) → Foam shaping → Calibration → Cutting.*

| Feature | Chemical Blowing Agent (CBA) | Physical Blowing Agent (PBA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Thermal decomposition releases gas | Phase change / expansion of a fluid |
| **State at injection** | Solid powder or masterbatch | Liquid or supercritical fluid |
| **Two examples** | Azodicarbonamide (ADC); Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃) | Carbon dioxide (CO₂); Nitrogen (N₂) |
| **Decomposition / activation temperature** | 160 °C – 220 °C (ADC); 100 °C – 140 °C (NaHCO₃) | Independent of temperature; pressure-driven |
| **Storage / handling** | Stable at room temperature | Requires pressurized storage |

**(b)** A foam extrusion line processes **20 kg/h** of polymer. The solid density is **900 kg/m³** and the foamed density is **75 kg/m³**. Calculate the **expansion ratio** and the **additional volume** of gas generated per hour. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass Flow Rate**

$$ \dot{m} = 20 \, \text{kg/h} $$

**Step 2 — Expansion Ratio**

$$ \phi = \frac{\rho_{solid}}{\rho_{foam}} = \frac{900}{75} = 12 $$

**Step 3 — Volume of Solid Polymer per Hour**

$$ V_{solid} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{solid}} = \frac{20}{900} \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} = 0.0222 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$

**Step 4 — Volume of Foam per Hour**

$$ V_{foam} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{foam}} = \frac{20}{75} \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} = 0.2667 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$

**Step 5 — Additional Volume of Gas**

$$ \Delta V_{gas} = V_{foam} - V_{solid} = 0.2667 - 0.0222 = 0.2444 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** The **expansion ratio is dimensionless**, not in m³/h. Students who write "the expansion ratio is 12 m³/h" lose 2 marks. The **unit of additional gas volume is m³/h**, not dimensionless. Mark the two distinctly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 – Operations**

- [ ] **Co-extrusion** combines multiple polymers in a single die; viscosity ratio must be **0.5 – 2.0** to avoid layer inversion.
- [ ] **Feedblock dies** suit symmetric A/B/A films; **multi-manifold dies** suit wide sheets.
- [ ] **Reactive extrusion** uses **co-rotating twin-screw extruders** because of their self-wiping, high surface renewal, and degassing capability.
- [ ] The **Damköhler number ($Da$)** compares reaction rate to convective rate — high $Da$ means reaction-limited, low $Da$ means mixing-limited.
- [ ] **Foam extrusion** requires a *pressure drop* (die) or *temperature rise* to nucleate cells.
- [ ] **CBA** decomposes chemically; **PBA** changes phase. Both reduce density by the **expansion ratio** $\phi$.
- [ ] **Cast film** is characterized by **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)**; **blown film** by **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)**.
- [ ] The **screw characteristic equation** $Q = \alpha N - \beta P / \eta$ is the single most tested formula in Module 2.
- [ ] **Specific Energy Input (SEI)** in kWh/kg is the energy benchmark for all extrusion operations.
- [ ] Always state **units explicitly** in numerical answers; mark loss for missing units is a recurring KTU board complaint.
- [ ] For co-extrusion layer-thickness problems, **verify the symmetry** — symmetric A/B/A problems have two equal skin layers.
- [ ] For foam numericals, the **expansion ratio is dimensionless**; the **gas volume has units of m³/h or L**.
- [ ] Twin-screw extruders are *the* default for reactive and foam extrusion; single-screw extruders are *the* default for film and basic profile extrusion.
- [ ] **Interfacial instability** in co-extrusion manifests as wavy, broken, or encapsulated layers; controlled by **viscosity matching** and **shear stress balance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examination Tip:** When asked "compare" or "differentiate" two operations, always produce a **tabulated answer** with at least **four** comparison points. The KTU Board examiner's key explicitly awards **+1 mark** per valid comparison point, with the final **+1 mark** for a clear overall summary statement.
